subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The term "accounting period" is used to refer to the
options: time span during which taxes are paid to the inland revenue board
budget period, usually one year, relied on by the accountant
time span, usually one year, covered by financial statement
period within which debtors are expected to settle accounts
explanation: The term 'accounting period' refers to the time span, usually one year, covered by financial statements. This is the period for which accounts are prepared and it allows for comparison of financial performance and position over time.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : ACCOUNTING CONCEPTS AND CONVENTIONS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Assigning revenues to the accounting period in which goods were sold or services rendered and expenses incurred is known as
explanation: The matching concept in accounting is the principle that requires a company to match income earned with the expenses incurred to earn the income during an accounting period. This means revenues and expenses are recorded in the period they are earned or incurred, regardless of when payment is made or received.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : ACCOUNTING CONCEPTS AND CONVENTIONS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The accounting convention which states that profit must not be recognized until realized while all losses should be adequately provided for is termed
explanation: The conservatism principle in accounting is a policy of anticipating possible future losses but not future gains. This policy tends to understate rather than overstate net assets and net income, and therefore lead companies to 'play safe'.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : ACCOUNTING CONCEPTS AND CONVENTIONS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Accounting information is used by investors and creditors of a company to predict
options: future cash flows of the company
explanation: Investors and creditors use accounting information to predict future cash flows of the company. This helps them to make decisions about investing in or lending to the company.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : USERS OF ACCOUNTING & ROLES OF ACCOUNTING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Antics Electronic Company recently bought six generators. Which of the following is the correct method of recording this transaction?
options: Debit generator account and credit cash account
Debit purchases account and credit cash account
Debit cash accounts and credit purchases account
Debit cash account and credit generator account
explanation: When a company buys an asset like a generator, it debits the asset account (generator account) and credits the cash account. This is because the asset increases (debit) and cash decreases (credit).

topics.title: PRINCIPLE OF DOUBLE ENTRY : CASH AND CREDIT TRANSACTIONS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: When a business incurs labour cost in installing a fixed asset, the cost is treated as
explanation: The definition of an asset's cost is all costs that are necessary to get an asset in place and ready for use. Therefore, the cost of the installation labour (wages and related fringe benefits) is part of the cost of the asset (and not an immediate expense of the accounting period).

topics.title: DEPRECIATION OF FIXED ASSETS : CAUSES AND ELEMENTS OF DEPRECIATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: What is the cardinal rule of the double entry system?
options: Debit the increasing account and credit the decreasing account
Debit the receiving account and credit the giving account
Debit the asset account and credit the liability account
Debit the revenue account and credit the expenditure account
explanation: The principal of Double Entry system of Accounting is "Every debit has a corresponding credit" hence the total of all debits has to be equal to the total of all credits. In simple words, Debit the receiver and Credit the Giver.

topics.title: PRINCIPLE OF DOUBLE ENTRY : MEANING AND APPLICATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Mallam Gambo bought a freezer for his shop costing N10,500. In recording, he debited office expenses account and credited the bank account. What book-keeping error has he committed?
explanation: Errors of Principle in accounting, happens when one or both of the entries are made in the wrong class or category of account. Transactions are recorded as per usually accepted accounting principles. If any of these principles is violated or ignored, errors resulting from such violation are known as errors of principle. For example, Purchase of assets recorded in the purchases book.

topics.title: CORRECTION OF ERRORS : ERRORS THAT DO NOT AFFECT THE AGREEMENT OF THE TRIAL BALANCE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The use of the folio in the ledger is for
explanation: In ledgers, folio column is used to provide a reference back to the journal from where the posting has been made so that if accountant is not sure about the posting then he can go back and check it from the journals as well as there he will have not only the amount but also the date, description and the other accounts being affected in the transaction.

topics.title: THE LEDGER : APPLICATION OF DOUBLE ENTRY IN LEDGER

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Dr Cr
# #
1/10 Bal. b/f 5000 2/10 Cash 3000
5/10 Sales 10000 15/10 Bank 6500

What is the closing balance of the account shown above?
explanation:
Dr side total = 5,000 + 10,000
= 15,000

Cr side total = 3,000 + 6,500
= 9,50

Since the Dr side exceeds the Cr side, Then Balance c/d = 15,000 - 9,500
= #5,500

topics.title: THE LEDGER : APPLICATION OF DOUBLE ENTRY IN LEDGER

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The suspense account is used to correct book-keeping error where the
options: error does not affect the agreement of the trial balance
explanation: Suspense Account is opened to tally the Trial Balance, when accounting errors cause disagreement of Trial Balance. The mistake may be rectified after the preparation of final accounts. The suspense account is a general ledger account that acts as a holding account until the error is discovered or the unknown transaction is identified.

topics.title: SUSPENSE ACCOUNT : MEANING AND USES OF SUSPENSE ACCOUNT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The discount column of a three column cashbook is not balanced off but periodically transferred to the discount account because discounts
options: do not form part of double entry
explanation: The cash and bank columns of triple column cash book are used as accounts and are periodically totaled and balanced just like in case of a double column cash book. The discount column is only totaled. It is not balanced because it does not work as an account.

topics.title: CASH BOOK : THREE COLUMN CASH BOOK

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: When is a petty cash account debited?
options: When the fund is established and every time money is spent
When the fund is established and every time it is replenished
When the fund is established and when the size of the float is decreased
Every time money is drawn from the petty cash
explanation: A petty cash account is debited when the fund is established and every time it is replenished. The petty cash fund is a small amount of cash on hand used for paying expenses too small to merit writing a check. When the fund is established, it is debited to record the initial amount. When the fund is replenished, it is debited to record the additional cash added to the fund.

topics.title: PETTY CASH BOOK : MEANING AND IMPORTANCE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: When preparing a bank reconciliation statement, which of the following is deducted from the balance per bank statement?
explanation: When preparing a bank reconciliation statement, unpresented cheques are deducted from the balance per bank statement. Unpresented cheques are cheques that have been written and recorded in the company's ledger but have not yet cleared the bank. They are deducted because they represent amounts that the bank does not yet know about.

topics.title: BANK RECONCILIATION STATEMENT : INTRODUCTION AND REASONS FOR DISAGREENMENT BETWEEN CASH BOOK & BANK STATEMENT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The financial position of an organization at a particular time can be ascertained from the
options: statement of sources and application of funds
explanation: Balance sheet is a statement which shows the financial position of a business at certain point of time. The distinction between income statement and the balance sheet is that the former is for a period and the latter indicates the financial position on a particular date.

topics.title: THE FINAL ACCOUNTS OF A SOLE TRADER : BALANCE SHEET

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Given;
Purchases = N20,000
Sales = N40,000
Carriage inwards = N5,000
Carriage outwards = N5,000
Opening stock = N10,000
Closing stock = N5,000

What is the cost of goods sold?
explanation:
Cost of goods sold = Opening stock + Purchases + Carriage inwards - Closing stock
= 10,000 +20,000 + 5,000 - 5,000
= #30,000

topics.title: THE FINAL ACCOUNTS OF A SOLE TRADER : TRADING ACTIVITIES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The net profit or loss for the year is determined in profit and loss account after
options: deducting cost of goods sold from sales and adding administrative expenses
eliminating all expenses from gross profit and adding any other income
explanation: The net profit is determined when expenses is subtracted from the gross profit and any other income given while the net loss is determined when the gross profit and any other income is subtracted from the expenses in profit or loss account.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A trial balance shows provision for doubtful debts, #190 and debtors, #6000. it is required to write off a further #320 as bad debts and maintain the provision at 5% of debtors. To do this, the profit and loss should show a
explanation:
Bad debt is debited in the profit or loss account
Dr = 320

Total debtor = #6,000 - #320
= #5,680

Provision = 5% x 5,680
= #284

Since provision increased from #190 to #284, it means there is an increase in provision for doubtful debt which is an expense.
Increase = 284 - 190
= # 94 Dr

Therefore, 320 + 94
= debit entry of #144

topics.title: RESERVES AND PROVISIONS : BAD DEBTS AND PROVISIONS FOR DOUBTFUL DEBTS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: If a bad debt previously written off is subsequently repaid, the amount collected is recorded as an
options: income in the profit and loss account
additional cash in the profit and loss account
explanation: A bad debt recovery is a payment received after it has been designated as uncollectible. If the original entry was instead a credit to accounts receivable and a debit to bad debt expense (the direct write-off method), then reverse this original entry.

topics.title: RESERVES AND PROVISIONS : BAD DEBTS AND PROVISIONS FOR DOUBTFUL DEBTS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
use the information below to answer questions below
On 1st January, 1993, Cash in hand was #60, bank overdraft #1100, Debtor #7240, stock #27450, furnishings #2000 and creditors #5850.

As at 31st December 1993, Cash in hand was #40, cash at bank #1050, debtors #6720, stock #29180, furnishings #2000 and creditors #6990

compute the capital as at 1st january 1993
explanation:
Capital as at 1st January 1993 = Assets - Liabilities

Assets = cash in hand+ Debtors+ Stock+ Furnishing
= 60 + 7240 + 27450 + 2000
= #36,750

Liabilities = Overdraft + creditors
= 1100 + 5800
=# 6950

Capital = 36,750 - 6, 950
= #29,800

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : ACCOUNTING EQUATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
use the information below to answer questions below
On 1st January, 1993, Cash in hand was #60, bank overdraft #1100, Debtor #7240, stock #27450, furnishings #2000 and creditors #5850.

As at 31st December 1993, Cash in hand was #40, cash at bank #1050, debtors #6720, stock #29180, furnishings #2000 and creditors #6990
determine his net profit as at 31st december, 1993
explanation:
Net profit = Closing capital - Opening capital

Capital as at 31st Dec 1993 = (40 + 1050 + 6720 + 29180 + 2000) - ( 6990)
= 38990 - 6990
= #32,000

Net profit = 32,000 - 29,800
= #2,200

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : ACCOUNTING EQUATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following categories of labour cost will be classified as direct?
explanation:
If a manufacturing firm manufacture a product, and pays salaries to factory workers making the product, those salaries are considered direct labour.

Direct labour refers to the cost of labour services which are easily and directly traceable to the creation of a product e.g wages.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The major difference between the receipt and payment account and the income and expenditure account is that while the former
options: is kept by the treasurer, the latter is not
deals with all receipts and payments in the year regardless of the time it relates to, the latter is for just that year
is a T-account, the latter is not.
is not in the ledger, the latter is
explanation:
The difference between receipt and payment account and income and expenditure account is that receipt and payment account include revenue and capital items while income and expenditure record only revenue items.

Also, receipt and payment account record actual cash receipt and payment are recorded while income and expenditure record cash accruals and prepayment.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The net profit from a trading account of a non-profit making organization would be treated as income in the
explanation: In order to increase the financial base of not for profit making organisation, trading account should be prepared to show the result of each activity. Profit on bar will be credited to income and expenditure account while loss will be debited.

topics.title: ACCOUNTS OF NON PROFIT MAKING ORGANISATION : INCOME AND EXPENDITURE ACCOUNTS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
use the information below to answer questions below
Ada and Udo are lawyers who went into partnership as Ado and Co. Ada brought cash of #12000, furnishings worth #18000 and a motor vehicle worth #70000. Udo on the other hand brought in cash #10000, his building valued at #105000 and a personal computer worth #35000.

What is the capital of Ado and Co.?
explanation:
The capital of Ado and Co. is the sum of the values of the assets each partner brought into the business.

Ada brought in assets worth #12000 (cash) + #18000 (furnishings) + #70000 (motor vehicle)
= #100000.

Udo brought in assets worth #10000 (cash) + #105000 (building) + #35000 (computer)
= #150000.

Therefore, the total capital of Ado and Co. is #100000 + #150000
= #250000.

q:

Ada and Udo are lawyers who went into partnership as Ado and Co. Ada brought cash of #12000, furnishings worth #18000 and a motor vehicle worth #70000. Udo on the other hand brought in cash #10000, his building valued at #105000 and a personal computer worth #35000.
What is the profit sharing ratio if it is based on capital contributed by Ada and Udo?
explanation:
Ade's share of capital = 12, 100 + 18,000 + 70,000
= #100,000

Udo's share of capital = 10,000 + 108,000 + 35,000
= #150,000

Profit sharing ratio = 100,000/ 150,000

= 2: 3

topics.title: PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : INTRODUCTION AND TYPES OF PARTNERS( PARTNERSHIP)

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The major distinguishing element between the final accounts of a partnership and a sole trader is the
explanation: The profit and loss appropriation account mark the beginning of the difference between the final accounts of a sole trader and that of a partnership. This is because while the sole trader does not share his profit with anybody, the profit of the partnership must be shared by the partners.

topics.title: THE FINAL ACCOUNTS OF A SOLE TRADER : BALANCE SHEET

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Goodwill appears in the books of a business only if it has been
options: raised in connection with the admission of a new partner
raised to account for the true value of a business on the death of a partner
raised in order to prevent the balance sheet showing that the business is insolvent
explanation: Goodwill appears in the books of a business only if it has been purchased at a certain price. Goodwill is an intangible asset that represents the value of a business's reputation and customer relationships. It is only recorded in the books when it is purchased.

topics.title: GOODWILL ACCOUNTS : INTRODUCTION AND REASONS FOR GOODWILL

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Kayode, Akpan and Kachalla are in partnership. Their respective capital accounts had the following balances: N40,000, N50,000 and N70,000. The partners agree to admit Wamo as a new partner with a one-fifth interest in the partnership capital in exchange for N50,000 cash.

Wamo's equity in the resulting partnership is
explanation:
Wamo's equity in the resulting partnership is calculated as one-fifth of the total partnership capital after his admission.

The total capital after his admission is N40,000 (Kayode) + N50,000 (Akpan) + N70,000 (Kachalla) + N50,000 (Wamo)
= N210,000.

Therefore, Wamo's equity is 1/5 x N210,000 = N42,000.

topics.title: GOODWILL ACCOUNTS : TREATMENT OF GOODWILL ON ADMISSION OF PARTNERS AND CHANGES IN RATIO

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Under which of the following conditions is a partnership dissolved?
options: Purchase of a large quantity of fixed asset
Retirement of a manager who is not a partner
explanation: The reasons for the dissolution of partnership are: admission of a new partner, insanity of a partner, death of a partner, withdrawal or retirement of partners, bankruptcy of a partner etc.

topics.title: DISSOLUTION OF PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : INTRODUCTION AND RULES FOR DISTRIBUTING OF ASSSETS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: When shares issued are paid for, accounting entry required is
options: debit bank or cash account and credit share capital and/or premium account
credit bank or cash account and debit share capital and/or premium account
debit shares account and credit capital account
credit shares account and debit capital account
explanation: When shares issued are paid for, the accounting entry required is to debit the bank or cash account and credit the share capital and/or premium account. This is because the company receives cash (an asset, which is debited) and issues shares (an increase in equity, which is credited).

topics.title: ISSUES OF SHARES : SHARES ISSUED PAYABLE BY INSTALMENTS

What is the nominal value of each share?
explanation:
The nominal value of each share is calculated by dividing the authorized share capital by the number of shares.

Value per share = #50m / 100m

= #0.5

topics.title: ISSUES OF SHARES : MEANING AND CLASSES OF SHARES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
use the information below to answer questions below
A limited liability company has an authorized share capital of N50 million split into 100 million shares 80 million share was offered for subscription at 60k per share payable in full on application. These have been fully subscribed and issued
what is the issued capital?
explanation:
The issued capital is the value of the shares that have been sold to shareholders. In this case, 80 million shares were sold at 60k per share, so the issued capital is 80 million shares x N0.60
= N48 million.

explanation: The receipt from a special tax levy to pay maturing interest obligation are recorded in the Debt Service Fund. This fund is used to account for the accumulation of resources for, and the payment of, general long-term debt principal and interest.

explanation: When fund is allocated to a head, it is referred to as vote. Vote is the amount for individual items on a government budget.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : ESTIMATES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following factors has aided the development of accounting?
options: The discovery of mineral resources in commercial quantity
the growth in size of businesses and separation of ownership and management
the development and management of sophisticated monetary system
explanation:
The establishment of trade centres around the medieval Italian cities is one of the important condition given rise to the development of accounting.

The objective of accounting was to provide information for the owner and the reporting together of all the personal and business affairs of a proprietor.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : MEANING AND OBJECTIVES OF ACCOUNTING AND BOOKKEEPING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following concept stipulates that accounting profit is the difference between revenue and expenses?
explanation: Accrual concept means that revenues and expenses are recognized as they are earned or incurred and not when money is received or paid. The profit of a business will show the difference between the revenues and expenses.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : ACCOUNTING CONCEPTS AND CONVENTIONS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: An effective accounting system should provide information
options: for customer feed back and requirement
on internal and external reporting for managers and third parties
for promoters, directors, labour union and distributors
explanation: For an accounting system to provide relevant information , the information must be available on time and have a level of detail significant enough to show trends, comparisons and other information that fuels business decisions.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : USERS OF ACCOUNTING & ROLES OF ACCOUNTING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following accounting records are source documents?
explanation: Source document are written evidences of business transactions that describes the essential facts of those transactions e.g receipt, invoice, credit note, debit note,statement of account, petty cash voucher, cheque etc.

topics.title: SUBSIDIARY BOOKS : SOURCE DOCUMENT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Given: Motor van, N3,600, Premises, N5,000; Loan from R. Nwaeke, N1,000; Cash at bank; N1,650; stock of goods N4,800; Creditors, N2,560; Cash in hand, N250 and debtors N6,910. calculate the capital figure.
explanation:
Capital = Asset - Liabilities
= (3,600 + 5,000 + 1,650 + 4,800 + 250 + 6910) - ( 1,000 +2,560)
= 22,210 - 3, 560
= #18,650

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : ACCOUNTING EQUATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following transaction is BEST recorded in the general journal?
options: Purchase of an asset on credit terms
Transfer of cash from head office branch
explanation: The general journal is used to record unique financial transactions that cannot be recorded in other specific journals like the sales journal, purchase journal, cash receipts journal, or cash disbursements journal. The purchase of an asset on credit terms is a unique transaction that would be best recorded in the general journal.

topics.title: THE JOURNAL : MEANING AND USES OF JOURNAL

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Diamond Ringo business was started when Mr. Diamond brought in a cheque of N500,000, which was paid into the bank account. Which of the following entries properly records the transaction?
options: Cash account was credited with N500,000 and capital account was debited with N500,000
Bank account was debited with N500,000 and capital account was credited with N500,000
Mr. diamond account was debited with N500,000 and the cheque account was credited with N500,000
explanation: The accounting entries is: Bank account was debited with N500,000 and capital account was credited with N500,000

topics.title: PRINCIPLE OF DOUBLE ENTRY : CASH AND CREDIT TRANSACTIONS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: One major advantage of ledger is that it
options: is only accessible to share holders during liquidation
removes the need for preparing a balance sheet after each transaction
can be used by any type of business
explanation: Ledger is a permanent record of transactions and it helps in the preparation of a balance sheet, trial balance and profit or loss account.

topics.title: THE LEDGER : MEANING AND DIVISION OF LEDGER

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A trail balance is usually prepared by an accountant from account balances in the ledger for the purpose of
options: testing arithmetical accuracies of the ledger account balances
providing a basis for establishing the accountant's competence
explanation: Trial balance is a statement showing list of balances in a double entry form extracted from the ledgers to test the arithmetical accuracy of the accounts. It is drawn up at a given date after all the postings have been made in the ledger.

topics.title: TRIAL BALANCE : MEANING AND IMPORTANCE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
What are the advantages of an imprest petty cash book?
I .cheques can be drawn easily
II .A cheque on the use of cash
III .Liability of the petty cashier is limited to the value of petty cash
IV .Loses through the petty cashier can be minimized
explanation:
The advantages of imprest system are:
- liabilities of the petty cashier can never exceed the imprest amount
- elimination of fraudulent practices
- ensures accountability for every expenditure since all are supposed by documentary evidence
- the burden of the main cashier will be greatly reduced
- serves as training for young account clerks

topics.title: PETTY CASH BOOK : ADVANTAGES OF PETTY CASH BOOK

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Bola, UTC's regular customer, owed N1,000 on account of a radio bought. If he sent in a cheque for N1,500, UTC would send him a
explanation: Credit note is a document sent by the seller to the buyer to correct an overcharge. Therefore, UTC will send a credit note of 1500 - 1000 = 500 to Bola.

topics.title: THE JOURNAL : MEANING AND USES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A payment of cash of N20 to John was entered on the receipt side of the cash book in error and credited to John's account. Which of the following journal entry can be used to correct the error?
explanation: The error was that cash was credited instead of being debited. To correct this, the cash account should be debited and John's account should be credited. Hence, the correct journal entry would be 'Cash: N20 Dr, John: N20 Cr'.

topics.title: CORRECTION OF ERRORS : ERRORS THAT DO NOT AFFECT THE AGREEMENT OF THE TRIAL BALANCE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
The following represent extracts from the trading account of a retail outlet for a given month:

Opening stock N2400
Closing stock N6400
Other expenses N2000
Sales N11000
Profit N900

What is the purchase figure for the month?
explanation:
Profit = Sales - ( opening stock + purchases - closing stock) - other expenses
900 = 11,000 - ( 2,400 + purchases - 6,400) - 2,000
11,000 - ( purchases - 4000) - 2000
900 = 11,000 - purchases + 4,000 - 2,000
13,000 - purchases = 900
Purchases = 13,000 - 900
= #12,100

To ascertain the actual expenses incurred and income earned during the year
To show the provision made during the year
To show the total expenses paid and income received during the year
explanation: Adjustment are made in the profit or loss account and balance sheet to ensure that the final accounts of an organization show the true view of their transactions. The reasons for the year adjustment is that, it will ensure that all the income for the year is recorded, expenses is also recorded and to prepare financial statement according to the concept of accounting.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The trial balance showed wages, #2500 and a note stated that #500 wages were due but unpaid. When preparing final accounts and balance sheet it is required to debit profit and loss account with
options: #3000 and show wages accrued #500 in the balance sheet
#2000 and show wages accrued #500 in the balance sheet
#3000 and show wages prepaid #500 in the balance sheet
#2000 and show wages paid in advance #500 in the balance sheet
explanation:
Unpaid means money owed to vendors for expenses incurred, but not yet paid. In accounting , it is called accounts payable.

Therefore, 2500 + 500 = 3,000 will be shown in the profit or loss account while the unpaid #500 will be posted to the current liabilities of the balance sheet since it is an expenses accrued.

explanation:
Selling price = 125% cost price

Cost of goods sold = 10/ 100 x 23,000

= #2,300

Turnover = cost of goods sold / Average stock

= #18,400

explanation:
The net profit for the year can be calculated by subtracting the total expenses and the cost of goods sold from the total sales.

Net profit = Gross profit - Costs

= 23,000 - 18,400 - 2,300
= #2,300

explanation: The total product costs can be determined by adding together the total direct materials and labour costs as well as the total manufacturing overhead costs.

topics.title: MANUFACTURING ACCOUNTS : INTRODUCTION AND PURPOSE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
The following information is provided for amusa company limited , a manufacturer

Prime cost = N999,000

Manufacturing overhead= N132,000

Closing work in progress = N75,000

values of finished goods transferred to the trading account = N1,116,000

If including in the manufacturing overhead were rents of N5,000 paid in advance, what is the opening work in progress for the period?
explanation:
Finished goods = prime cost + manufacturing overhead + ( opening WIP - closing WIP)

1,116,000 = 999,000 + ( 132,000 - 5,000) + opening WIP - 75,000

1,116,000 = 1051000 + opening WIP

Opening WIP = 1,116, 000 - 1,051,000

= #65,000

explanation: The income and expenditure account include cash accruals and prepayment. Also,it records only revenue items.

topics.title: ACCOUNTS OF NON PROFIT MAKING ORGANISATION : INCOME AND EXPENDITURE ACCOUNTS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A member of a club paid N1,000 as is subscription for 1993 and N1,200 as subscription for 1992. If the financial year of the society ended on 31st December 1993, the N1,200 subscription will be treated in the balance sheet as
explanation: The N1,200 subscription paid for 1992 should be treated as a "current liability" in the balance sheet for the financial year ending on 31st December 1993. This is because it represents an obligation that the club still owes to its members for the subscription year 1992 as of the end of 1993. Until the subscription for 1992 is utilized or transferred to income (for example, if it is recognized as revenue in the subsequent financial year when the subscription is earned), it remains a liability. Therefore, it is classified as a current liability in the balance sheet.

q: Which of the following is a strong feature of partnership?
options: The life of the partnership is generally assumed to be indefinite
The owners are liable personally for all debts of the business
The transfer of ownership interest is frequent and easy to accomplish
The partnership is complex to form because of many legal and reporting requirements
explanation: There is usually an unlimited liability for partners i.e they are liable for the total debts of the business and it is not a separate legal entity.

options: credited to profit and loss appropriation account
explanation: When a partnership revalues its assets, the difference between the old value and the new value is credited to an asset revaluation account. This reflects the increase in the value of the assets. In this case, the difference of N40,000 should be credited to the asset revaluation account.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer questions below
Halidu and Hamed are business partners with N30,000 and N20,000 capital respectively. At the end of the financial year, a profit of N12,000, which included Halidu's salary of N3,000 was made.

Halidu's share of the profit is
explanation:
Halidu share of profit = Net profit - salary
= 12000 - 3000
= 9000

The share of profit will be in the ratio of their capital i.e. 30000/20000
= 3:2

Total ratio = 3 + 2 = 5

Therefore, Halidu share = 3/5 x 9000
= #5400

topics.title: PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : ACCOUNTING ENTRIES AT THE END OF THE FINANCIAL YEAR

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer questions below
Halidu and Hamed are business partners with N30,000 and N20,000 capital respectively. At the end of the financial year, a profit of N12,000, which included Halidu's salary of N3,000 was made.
Hamed's share of the profit should be
explanation:
- if profit for the year is greater than total of appropriations:
Dr Appropriation account
Cr Partners' current accounts

- if total of appropriations is greater than profit for the year:
Dr Partners' current accounts
Cr Appropriation account

Now, let us calculate the total appropriation:
Halidu capital + Hamed capital + Halidu's salary
= 30,000 + 20000 + 3000
= #53000

The share of profit will be in the ratio of their capital i.e. 30000/20000
= 3:2
Total ratio = 3 + 2 = 5

Hamed's share = N9,000 x 2/5
= N3,600

Therefore, The share of profit should be credited to appropriation account since the appropriation is greater than Hamed share of profit

limited liability partners the chance of enjoying the limited liability protection

debtors the chance of enjoying the limited liability protection
explanation: The conversion of a partnership business into a limited liability company affords the general partners the chance of enjoying the limited liability protection. In a limited liability company, the owners' personal assets are protected from business debts and liabilities, a feature that is not present in a general partnership.

topics.title: BRANCH ACCOUNTING : BRANCH ADJUSTMENT METHOD

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following statement is correct about the head office current account and the branch current account?
options: the head office current account has a credit balance while the branch current account has a debit balance
the head office current account has a debit balance while the branch current account has a credit balance
explanation: The head office current account and the branch current account are two sides of the same coin. When the head office sends money to the branch, it records it as a debit (money out) in its current account, while the branch records it as a credit (money in) in its current account. Therefore, the head office current account has a credit balance while the branch current account has a debit balance.

topics.title: BRANCH ACCOUNTING : INTRODUCTION AND ADVANTAGES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Which of the following are sources of revenue to state government in Nigeria?
I Statutory allocation
II Fines from customary courts
III Petroleum tax
IV Income tax
explanation: The sources of revenue to state government in Nigeria include Statutory allocation and Fines from customary courts. Statutory allocation is the allocation of revenue to the states by the federal government based on certain criteria such as population, equality, landmass, etc. Fines from customary courts are also a source of revenue to the state government. Petroleum tax and Income tax are not direct sources of revenue to the state government in Nigeria.

explanation: The payment of a liability decreases assets and liabilities as the liability could be paid only through paying in cash or cash equivalents. Hence, it decreases the asset when liability is paid off then it is decreased.

Dr Bank Account Cr

Motor vehicle 6500
Bal c/d 59410
88500 88500


Bal c/d = #59,410 debit balance

In accounting, a debit represents an increase in assets, expenses, or dividends, and a credit represents an increase in liabilities, equity, or revenue. The balance in an account is determined by calculating the difference between the total debits and the total credits recorded in that account.

If the total debits are greater than the total credits in an account, it indicates a debit balance. This means that there have been more increases on the debit side of the account compared to the credit side.

topics.title: BANK RECONCILIATION STATEMENT : INTRODUCTION AND REASONS FOR DISAGREENMENT BETWEEN CASH BOOK & BANK STATEMENT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following demonstrates the imprest system?
options: Float → expenses paid → cash in bank → float
Float → cash from bank → expenses paid → float
Float → expenses paid→ cash from bank → float
Float → cash in bank → expenses paid → float
explanation: The imprest system is a financial accounting system that aims to maintain a fixed 'float' - a set amount of money. The process is as follows: A fixed amount (float) is reserved. This money is then used to pay for things (expenses paid). When the float is nearly exhausted, money is taken from the bank to restore the float to its original level (cash from bank). This cycle then repeats. Therefore, the correct sequence is: Float → expenses paid → cash from bank → float.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
use the information below to answer questions below
On the 30/5/93 Tolu & Co. paid 2 years rent of N50,000 which was debited to the rent account.
The adjustment needed as at 31st December 1993 is?
options: debit rent account and credited adjustment account
debit prepayment account and credit rent account
debit rent account and credit prepayment account
debit adjustment account and credit prepayment account
explanation:
When Tolu & Co. paid 2 years' rent of N50,000 on May 30, 1993, it was initially debited to the rent account. However, since it was paid in advance for 2 years, it should be treated as a prepaid expense and adjusted accordingly at the end of the accounting period (December 31, 1993).

To make the necessary adjustment, the prepayment account should be debited to reflect the amount of prepaid rent, and the rent account should be credited to reduce the balance in the rent account.

explanation: During inflation, the cost of purchasing new inventory increases. explanation:

q: The understatement of closing value of work-in-process would have the effect of?
explanation: The understatement of the closing value of work-in-process would result in an understatement of the cost of goods manufactured. This is because the cost of goods manufactured includes the cost of the work-in-process. If the value of work-in-process is understated, it means that the total cost of goods manufactured will also be understated. Therefore, the correct answer is 'understating cost of goods manufactured'.

options: all income and expenditure during a period

explanation:
An income and expenditure account is used by non-trading entities (organizations that don't primarily focus on buying and selling goods) to track their financial performance.
It summarizes all the revenue earned by the entity (e.g., membership fees, service charges, donations) and all the expenses incurred during a specific accounting period (e.g., salaries, rent, utilities).
This account helps determine the surplus or deficit for the period, reflecting whether the entity's income exceeded its expenses (surplus) or vice versa (deficit).

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Adamu, Babaji and Chukwu are in partnership and they share profit and losses on ratio 3:2:1. Their respective capitals are N20 000, N15 000 and N5 000 on which on which they are entitle to interest at 5% per annum. The profit for the year before charging interest on capital amounted to N5 500.

Calculate the profit for Adamu?
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the total interest on capital for all partners:

Interest on Adamu's capital = 5% of N20,000 = 0.05 x N20,000 = N1,000
Interest on Babaji's capital = 5% of N15,000 = 0.05 x N15,000 = N750
Interest on Chukwu's capital = 5% of N5,000 = 0.05 x N5,000 = N250

Total interest on capital = N1,000 + N750 + N250 = N2,000

Step 2: Subtract the total interest on capital from the profit for the year:

Profit before interest on capital = N5,500
Total interest on capital = N2,000

Profit after interest on capital = N5,500 - N2,000 = N3,500

Step 3: Calculate Adamu's share of the profit based on the given profit-sharing ratio (3:2:1):

Adamu's share = (3 / 6) x Profit after interest on capital
Adamu's share = (1/2) x N3,500 = N1,750

So, the profit for Adamu is N1,750.

topics.title: PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : ACCOUNTING ENTRIES AT THE END OF THE FINANCIAL YEAR

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Faruk and Osawe are in partnership sharing profits and losses in the ratio of 3:7. Faruk is to receive a salary of N9 000. In one accounting period, the business recorded a loss of N1 500 (before deduction of Faruk's salary). The appropriate distribution of the net loss would be?
explanation:
We are given that Faruk and Osawe share profits and losses in a ratio of 3:7.

Salary for Faruk:
Faruk is entitled to a salary of N9,000 regardless of the profit or loss.

Total Loss (Before Salary):
The business recorded a loss of N1,500.

Total Loss (After Salary):
To determine the net loss that needs to be shared according to the profit-sharing ratio, we add Faruk's salary to the initial loss:
N1,500 (initial loss) + N9,000 (salary) = N10,500 (total loss)

Sharing the Net Loss:
Now, we need to divide the total loss (N10,500) based on the profit-sharing ratio (3:7).
Faruk's share: (3/10) x N10,500 = N3,150
Osawe's share: (7/10) x N10,500 = N7,350

explanation: The rules of Garner vs Muray states that " where upon dissolution, a partner's capital account is in debit and he is unable to contribute the full deficiency, the loss must be divided amongst the solvent partners in the ratio of their last agreed capital and not in the proportion ordinary losses are to be borne".

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A company has 5% debentures worth N500 000 ordinary share capital N 2 000 000, and preference shares N1500 000. if the company made a profit of N 1 000 000, the debenture interest would amount to
explanation:
The debenture interest would amount to N 50 000. This is calculated by taking 5% of the debentures worth N500 000. Debentures are a type of debt instrument that companies use to raise capital. They pay a fixed rate of interest to the debenture holders, which is a charge against profits.

In this case, the rate of interest is 5%, so the interest payable would be 5% of N500 000
= N 25 000.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Alabede (NIG) limited issued 50 000 ordinary shares of N1 each at a market value of N2.50 each. the share premium is?
explanation:
Market price = #2.5 x 50,000
= #125,000

Cost price = #1 x 50,000
= #50,000

= 125,000 - 50,000
= #75,000

Alternatively:
Share premium is the amount received by a company over and above the face value of its shares. In this case, the face value of each share is N1 and they were sold at N2.50 each. Therefore, the share premium per share is N2.50 - N1 = N1.50.

Since 50,000 shares were issued, the total share premium is 50,000 x N1.50 = N75,000.

= 10 % x 200

A dividend of 10% on a share of N1 nominal value means that each share will receive N0.10 as dividend.
Therefore, a shareholder with 200 shares will receive 200 x N0.10 = N20 as dividend.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: If goods are invoiced to the branch at cost and the invoice par value is N 2 000 with 5% discount rate, can remitted to the head office is
explanation:
The invoice par value is N 2 000 with a 5% discount.
The discount amount is 5/100 x 2000
= N 100.

Therefore, the amount that can be remitted to the head office is the invoice par value minus the discount, which is 2000 - 100
= N 1 900.

topics.title: BRANCH ACCOUNTING : PRICING METHODS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Which of the following are advantages of departmental accounts?
I. The department making the highest profit can be easily determined
II. The capital of the business can be calculated easily
III. Easy knowledge of the sources of funding
IV. Encouragement of healthy rivalry among the various departments
explanation:
The main advantages of departmental accounting are as follows:
- it provides an idea about the affairs of each department
- it helps to evaluate the performance of each department
- it helps to reward the departmental managers and staff on the basis of performance

topics.title: DEPARTMENTAL ACCOUNT : INTRODUCTION AND ADVANTAGES OF DEPARTMENTAL ACCOUNT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Public sector accounting is based on
explanation: Cash basis is mostly used by public sector while accrual basis is used by private firms. Under the cash basis, financial transactions are recorded in the books when actual payment is made or money is received. This approach does not take into consideration creditors and debtors.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : INTRODUCTION AND USERS OF GOVERNMENT ACCOUNTING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The end result of governmental accounting procedure is to
options: give financial information to the public and investors
produce timely and accurate financial reports for legislators and the public
give information on the performance of public enterprises
explanation: The end result of governmental accounting procedure is to produce timely and accurate financial reports for legislators and the public. This is because the main purpose of governmental accounting is to provide a clear picture of the financial position and performance of the government to its stakeholders, which includes legislators and the public. These reports help in decision making, accountability and transparency.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : PURPOSE AND DIFFERENCE BETWEEN GOVERNMENT AND COMMERCIAL ACCOUNTING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The Act establishing the institute of Chartered Accountants of Nigeria (ICAN) came into force on?
explanation: ICAN, whose membership comprises accountants, auditors and accounting technicians was established in 1965 by Act of Parliament No. 15 September 1, 1965 ( the ICAN Act).

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : MEANING AND OBJECTIVES OF ACCOUNTING AND BOOKKEEPING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Cost reports for the intention of management should reflect?
options: cost and comparable data useful in decision-making
explanation: Cost report include all the costs incurred by the date of the report, where they are known. It allows the field management team to predict cost issues.

topics.title: ACCOUNTING RATIO (2) : PROFITABILITY, LIQUIDITY AND INVESTMENT RATIO

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Responsibility accounting is particularly concerned with?
explanation: Responsibility accounting focuses main attention on responsibility centres. The managers of different activity centres are responsible for controling the costs of their centres. Information about costs incurred for different activities is supplied to the persons in -charge of various centres.

topics.title: ACCOUNTING RATIO (2) : INTRODUCTION AND INTERPRETATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: An advantage of the use of the voucher system is that it?
options: reduces the number of cheques that will be written during any given period
provides a highly flexible system for handling unusual transactions
provides a comprehensive record of business done with particular suppliers
ensure that every expenditure is reviewed and verified before payment is made
explanation: The benefits of voucher systems include facilitating transaction authorization, segregation of functions, and providing a money trial to aid in conducting audits. Vouchers are useful in reducing theft in companies

topics.title: CONTROL ACCOUNTS : INTRODUCTION, ADVANTAGES OF CONTROL ACCOUNTS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A business transaction is recorded in the books of account when the?
options: owner of the business invests his N10,000 in another company
business retains part of of its profits for future expansion purposes
business invest applies for overdraft facilities from its bankers
owner of the business collects N5,000 from the accountant
explanation:
All business transaction is recorded in the books of account separately if it is material.
When the owner of the business withdraws money from the business for personal use, it is called drawings and must be recorded for future reference.

The only business transactions that are entered into the books of account are those that involve money and may be described in monetary terms.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : ACCOUNTING EQUATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: When a transaction causes an assets account to increase, there is?
options: a decrease of equal amount in the owner's equity account
an increase of equal amount in another assets account
a decrease of equal amount in a liability account
explanation: If an asset increases, either another asset decreases, or a liability increases, or owners equity increases.

topics.title: TRIAL BALANCE : MEANING AND IMPORTANCE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The purchase of two generators by Hassan Electronics Enterprises should be recorded as?
options: an expenses in its general office expenses account
a part of capital in the capital account
explanation: An asset purchase occurs when an acquire only buys the assets of an acquiree. An asset purchase involves the purchase of the selling company's assets including equipment, vehicles, stock etc.

topics.title: DEPRECIATION OF FIXED ASSETS : MEANING AND REASONS FOR CHARGING DEPRECIATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: If the inventory at the end of the current year is understated and the error is not caught during the following year, the effect is to?
options: overstate income this year and understate in next year
understate income this year and overstate in next year
understate this year's income with no effect on the next year's
overstate the income for the two-year period
explanation: If the inventory at the end of the current year is understated, the cost of goods sold (COGS) will be overstated, which will result in understating the income for the current year. If the error is not corrected in the following year, the beginning inventory for the next year will be understated, which will result in understating the COGS and overstating the income for the next year. Therefore, the effect is to understate income this year and overstate in next year.

topics.title: STOCK VALUATION : MEANING OF STOCK VALUATION AND METHODS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The total of the discounts received column in the cash book is posted to the?
explanation: In accounting, discounts received by a business are considered gains or income. When discounts are recorded in the cash book, they are posted to the credit side of the discounts received account in the general ledger to reflect this income.

topics.title: RESERVES AND PROVISIONS : PROVISION FOR DISCOUNTS ALLOWED AND RECEIVED

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer questions below
The initial imprest as at July Ist was N500. Petty cash vouchers with the custodian by July 15th added up to N394. An IOU slip received from a co-worker was N65 and there was a shortage of N5 cash. The co-worker made refund on July 18th just before the imprest was replenished.

What was the actual cash in the till as at 15th July?
explanation:
Actual cash in the till as at 15th July:
= 500 - ( 394 + 5 + 65)
= 500 - 464
= #36

topics.title: PETTY CASH BOOK : MEANING AND IMPORTANCE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer questions below
The initial imprest as at July Ist was N500. Petty cash vouchers with the custodian by July 15th added up to N394. An IOU slip received from a co-worker was N65 and there was a shortage of N5 cash. The co-worker made refund on July 18th just before the imprest was replenished.
The amount required to replenish the payments made from the imprest was?
explanation:
Amount required to replenish the imprest:
= Petty cash vouchers + Shortage
= 394 + 5
= N399

topics.title: PETTY CASH BOOK : MEANING AND IMPORTANCE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: State Bank collected a note for A-Makura Company. This collection, not yet recorded in AL-Makura's books, appears on the bank reconciliation as?
options: a deduction from balance per bank statement
an addition to balance per bank statement
explanation: The note collected for Al-makura company will increase the account of Al-makura company, therefore it will give an addition to the balance per book.

topics.title: BANK RECONCILIATION STATEMENT : INTRODUCTION AND REASONS FOR DISAGREENMENT BETWEEN CASH BOOK & BANK STATEMENT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Mayana Corporation uses special journals to record its transactions. If one of Mayana's customers returns merchandize purchased with cash (for a refund), it makes an entry in the?
explanation:
It is a bank used to record all paymens made in cash such as for accounts payable: marchandise purchase, and operating expenses.
There are usually seperated columns for the date, cheque number, explanation cash credit, purchase discounts credit other credit, accounts debited, account payable debit.

topics.title: SUBSIDIARY BOOKS : MEANING AND IMPORTANCE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Sobande Incorporation acquired a machine that involved the following expenditures and related factors.

#
Gross invoice price 15000
Sales tax 900
Purchases discount taken 300
Freight 750
Assembly of machine 500
Installation of machine 800
Assorted spare parts for future use 1200
Turing and adjusting machine 700

What is the initial accounting cost of the machine?
explanation:
Cost of machine = invoice price + sales tax - discount taken + freight + Assembly and Installation + Turning
= 15,000 + 900 - 300 + 750 + 500 + 800 + 700
= #18, 350

topics.title: DEPRECIATION OF FIXED ASSETS : MEANING AND REASONS FOR CHARGING DEPRECIATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A pottery company had sales of N176,000 during the current period and a gross profit rate of 40%. The company's cost of merchandize available for sale during the period was N128,000. The company's ending inventory is?
explanation:
100 - 40 = 60%

Cost price = 60/100 x 176000

= #105,000

Cost of goods available for sale = cost price /purchases + closing stock
128,000 = 105,600 + Closing stock
= Closing stock = 128,000 - 105,600
= #22,400

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: On Ist January, 1993, lobo Company purchased equipment for N18,000. it uses straight-line depreciation with an estimates eight-year useful life and a N2,000 salvage value. On 31st December, 1996, it sells the equipment for N8000. In recording this sales, it should reflect?
explanation:
Yearly depreciation = 18,000 - 2,000/ 8

= 16,000/8

= #2,000

From 1993 to 1996 is 4 years
Therefore, 2000 x 4 = 8,000

Value = 18,000 - 8,000
= #10,000

Loss = 10,000 - 8,000
= 2,000

topics.title: DEPRECIATION OF FIXED ASSETS : METHODS OF DEPRECIATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The balance on a purchase ledger control account represents the?
options: present amount that a business owes its suppliers at date
total credit available to the end of the year to be utilized in future
total credit the business enjoyed for the particular year from its suppliers
total credit owd the business by its customers
explanation: Purchases ledger control account is also called creditors control account which represents the record of the amount that the business owes its suppliers. So, the balance in the account represents the amount that a business owes its suppliers as at the date.

topics.title: CONTROL ACCOUNTS : PURCHASES LEDGER CONTROL ACCOUNT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A powerful instrument of control over both the sales ledger and the clerk whose duty is to post the ledger is the?
explanation: The total debtors account provides a summary of all the amounts owed by all the customers (debtors) at any point in time. It is a control account because it helps to ensure that the individual amounts in the sales ledger agree with the total amount of trade receivables. If there is a discrepancy, it can be investigated and corrected. The total debtors account is also a tool for the clerk who posts the ledger because it helps them to keep track of the total amount of sales on credit.
