topics.title: CONTROL ACCOUNTS : SALES LEDGER CONTROL ACCOUNT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The statement of affairs prepared from incomplete records can be described as?
options: the summary of all the business transactions of the trader ascertained by the accountant
a balance sheet at a particular date showing the assets and liabilities of the business
a schedule of all the business ventures entered into for the period to which the records relates
the statement that shows the profit or loss made during the period
explanation: This statement of affairs is prepared from incomplete records as a balance sheet at a particular date showing the assets and liabilities of the business. It is prepared to determine the capital of the business.

topics.title: SINGLE ENTRY AND INCOMPLETE RECORDS : STATEMENT OF AFFAIRS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The contribution marginal on a job is the?
options: excess of sales revenue over variable costs
difference between fixed and variable costs
explanation: Contribution margin is the profit element determined through the excess of sales revenue over the variable cost.

topics.title: MANUFACTURING ACCOUNTS : INTRODUCTION AND PURPOSE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The objective of allocating all costs to producer is to?
options: avoid unallocated overhead and compute total accounts
co-ordinate the cost and financial accounts
compute the contribution of the product to the final profit
explanation: The objective of allocating all costs to a product is to avoid unallocated overhead and compute total accounts. This is because cost allocation helps in determining the total cost of producing a product, which includes both direct and indirect costs (overheads). Unallocated overheads can distort the true cost of a product, leading to inaccurate pricing and profitability analysis.

topics.title: MANUFACTURING ACCOUNTS : INTRODUCTION AND PURPOSE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
use the information below to answer questions below
Raw materials inventory at the beginning of a period was N 46,800 and at the close of the period there was a balance of N38,600. From the purchases made during the period, defective materials costing N9,200 were returned. Cost of materials consumed during the period was N448,500.

What was the total purchases made during the period?
explanation:
Cost of materials consumed = opening stock + purchases - closing stock - defective materials
448,500 = 46,800 - 38,600 - 9,200 + purchases
448,500 = purchases - 10000
Purchases = 448,500 + 1000
= #449,500

topics.title: MANUFACTURING ACCOUNTS : INTRODUCTION AND PURPOSE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
use the information below to answer questions below
Raw materials inventory at the beginning of a period was N 46,800 and at the close of the period there was a balance of N38,600. From the purchases made during the period, defective materials costing N9,200 were returned. Cost of materials consumed during the period was N448,500.
What was the cost of materials available for use during the period?
explanation:
Cost of materials available for use = opening stock + purchases - returns inwards
= 449,500 + 46,800 - 9,200
= #487, 100

topics.title: MANUFACTURING ACCOUNTS : INTRODUCTION AND PURPOSE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A non-profit-making organization differs from a profit-making one in that
options: proceeds from sale of shares form part of its income
annual subscriptions and levies form part of its income
explanation: A non-profit-making organization differs from a profit-making one in that all its income is committed. This means that all the income that a non-profit organization generates is used to further its mission or cause, rather than being distributed to shareholders or owners as in the case of a profit-making organization. This is the fundamental difference between the two types of organizations.

topics.title: ACCOUNTS OF NON PROFIT MAKING ORGANISATION : INTRODUCTION AND ADVANTAGES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The limitations of the receipts and payments account arise mainly because of the reliance on?
options: the transaction papers as evidence of transaction
explanation: The limitation of the receipt and payments account arises mainly because of the reliance on cash movement as evidence of the transaction, non-cash items are not disclosed.

topics.title: ACCOUNTS OF NON PROFIT MAKING ORGANISATION : RECEIPTS AND PAYMENTS ACCOUNTS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The trading account is to a sole trader what income and expenditure account is to a?
explanation: The trading account is used by businesses to calculate the gross profit or loss, while the income and expenditure account is used by non-profit organizations to calculate the surplus or deficit. Therefore, the trading account is to a sole trader what income and expenditure account is to a non-profit making organization.

topics.title: THE FINAL ACCOUNTS OF A SOLE TRADER : PROFIT OR LOSS ACCOUNT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Which of the following indicate that a partnership business is in place?

i. There is a business
ii. It is run commonly by partners
iii. It has profit-making in view
iv. Partners' liability is limited
explanation: A partnership business is indicated by the presence of a business that is commonly run by partners with the aim of making profit. The liability of partners in a partnership is not limited, it is unlimited. This means that if the business incurs debts, the partners are personally responsible for paying off these debts. Therefore, options i, ii and iii are correct.

topics.title: PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : INTRODUCTION AND TYPES OF PARTNERS( PARTNERSHIP)

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: When forming a partnership, new partners should record non-monetary assets on the new partnership's books at?
options: their historical costs when first purchase by each new partner
the highest values practical so that future income tax deductions are maximized
explanation: When forming a partnership business, new partners assets should be revalued to show the current value. Their current fair market value should be recorded in the new partnership books.

topics.title: PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : ACCOUNTING ENTRIES AT THE END OF THE FINANCIAL YEAR

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Umar and Ahmed share profit and losses equally and have capital balances of N40,000 and N60,000 respectively. If Abdullahi purchases one-third interest with no bonus, how much will he have to contribute to the partnership?
explanation:
The total capital of the partnership is:
= N40,000 + N60,000
= N100,000.

If Abdullahi purchases one-third interest, he will have to contribute one-third of the total capital which is:
= N100,000/3
= N33,333.3

topics.title: PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : ACCOUNTING ENTRIES AT THE END OF THE FINANCIAL YEAR

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following is an intangible asset and a measure of a firm's superior earning power?
explanation: Goodwill is an intangible asset, it is the excess of the purchase consideration over the total value of assets less liabilities. It measures a firm's superior earning power and the attractive force which brings in customers.

topics.title: GOODWILL ACCOUNTS : GOODWILL VALUATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The price paid by an acquiring company is the?
explanation: The price paid by an acquiring company is the purchase consideration. The excess of purchase consideration over the value of asset is what the acquiring company paid which is Goodwill

topics.title: GOODWILL ACCOUNTS : GOODWILL VALUATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer question below
Dan and Baker are in partnership with capital of N50,000 and N30,000 each. The partnership agreement provides that:
(i) profits be shared in the ratio of capital.
(ii) Baker be paid a salary of N8,000.
(iii) both partners earn interest on capital at 6% p.a.
(iv) both partners pay interest on drawing at 6% p.a.

At the end of the year, Dan drew N15,000 while Baker drew N14,000 in four installment on 31/3,30/6,30/9 and 31/12.
The net profit for the year was N48,000. N5,000 is to be written off the Goodwill account.

What is the interest on the drawing by Baker?
explanation:
Interest on drawings is calculated based on the amount drawn and the period for which the amount was used. In this case, Baker drew N14,000 in four equal installments throughout the year. Therefore, each installment attracted interest for a different period. The total interest on drawings by Baker is calculated as follows:

For the first installment (31/3): N3,500 x 6/100 x 9/12
= N157.50

For the second installment (30/6): N3,500 x 6/100 x 6/12
= N105.00

For the third installment (30/9): N3,500 x 6/100 x 3/12
= N52.50

For the fourth installment (31/12): N3,500 x 6/100 x 0/12
= N0.00

Adding up these amounts gives a total interest on drawings of:
= 157.50 + 105.00 + 52.50
= N315.00.

topics.title: PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : ACCOUNTING ENTRIES AT THE END OF THE FINANCIAL YEAR

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer question below
Dan and Baker are in partnership with capital of N50,000 and N30,000 each. The partnership agreement provides that:
(i) profits be shared in the ratio of capital.
(ii) Baker be paid a salary of N8,000.
(iii) both partners earn interest on capital at 6% p.a.
(iv) both partners pay interest on drawing at 6% p.a.

At the end of the year, Dan drew N15,000 while Baker drew N14,000 in four installment on 31/3,30/6,30/9 and 31/12.
The net profit for the year was N48,000. N5,000 is to be written off the Goodwill account.
Determine the total interest on capital due to the partners?
explanation:
Total capital = 50,000 + 30,000
= #80,000

Interest on capital = 6/100 x 80,000
= #4,800

topics.title: PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : ACCOUNTING ENTRIES AT THE END OF THE FINANCIAL YEAR

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The ordinary shareholders enjoy the following rights except the right to?
explanation: All of the above rights are enjoyed by ordinary shareholders except that they dont receive dividends at a predetermined rate, they receive dividends last after all others have received the dividend

topics.title: COMPANY ACCOUNT : INTRODUCTION , TYPES AND KINDS OF COMPANIES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Granada Corporation has net assets of N600,000 and contributed capital of N180,000. The corporation has 30,000 shares of common stock outstanding with no preferred stock. This suggest that the corporation has?
options: a book value of N14 per share
a book value of N20 per share
explanation:
Book value per share = total assets/number of shares

= 600,000/30,000

= #20 per share

topics.title: ACCOUNTING RATIO (2) : PROFITABILITY, LIQUIDITY AND INVESTMENT RATIO

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Under which of these conditions can a company issue shares at discount?

i. A resolution must be passed at a general meeting
ii. The amount of discount must be stated in the resolution
iii. The share must have existed for at least six years
iv. On the order of a court.
explanation: According to the Companies Act, a company can issue shares at a discount under certain conditions. These include: a resolution must be passed at a general meeting, the amount of discount must be stated in the resolution, and the issuance must be on the order of a court. The condition that the share must have existed for at least six years is not a requirement for issuing shares at a discount.

topics.title: ISSUES OF SHARES : MEANING AND CLASSES OF SHARES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The part of equity which is payable on winding up is called?
explanation: The part of equity which is payable on winding up is called capital reserve. This is because capital reserve is a part of shareholders' equity that represents the amount of money that a company can distribute to shareholders after paying off all its liabilities. It is created out of profits earned by the company and is used to write off capital expenses or distribute as dividends to shareholders.

topics.title: COMPANY ACCOUNT : INTRODUCTION , TYPES AND KINDS OF COMPANIES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: To compute gearing ratio, divide?
explanation: To compute the gearing ratio, you divide long-term debts by equity capital. The gearing ratio is a measure of financial leverage, demonstrating the degree to which a firm's operations are funded by equity versus creditor financing. It is calculated by dividing the company's long-term debts by its equity capital. This ratio indicates the proportion of the company's capital that is financed by debt and thus, shows the financial risk of the company.

topics.title: ACCOUNTING RATIO (2) : PROFITABILITY, LIQUIDITY AND INVESTMENT RATIO

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following entries is effected by a department when goods are charged to it at selling prices?
explanation: When goods are charged to a department at selling prices, the stock account is debited. This is because the stock account represents the value of goods that are currently held by the department. When goods are charged to it, the value of the stock increases, hence the stock account is debited (in accounting, debiting an asset account indicates an increase).

topics.title: DEPARTMENTAL ACCOUNT : METHODS OF APPORTIONMENT OF EXPENSES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The difference between the closure of the books of a branch and those of a separate company is that?
options: there is retained earnings account on the branch books
the revenue and expenses account is closed to branch current account
there is no retained earnings account on the branch
the revenue and expenses account is not closed to the home office current account
explanation: In branch accounting, the revenue and expense accounts are closed to the branch current account. This is because the branch is not a separate legal entity, but a part of the main company. The branch current account is a representation of the branch's financial activities and is used to track the branch's income and expenses. The branch does not have its own retained earnings account because it is not a separate legal entity.

topics.title: BRANCH ACCOUNTING : INTRODUCTION AND ADVANTAGES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The term fiscal compliance means?
options: all financial and related laws and regulations are adhered to
only the budget fir the current period is complied with and no deficits allowed
all physical assets requirements have been met
fiscal planning is necessary for proper accountability in the public sector
explanation: In public sector accounting, the fiscal compliance means all financial and related laws and regulations are adhered to.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : INTRODUCTION AND USERS OF GOVERNMENT ACCOUNTING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: In a public corporation, the capital expenditure incurred in a financial period is?
options: spread over the useful life of the assets through depreciation
apportioned at a pre-determined rate stipulated by law
written off in the year in which they occur
merged with recurrent expenditure and reported in one required lump sum
explanation: In a public corporation, the capital expenditure incurred in a financial period is spread over the useful life of the assets through depreciation. This is because capital expenditures represent investments in long-term assets that will provide benefits over several years. Therefore, instead of recognizing the entire cost in the year in which the expenditure is incurred, the cost is spread over the useful life of the asset through a process called depreciation. This ensures that the cost of the asset is matched with the revenue it generates over its useful life.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : PURPOSE AND DIFFERENCE BETWEEN GOVERNMENT AND COMMERCIAL ACCOUNTING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Money not required to meet chargeable expenditure in any fiscal year under cash accounting, should be?
options: carried forward to the next financial year
reserved to meet any deficits or contingencies
explanation: In consolidated revenue fund, all the revenue received by the government are paid and all the recurrent expenditure are also charged to this fund. Money not required or not expended in any fiscal year should be surrendered to it.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : SOURCES AND CLASSIFICATION OF GOVERNMENT REVENUE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: If a monthly insurance premium is remitted to a company by the state government, the initial deductions from employees are recorded in?
explanation: The initial deductions of insurance premium of employees by the state government to a company is recorded in such account of the agency

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : ESTIMATES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Accrual concept stipulates that?
options: revenue should be recognized when it is earned
costs should be recognized when the expenditure is paid
revenue should be recognized only when cash is paid
costs should be recognized when the are incurred
explanation: Accrual concept means that revenues and expenses are recognized as they are earned or incurred and not when money is received or paid. The profit of a business will show the difference between the revenues and expenses.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : ACCOUNTING CONCEPTS AND CONVENTIONS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The basic role of accounting is to?
explanation: The basic role of accounting is to measure the performance of a business. It provides the necessary financial information that allows managers, investors, and others to make decisions about the allocation of resources. While accounting can help in detecting fraud, attesting to financial statements, and protecting shareholders, these are not its primary role.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : USERS OF ACCOUNTING & ROLES OF ACCOUNTING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The need for changes in accounting theory in Nigeria was influenced by the?
explanation: The need for changes in accounting theory in Nigeria was influenced by the dynamics of commercialization and privatization policies. Commercialization and privatization policies led to the emergence of new business structures and practices, which necessitated changes in accounting theory to accommodate these changes.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : MEANING AND OBJECTIVES OF ACCOUNTING AND BOOKKEEPING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A bookkeeping error occurs when there is?
options: intentional failure to record transactions completely
incorrect records and oversights that are not intended
explanation: An acounting error is a non-fradulent error in double entry bookeeping transactions. Data entry accounting errors occur when inaccurate data or information is entered in the books.

topics.title: CORRECTION OF ERRORS : ERRORS THAT DO NOT AFFECT THE AGREEMENT OF THE TRIAL BALANCE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: When a proprietor withdraws cash from the business for private use, he?
options: debits cash account and credits drawings account
credits cash account and debits bank account
debits bank account and credits drawings account
credits cash account and debits drawings account
explanation: Withdrawal of any amount in cash or kind from the enterprise for personal use by the proprietor is termed as Drawings: The drawings account will be debited and the cash or goods withdrawn will be debited.

topics.title: PRINCIPLE OF DOUBLE ENTRY : CASH AND CREDIT TRANSACTIONS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A basic unifying concept in accounting implies that?
options: where creditors'account is zero, the assets are equal to the owners' equity
there should be a balance in the creditors' account in order to measure total assets
revenues should be supported by invested and owners'capital
total assets can be less than liabilities and equity
explanation: The equation thus becomes A - L -E = 0 ( zero). When the debits equals the total credits for each account, then the equation balances.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : ACCOUNTING EQUATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A source document for the sales day book is?
explanation: The sales day book can be classified as a manual ledger documenting detailed information on a sales made by a saleperson to the customers for a given business day. For example, it would document information on customer name, invoice date, unique invoice number, and the amount of credit sale made on that day.

topics.title: SUBSIDIARY BOOKS : SOURCE DOCUMENT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: In a cash book, the opening balance was N7,600, closing balance N9,200 and the total cash received during the period was N18,000. What was the amount of cash paid out during the period?
explanation:
Cash book
##
Bal b/f 7600 Bal c/d 9200
Total cash received 18000 Cash paid out ( missing figure) 16400
2560025600

topics.title: CASH BOOK : MEANING AND TYPES OF CASH BOOK

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: If the total discount allowed in a cash book was N230 and the total discount received was N255, which of the following is true concerning the two discounts?
options: They must be balanced in the cash book and the difference taken to the debtors ledger
They must be balanced in the cash book and the difference taken to the trial balance
They should not be balanced in the cash book before being taking to the trial balance
They should not appear in the trial balance as they were already either received or paid out
explanation: Cash book is the book of prime entry for discounts. Total of discounts columns are used to post to ledgers.

topics.title: RESERVES AND PROVISIONS : PROVISION FOR DISCOUNTS ALLOWED AND RECEIVED

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following bank reconciliation items should not be added or subtracted from the bank statement balance to determine the adjusted cash balance?
options: Deposit in the mail not yet received by the bank
Bank error, charging a company for another company's cheque
explanation: Bank service charges are not added or subtracted from the bank statement balance to determine the adjusted cash balance. Instead, they are subtracted from the book balance. This is because bank service charges are expenses incurred by the business and are recorded in the company's cash book when the bank statement is received. The other options like outstanding cheques, deposits in transit, and bank errors are adjustments made to the bank statement balance to reconcile it with the book balance.

topics.title: BANK RECONCILIATION STATEMENT : INTRODUCTION AND REASONS FOR DISAGREENMENT BETWEEN CASH BOOK & BANK STATEMENT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The fixed amount of money given to a petty cashier at the beginning of a period is called?
explanation: Imprest is used by a business for small items of expenditure, and restored to fixed amount periodically. Hence,a petty cash book is kept on the imprest amount.

topics.title: PETTY CASH BOOK : MEANING AND IMPORTANCE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A provision for bad debt account had N3,800 at the beginning of the year and N4,220 at the close of the year. If bad debts are calculated at the rate of 1/2% of annual sales, what was the credit sales for the period?
explanation:
Bal c/d - Bal b/f = Bad debts
Bad debts = 4,220 - 3,800
= 420

Credit sales = bad debt written off/rate of bad debts
1/2% = 0.005

Credit sales = 420/0.005
= 84,000

topics.title: RESERVES AND PROVISIONS : BAD DEBTS AND PROVISIONS FOR DOUBTFUL DEBTS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Asa Company bought a vehicle worth N20,000 and the vehicle is to be depreciated at 5%. Using the reducing balance method of depreciation, what would be the value of the asset after 3 years?
explanation:
1st year = 20,000 x 95/100
= 19,000

2nd year = 19000 x 95/100
= 18,050

3rd year = 18,050 x 95/100
= #17,147.50

topics.title: DEPRECIATION OF FIXED ASSETS : METHODS OF DEPRECIATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer questions below
#
Capital: Opening balance 307 000
Closing balance 342 000
Net profit for the period 121 600
Opening stock 98 800
Purchases 706 000
Carriage inwards 17 500
Cost of goods sold 740700
Total selling, general and administration expenses 526 000
Discount received 9 650


what is the closing stock for the period?
explanation:
Cost of goods sold = opening stock + purchases + carriage inwards - closing stock
740,700 = 98,000 + 706,000 + 17500 - closing stock
740,700 = 822,300 - Closing stock
Closing stock = 822,300 - 740,700
= #81,600

topics.title: THE FINAL ACCOUNTS OF A SOLE TRADER : TRADING ACTIVITIES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer questions below
#
Capital: Opening balance 307 000
Closing balance 342 000
Net profit for the period 121 600
Opening stock 98 800
Purchases 706 000
Carriage inwards 17 500
Cost of goods sold 740700
Total selling, general and administration expenses 526 000
Discount received 9 650

Calculate the gross profit for the period
explanation:
Gross profit + Discount received - expenses = Net profit
Gross profit + 9,650 - 526,000 = 121,600
Gross profit = 121,600 + 516,350
= #637,950

topics.title: THE FINAL ACCOUNTS OF A SOLE TRADER : PROFIT OR LOSS ACCOUNT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Chibuike bought 36 notebooks at N10 each from John and was given a trade discount of 5%. In addition, he was offered 1% cash discount which he took advantage of. How much did Chibuike pay?
explanation:
Total price = 10 x 36
= 360

Trade discount = 5/100 x 360
= 18

Net price = 360 - 18
= 342

Cash discount = 342 x 1/100
= 3.42

Net price = 342 - 3.42
= 338.58

topics.title: ACCOUNTING RATIO ( 1) : MARK UP AND MARGIN

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Depreciation on a particular piece of machinery was N2,700 during the fifth year of its service life and N4,050 during the sixth year. The logical explanation for this is that?
options: an addition was made to the asset during the sixth year
the estimate of salvage value on this equipment was decreased
a unit-of-output method of depreciation was used during the sixth year
the estimate remaining service life of the assets was increased at the beginning of the sixth year
explanation: The unit-of-output method of depreciation is a depreciation method that allocates the cost of an asset over its useful life based on the number of units it produces or the hours it operates, rather than the number of years it is in use. In this case, the machinery might have produced more units or operated for more hours in the sixth year than in the fifth year, resulting in a higher depreciation expense.

topics.title: DEPRECIATION OF FIXED ASSETS : CAUSES AND ELEMENTS OF DEPRECIATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The main features of the single system are that?
options: books of accounts are not maintained and business relies only on bank statement
the journal records are absent and only the main ledger is kept 7
there are incomplete classifications and recording procedures
only credit sales transactions and credit purchase are recorded
explanation:
The main characteristics or features of single entry bookeeping system are:
- lacks fixed rules and principles
- incomplete acounting system
- lacks arithmetical accuracy
- no final accounts
- no true profit or loss
- no disclosure of financial position
- suitable for small business firms

topics.title: SINGLE ENTRY AND INCOMPLETE RECORDS : INTRODUCTION AND ADVANTAGES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The principal function of a sales ledger control account is to?
options: serve as internal check and provide quick information for the preparation of interim financial statements
serve as external check and provide quick information for the preparation of interim financial statements
provide quick information for the preparation of customers' statement
provide information for the control of salesmen's activities
explanation: A sales ledger control account is a part of the double entry bookkeeping system and is used as an internal check mechanism. It provides a summary of sales on credit and is used to quickly gather information for the preparation of interim financial statements. It does not serve as an external check, nor does it provide information for the control of salesmen's activities or the preparation of customers' statements.

topics.title: CONTROL ACCOUNTS : SALES LEDGER CONTROL ACCOUNT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: In dealing with incomplete records, fixed assets are posted to?
options: opening profit and loss as brought forward figures
closing balance sheet as carried forward figures
closing balance sheets as brought forward figures
closing profit and loss as brought forward figures
explanation: In dealing with incomplete records, fixed assets are posted to the closing balance sheet as carried forward figures. This is because fixed assets are long-term assets that a company plans to use in its operations over a long period of time, and they are carried forward to the next accounting period. They are not posted to the opening profit and loss as brought forward figures, closing balance sheets as brought forward figures, or closing profit and loss as brought forward figures.

explanation:

topics.title: ACCOUNTING RATIO (2) : PROFITABILITY, LIQUIDITY AND INVESTMENT RATIO

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: When the factory cost of production has been ascertained, manufactured goods are transferred to trading account by?
options: debiting manufacturing account and crediting trading account
crediting sales account and debiting trading account
debiting sales account and crediting trading account
crediting manufacturing account and debiting trading account
explanation:
Manufacturing Account: Tracks all production costs incurred, including materials, labor, and overheads.

Trading Account: Summarizes the cost of goods sold (COGS) and sales revenue.

Transferring Manufactured Goods: Once production costs are finalized in the manufacturing account, the cost of goods manufactured (COGM) is transferred to the trading account.

Debiting the manufacturing account reduces its balance, recognizing the completion of production costs.

Crediting the trading account reflects an increase in the cost of goods available for sale.

This approach aligns with standard accounting principles, ensuring accurate reflection of costs and revenues in both accounts.

topics.title: MANUFACTURING ACCOUNTS : INTRODUCTION AND PURPOSE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Lawal and co makes blocks and sell to builders. In computing prime cost, which of the following costs would be considered appropriate?
explanation:
Carriage inwards is the cost incurred in transportation of raw materials and consumables from procurements site to the factory where it is finally consumed.

The prime cost is the expenditure directly charged to a particular unit and it is calculated as:
Prime cost = Opening stock of RM + Purchases of RM + Carriage inwards - Closing stock of RM + Direct labour + Direct expenses + Direct materials.

topics.title: MANUFACTURING ACCOUNTS : INTRODUCTION AND PURPOSE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Manufacturing costs can be classified based on?
options: timing of charges degree of averaging and size of capital
degree of averaging, ease of traceability and timing of charges
degree of averaging, size of floor space occupied and cost accounts
number of employees, timing of charges and management style
explanation: Manufacturing costs can be classified based on the degree of averaging (how costs are spread over units produced), ease of traceability (how easily a cost can be assigned to a product), and timing of charges (when the cost is recognized). The size of floor space occupied, number of employees, and management style are not standard bases for classifying manufacturing costs.

topics.title: MANUFACTURING ACCOUNTS : INTRODUCTION AND PURPOSE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The difference between the factory cost of production and the prime cost of production is?
explanation: The prime cost of production is the direct cost of producing goods or services. It includes direct materials and direct labour. The factory cost of production, on the other hand, includes the prime cost plus overheads. Therefore, the difference between the factory cost of production and the prime cost of production is the overhead.

topics.title: MANUFACTURING ACCOUNTS : INTRODUCTION AND PURPOSE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Receipt and payments account of a non-profit making organization is principally a summary of the?
explanation: The receipt and payment account is the summary of cash and bank transactions which helps in the preparation of income and expenditure account and the balance sheet.

topics.title: ACCOUNTS OF NON PROFIT MAKING ORGANISATION : RECEIPTS AND PAYMENTS ACCOUNTS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Given:
Club debtors at start N150
Cash sales N4850
Club debtors at close N900
Cash received from debtors N550

What is the amount of club sales?
explanation:
Purchases ledger control Account
##
Bal b/f 150Bal c/d 900
Credit sales 1300 Cash from debtors 550
14501450


Club sales = Cash sales + Credit sales
= 1300 + 4850
= #6,150

topics.title: ACCOUNTS OF NON PROFIT MAKING ORGANISATION : RECEIPTS AND PAYMENTS ACCOUNTS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A credit balance on a receipt and payments account indicates that the non-profit making organization?
options: had more revenues than expenses during the period
did not owe much liabilities during the period
received more cash than it paid out during the period
made more cash payments than it received during the period
explanation:
A receipt and payments account is a summary of cash inflows (receipts) and cash outflows (payments) for a non-profit organization during a specific period.
A credit balance in this account signifies that the total of the "receipts" column is greater than the total of the "payments" column.
This indicates that the organization received more cash than it paid out during the period.

topics.title: ACCOUNTS OF NON PROFIT MAKING ORGANISATION : RECEIPTS AND PAYMENTS ACCOUNTS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Goodwill can be valued in partnership when?
explanation:
The conditions under which goodwill is valued in a partnership are:
- on the admission of a new partner
- change in the profit and loss sharing ratio
- on the retirement of a partner
- on amalgamation of partnership
- on dissolution of a partnership
- on the death of a partner
- takeover of a partnership by another business or purchase of a partnership by another business
- on the resignation of a partner

topics.title: GOODWILL ACCOUNTS : INTRODUCTION AND REASONS FOR GOODWILL

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: As part of the initial investment, a partner contributes office equipment that originally cost N20,000 and on which provision for depreciation of N12,500 had been recorded. If the partners agree on a valuation of N9,000 for the equipment, what amount should be debited to the office equipment account?
explanation: The question states that the partners agreed on a valuation of N9,000 for the equipment. This means that the office equipment account should be debited with the agreed value, which is N9,000. The original cost and the provision for depreciation are irrelevant in this case because the partners have agreed on a different value for the equipment.

topics.title: PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : ACCOUNTING ENTRIES AT THE END OF THE FINANCIAL YEAR

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: When a partner makes a drawing of stock items from a partnership, the accounting impact of the drawing is to increase the partner's?
explanation: To the firm it is an income and therefore the capital or current accounts of the partners are debited and interest on drawing account is credited.

topics.title: PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : ACCOUNTING ENTRIES AT THE END OF THE FINANCIAL YEAR

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Appropriation Account
Kudu Wale
# #
Interest on capital 750 550
Salaries 800 600
Share of profits 3300 3300

Determine the net profit of the partnership.
explanation:
Net profit = Interest on capital + salaries + share of profits
= (750 + 550) + ( 800 + 600)+ ( 3,300 + 3,300)
= #9,300

topics.title: PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : ACCOUNTING ENTRIES AT THE END OF THE FINANCIAL YEAR

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Dele and Seun who are in partnership, have decided to covert their business into a limited liability company where both become directors. To convert the business?
options: they will simply continue since there are no new members
the partnership is formally ended and new company books opened
the shares and all other items will be shared equally and not in their former ratios
computation of goodwill must be done as it is legally required
explanation: The partnership is formally ended and new company books opened when their business is converted into a limited liability company

topics.title: COMPANY ACCOUNT : FORMATION OF COMPANY

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: If a sole proprietorship is purchased for cash, then?
options: the purchaser debits his business purchase account with the consideration he pays
all assets and liabilities must be bought
the vendor debits his business purchase account with the consideration he receives
explanation: When the sole proprietorship business is purchased, the purchaser debits his business purchase account with the consideration he pays.

topics.title: PURCHASES OF BUSINESS : ENTRIES IN THE BOOK OF THE PURCHASER

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The market price per ordinary share of a corporation listed on the stock exchange is most closely related to its?
explanation: The market price per ordinary share of a corporation listed on the stock exchange is most closely related to its earnings per share (EPS). EPS is a financial ratio, which divides net earnings available to common shareholders by the average outstanding shares over a certain period of time. The EPS is a per-share profit measure, and the market often uses it to determine a company's profitability. Therefore, it is closely related to the market price of the share.

topics.title: ACCOUNTING RATIO (2) : PROFITABILITY, LIQUIDITY AND INVESTMENT RATIO

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following events increases a corporation's shareholders equity?
options: Donation of shares out of the corporation's own stock to the corporation
Shares of previously subscribed stock issue to subscribing stockholders
A municipality donation of land to the corporation
explanation: A corporation's shareholders' equity increases when the corporation receives a donation of land from a municipality. Shareholders' equity represents the net value of a company, meaning its total assets minus its total liabilities. When a corporation receives a donation, such as land, it increases the total assets of the corporation without increasing its liabilities, thereby increasing the shareholders' equity.

topics.title: COMPANY ACCOUNT : FINAL ACCOUNTS OF A LIMITED COMPANY AND TERMINOLOGIES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:

N N
Paid-up capital 200000 Fixed assets 300000
Share premium 15000
Profit & loss a/c 60000 investments 180000
Long-term loan 180000 stock 28000
Debtors 90000
Creditors 200000 Provision(3000) 87000

liabilities 100000 bank 10000
755000 755000


The quick ratio is
explanation:
Quick ratio = Current assets - Closing stock ( inventory)/ Current liabilities

Current assets = 28,000 + 90,000 + 87,000 + 60,000+10,000
= 275,000

Current liabilities = 200,000 + 100,000
= 300,000

Quick ratio = 275,000 - 28,000/300,000

topics.title: ACCOUNTING RATIO (2) : PROFITABILITY, LIQUIDITY AND INVESTMENT RATIO

topics.title: COMPANY ACCOUNT : FINAL ACCOUNTS OF A LIMITED COMPANY AND TERMINOLOGIES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: If a promoter pays a lawyer N5,000 for services rendered in preparing a Memorandum of Incorporation, the journal entry is to debit?
explanation: When a promoter pays for services rendered in preparing a Memorandum of Incorporation, it is considered as a preliminary expense. Preliminary expenses are the costs incurred before a company is formed. These costs are debited to the preliminary expenses account and credited to the cash account, as cash is going out of the business.

topics.title: COMPANY ACCOUNT : FORMATION OF COMPANY

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Calls in advance are treated in the balance sheet as?
explanation: Call in advance is an amount which is excess paid by shareholders against which the cells are not yet due. Calls in advance is a current liability.

topics.title: ISSUES OF SHARES : CALLS IN ARREARS AND FORFEITURE OF SHARES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Shares issued to a vendor in payment of business purchased would require a debit to?
options: cash account and credit to share capital account
share capital account and credit to vendor's account
vendor's account and credit to share capital account
share capital account and credit to cash account
explanation: When shares are issued to a vendor in payment for a business purchased, the vendor's account is debited because the vendor is the receiver of the shares. The share capital account is credited because the company's share capital increases as a result of issuing shares. This follows the double-entry bookkeeping principle where every debit has a corresponding credit.

topics.title: ISSUES OF SHARES : SHARES ISSUED PAYABLE BY INSTALMENTS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The best method of allocating expenses among departments is to?
options: allocate expenses to each department in proportion to the sales of that department
charge against each department those costs which are within its control
charge expenses against each department in proportion to the purchases of that department
allocate expenses to each section of the department in relation to the number of people
explanation: Expenses can be allocated using the direct method i.e allocates costs of each of the service departments to each operating department based on each department's share of the allocation base.

topics.title: DEPARTMENTAL ACCOUNT : METHODS OF APPORTIONMENT OF EXPENSES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: To record the transfer of stock from one department to another, the correct entry would be to debit?
options: merchandise account and credit department stock account
explanation:
The journal entry for inter-departmental follows:
debit receiving department and credit the transfering department

topics.title: DEPARTMENTAL ACCOUNT : METHODS OF APPORTIONMENT OF EXPENSES

q:

The profit marginal should be?
options: debit to goods sent to branch account
explanation:
20/100 = 1/5

1/5 x 10,000

Profit = 2000 will be credited to the branch stock adjustment account.

Note that: Branch stock adjustment is used to ascertain the gross profit of the branch

topics.title: BRANCH ACCOUNTING : BRANCH ADJUSTMENT METHOD

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to Answer questions below
Among Limited invoiced goods at a cost of N10,000 to its Ikeja branch at a marginal of 20%. The branch later returned goods worth N1,200 at invoice price to the head office.
What is the correct entry in the head office books at the point of initial transfer of the goods?

Branch stock account would be debited with N12,000
Goods sent to branch account would be debited with N10,800
Goods sent to branch account would be credited with N12,000

20/100 = 1/5

1/5 x 10,000

Profit = 2000

Goods sent to the office = 10,000 + 2000
= #12,000 credited to the branch account

topics.title: BRANCH ACCOUNTING : BRANCH ADJUSTMENT METHOD

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Capital and revenue expenditures of government are usually accounted for under funds which include?
options: A personal advances, technical and public funds
personal advances, treasury clearance and special trust funds
explanation: Capital and revenue expenditures of government are usually accounted for under various funds. These include technical, special trust and commercial funds. Technical funds are used for specific technical projects, special trust funds are used for specific purposes that the government is entrusted with, and commercial funds are used for commercial activities that the government is involved in. Hence, 'technical, special trust and commercial funds' is the correct option.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : PURPOSE AND DIFFERENCE BETWEEN GOVERNMENT AND COMMERCIAL ACCOUNTING

q: Which of the following account is kept by local governments in Nigeria?
explanation: Advances account of funds is any advance made on a future commitment or payment. The local government is responsible for keeping this kind of account.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : INTRODUCTION AND USERS OF GOVERNMENT ACCOUNTING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The conventional sources of public revenue includes?
options: crude oil, interest, posted price and taxation
explanation: The conventional sources of public revenue include 'taxation, deficit budgeting, royalties and loans'. Taxation is a mandatory financial charge imposed by a government on its citizens to fund public expenditures. Deficit budgeting occurs when government's expenditures exceed its revenues, creating a deficit that must be funded by borrowing. Royalties are payments made to the government for the right to extract natural resources. Loans are borrowed funds that the government must repay with interest.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : SOURCES AND CLASSIFICATION OF GOVERNMENT REVENUE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: On November 1, 1998, Zaria Holdings owed ₦ 13,600 in respect of creditor. On November 15, it purchased goods worth ₦ 69,000 and paid cheques of ₦51,600. On November 29, one of the Holdings cheques worth ₦3,000 was returned while the creditor granted ₦1,500 discount. The amount owed by Zaria Holdings as at November 29 is
explanation:
The amount owed by Zaria Holdings as at November 29 can be calculated as follows:

Initial debt of ₦13,600 + Purchased goods worth ₦69,000 - Paid cheques of ₦51,600 + Returned cheque worth ₦3,000 - Discount granted ₦1,500

= ₦32,500.

- Credit Accounts Receivable(debtor) ( to remove the amount that will not be collected)

Under the allowance method:

transactions systematically to ascertain the financial position of a business
explanation: Book keeping is the recording of financial transactions, and is part of the process of accounting in business. Transactions include purchases, sales, receipts, and payments by an individual person or an organization/corporation. Its main objective is to systematically ascertain the financial position of a business.

explanation: A tangible asset is an asset that has a physical form. It's something you can see or touch. Examples of tangible assets include buildings, land, vehicles, equipment, and inventory. In the given options, 'Motor vehicles' is a tangible asset because it has a physical form that can be seen and touched.

explanation: Single column cash book has only cash column in it. It records the Cash transactions related to a period. explanation:

explanation: Sales are credited and purchases are debited in the trial balance. If sales of ₦3,000 and purchases of ₦2,000 are omitted, the trial balance will be out of balance by the difference of ₦1,000. This will affect the trial balance totals.

explanation: At the end of each fiscal year, a company prepares for the new fiscal year by closing its books. As part of the process, the entire balance of all revenue and expense accounts are transferred to the company's balance sheet by a sequence of journal entries; debit to profit and loss account and a credit to the expense account, leaving the revenue and expense accounts with a zero balance.

NOTE THAT:
cost of the opening raw materials= Cost of raw materials consumed -carriage inwards + returns of raw materials+closing stock

Working Note:

- Power factory = 2/5 x 75000 = 30000

- Rent factory = 1/7 x 56000 = 8000

Production cost = cost of the opening raw materials + Manufacturing wages + factory overhead
= 404950+27000+(10000+30000+6000+8000)
= 485950

topics.title: MANUFACTURING ACCOUNTS : INTRODUCTION AND PURPOSE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Given

₦
Cash book item:
Paid to suppliers10 800
Expenses paid 6 900
Drawings made 900
Balances at start 15 750
Balances at end 3 870
Drawings from bank to shop 1 720


Determine the receipt from debtors
explanation:
- Calculate the total cash inflows needed:
Final balance: ₦3,870
Cash outflows (suppliers + expenses + drawings made): ₦18,600

Total cash inflows needed = Final balance + Cash outflows
Total cash inflows needed =₦3,870 + ₦18,600
= ₦22 ,470

- Determine the actual total cash inflows:
Initial balance: ₦15,750
Drawings from bank to shop: ₦1,720

Total actual cash inflows = Initial balance + Drawings from bank to shop
Total actual cash inflows = ₦15,750 + ₦1,720
=₦17,470

Calculate the receipt from debtors:
Receipt from debtors is the amount required to cover the difference between the needed inflows and the actual inflows.

Receipt from debtors=Total cash inflows needed−Total actual cash inflows

Receipt from debtors = ₦22, 470 − ₦ 17 ,470
=₦ 5,000

explanation:
Single entry system does not support advance calculations such as calculation of trial and balance. The disadvantages (shortcoming) are:
- lack of mathematical accuracy
- insufficient data to generate proper financial statements
- inability to strategize
- tax problems
- theft

topics.title: SINGLE ENTRY AND INCOMPLETE RECORDS : INTRODUCTION AND ADVANTAGES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: When a customer's cheque is returned unpaid in a debtor's control accounting system, the treatment will be
options: debit bank, credit customer and credit control account
credit bank, debit customer and debit control account
debit customer, credit control account and credit bank
credit control account, debit bank and debit customer
explanation: When a customer's cheque is returned unpaid, it means that the bank account will be reduced (credited) because the expected money did not come in. The customer's account will be increased (debited) because the customer still owes the money. The debtor's control account, which is a summary account that reflects the total of the individual debtor accounts, will also be debited to reflect the increase in the total amount owed by customers.

topics.title: CONTROL ACCOUNTS : SALES LEDGER CONTROL ACCOUNT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Didi Ltd. offered 10,000 ordinary shares of ₦1.50 each at a discount of 2% which were fully subscribed. With regard to the offer above.
options: Shares are never offered at a discount; the offer is invalid
The value of shares in the capital account will be lowered by 2%
The company incurs a loss to the tune of 2% of the offer
Each of the subscribers loses 2% of investment
explanation: When a company issues shares at a discount, it means that the shares are sold for less than their nominal value. In this case, Didi Ltd. is selling shares at a 2% discount. This does not mean that the value of the shares in the capital account will be lowered by 2%, nor does it mean that each of the subscribers loses 2% of investment. Instead, it means that the company incurs a loss to the tune of 2% of the offer because it is receiving less money for the shares than their nominal value.

explanation:
Accumulated fund also known as general fund or capital fund is a balance sheet item which represents the surplus of assets over liabilties of a non-trading concerns. In other words, we can say that the capital fund of non-profit organizations is equivalent to the capital of for-profit organizations.

It is not referred to as members equity, share capital, or surplus fund. Members equity and share capital are terms used in for-profit organizations, while surplus fund is not a standard accounting term.

topics.title: PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : ACCOUNTING ENTRIES AT THE END OF THE FINANCIAL YEAR

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer this question

Rakiya and Joy are in partnership and agreed that 5% interest per annum is to be charged on drawings. The drawing made by both partners in one year were: Rakiya,₦200 on March 31 and ₦300 on September 30, Joy, ₦100 on April 1 and ₦240 on July 1.

The interest on Joy's drawing is
explanation:
For April 1st, the number of months = 9 while drawings = 100.
Therefore, 100 x 5% x 9/12
= 3.75

For July 1st, the number of months = 6 while drawings = 240
Therefore, 240 x 5% x 6/12
= 6

Total interest on joy's drawings = 3.75 + 6
= 9.75

topics.title: PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : ACCOUNTING ENTRIES AT THE END OF THE FINANCIAL YEAR

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer this question

Rakiya and Joy are in partnership and agreed that 5% interest per annum is to be charged on drawings. The drawing made by both partners in one year were: Rakiya,₦200 on March 31 and ₦300 on September 30, Joy, ₦100 on April 1 and ₦240 on July 1.
Assuming that Rakiya was not credited with any income during the period, what is her closing current account balance?
explanation:
Total drawings = ₦200 + ₦300
= ₦500

Total interest charged by Rakya:
For March 31, number of months = 9 while drawings = 200
= 200 x 5% x 9/12
= 7.5

For September 30, number of months = 3 while drawings = 300
= 300 x 5% x 3/12
= 3.75

Total drawings = 7.5 + 3.75
= 11.25

To determine the closing current account balance, we subtract the total drawings and interest charged from zero (assuming Rakiya started with a zero balance):

Closing current account balance = 0 - Total drawings - Total interest charged
Closing current account balance = 0 - ₦500 - ₦11.25
Closing current account balance = -₦511.25

Therefore, Rakiya's closing current account balance is ₦511.25 debit.

topics.title: ACCOUNTS OF NON PROFIT MAKING ORGANISATION : INCOME AND EXPENDITURE ACCOUNTS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The most appropriate basis for apportioning inventory holding cost among departments is to use the value of
explanation: Inventory holding cost is the cost incurred by a business to store its inventory over a certain period of time. The most appropriate basis for apportioning inventory holding cost among departments is to use the value of average stock. This is because the average stock takes into account both the opening and closing stock during a particular period, providing a more accurate reflection of the inventory levels throughout that period.

topics.title: DEPARTMENTAL ACCOUNT : METHODS OF APPORTIONMENT OF EXPENSES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The body constitutionally charge with the accounts of the Federation and other Assembly is the
explanation: Public Accounts Committee refers to a committee in the legislature that must study public audits, invite ministers, permanent secretaries or other ministry officials to the committee for questioning, and issue a report of their findings subsequent to a government budget audit.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : INTRODUCTION AND USERS OF GOVERNMENT ACCOUNTING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
I. Meet growth and diversification needs
II. Reach out to particular customers or markets
III. Comply with some regulatory directives
IV. Increase employees income

Which of the reasons above do consider in establishing branches.
explanation: Establishing branches can be done for various reasons. These include meeting growth and diversification needs (I), reaching out to particular customers or markets (II), and complying with some regulatory directives (III). Increasing employees' income (IV) is not a primary reason for establishing branches. Therefore, the correct answer is 'I, II and III'.

topics.title: BRANCH ACCOUNTING : INTRODUCTION AND ADVANTAGES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The channel through which all government borrowing and domestic lending transactions pass is called
explanation: The National Loan Fund is the channel through which all government borrowing and domestic lending transactions pass. It was established to provide a single unified fund into which all government borrowing is paid, and from which all government lending is made.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : SOURCES AND CLASSIFICATION OF GOVERNMENT REVENUE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Given

Dept A Dept B
Floor space 400m2 60m2
Machine 1200 1400
Turnover ₦36million ₦64million
Labour hours 1000 1400


A joint cost ₦72 million incurred by the two departments was apportioned ₦30 million to A and ₦42 million to B. The basis used for apportionment must have been
explanation:
The ratio of labor hours of A to B are 1000/1400 = 1: 0.72 and the ratio of cost of A to B is also 30/42 = 1: 0.72

Labor hours must have been the basis or apportionment.

topics.title: DEPARTMENTAL ACCOUNT : METHODS OF APPORTIONMENT OF EXPENSES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Recurrent expenditure are expenses for the period not exceeding ...
explanation: Recurrent expenditure refers to all payments other than for capital assets, including on goods and services e.g wages and salaries, employer contributions, interest payments, subsidies and transfers. They are recurring expenses that are incurred by business to generate revenues for the accounting period.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : ESTIMATES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A major benefit derived from the reconciliation of branch and head office books is to
options: ascertain the correct profit or loss on branch activities
enable the branch to keep control over stock
enable the branch to ascertain debtors balance
explanation: The reconciliation of branch and head office books is primarily done to ascertain the correct profit or loss on branch activities. This is because the reconciliation process involves comparing the records of the branch and the head office to identify any discrepancies and correct them. This ensures that the financial statements accurately reflect the branch's operations and its contribution to the overall company's profit or loss.

topics.title: BRANCH ACCOUNTING : BRANCH ADJUSTMENT METHOD

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Haial Limited with three departments has a total of ₦7,200,000 as the net debtors for the year ended 31/12/97. The company's policy provides for 15% bad debt annually. Which of the following represents the total balance of debtors before adjustments?
explanation:
₦7,200,000 represents 85%(100 - 15) of total debts.

Total debts before adjustment will now be (85 × 7,200,000)/100

= ₦6,120,000.

topics.title: CONTROL ACCOUNTS : SALES LEDGER CONTROL ACCOUNT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The current growth in the volume of trade and financial dealings in Nigeria is helped by
explanation: Financing activities are transactions or business events that affect long-term liabilities and equity. In other words, financing activities are transactions with creditors or investors used to fund either company operations or expansions. These transactions are the third set of cash activities displayed on the statement of cash flows. Nigeria financial system has in recent times promote the economic transactions to a large extent and plays a significant role in the country's growth and is critical to the overall development of the country.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : MEANING AND OBJECTIVES OF ACCOUNTING AND BOOKKEEPING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Cost accounting entails the provision of information
explanation:
The objectives of cost accounting are:
- Ascertaining cost
- Determining selling price
- Measuring and increasing efficiency
- Cost control and Cost reduction
- Cost management
- Determining profits
- Providing basis for managerail decision making.

topics.title: ACCOUNTING RATIO (2) : INTRODUCTION AND INTERPRETATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The main objective of accounting report is to provide information about
explanation: The main objective of an accounting report is to provide information about a company's economic resources. This includes all the assets, liabilities, and equity of the company. It helps stakeholders to understand the financial position of the company and make informed decisions.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : USERS OF ACCOUNTING & ROLES OF ACCOUNTING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: If liabilities amounted to ₦12,045, other assets ₦36,800 and equity ₦26,896, the cash at hand would be
explanation:
Cash + Other Assets = Liabilities + Owners Equity
Cash at hand = Liabilities + Equity - Other Assets = ₦ (12,045 + 26,896 - 36,800)
= ₦2,141

topics.title: ACCOUNTING EQUATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:

= 333,050

Since the debit side is greater than the credit side, therefore the cash balance will be = 333,050 - 320100
= 12,950

topics.title: TRIAL BALANCE : MEANING AND IMPORTANCE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The accuracy of journalizing is checked by
options: ensuring that debit totals equals credit totals
posting all journal entries to ledger accounts
adding all figures in the debit column
comparing accounts in the ledger against the journals
explanation: The accuracy of journalizing is checked by ensuring that debit totals equals credit totals. This is based on the fundamental concept of double-entry bookkeeping, where for every debit entry, there must be an equal and opposite credit entry. This ensures that the accounting equation (Assets = Liabilities + Equity) is always in balance.

topics.title: THE JOURNAL : MEANING AND USES OF JOURNAL

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Hauwa Ltd bought 10bags of rice for ₦500 each. The company was given 5% and 12% trade and cash discounts respectively. What will be recorded as a discount received in the company's book?
explanation:
Note that only cash discount will appear in the ledger.
Trade discounts are not recorded in the books of accounts. Instead, it is generally given at sales, like on bulk purchases. Hence, the Purchase amount is shown as a net trade discount in the books.

A cash discount is received as an incentive for early payment. It is shown as an income in the Profit and loss account. Initially, the Purchases are shown as the full amount. Then, the payable is reduced with the amount of discount received.

Therefore, Gross amount = 10 x 500 = 5000
Discount received = 12% x 5000
= #600

topics.title: RESERVES AND PROVISIONS : PROVISION FOR DISCOUNTS ALLOWED AND RECEIVED

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A cash book had opening balance of ₦15,200, closing balance ₦18,400 and total cash received during the period ₦36,000. What was the amount of cash paid out during the same period?
explanation:
The amount of cash paid out during the period is calculated by adding the opening balance to the total cash received and then subtracting the closing balance.

Cash paid out = Opening balance + Total cash received - Closing balance = ₦15,200 + ₦36,000 - ₦18,400
= ₦32,800.

topics.title: CASH BOOK : MEANING AND TYPES OF CASH BOOK

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: In a petty cash book, the closing balance was ₦235, imprest ₦1,250, while the stationery expenses were ₦655. How much was paid for other general expenses?
explanation:
closing balance + stationery + other expenses = imprest
other expenses = imprest - (closing balance + stationery )
= ₦[1,250 - (235 + 655)]
= ₦360.

topics.title: PETTY CASH BOOK : MEANING AND IMPORTANCE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: When a bill is negotiated to a bank, it is said to be
explanation: It is an accepted draft or bill of exchange sold for early payment to a bank or credit institution at less than face value after the bank deducts fees and applicable interest charges. The bank or credit institution then collects full value on the draft or bill of exchange when payment becomes due.

topics.title: BANK RECONCILIATION STATEMENT : INTRODUCTION AND REASONS FOR DISAGREENMENT BETWEEN CASH BOOK & BANK STATEMENT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Given that the balance as per cash book after necessary adjustments was ₦4,315, unpresented cheques were ₦1,688 and the bank statement balance was ₦4,791, what was the balance of the uncredited cheques?
explanation:
We add adjustment and unpresented cheques and subtract bank statement balance from it
= ₦ 4,315 + ₦ 1,688 - ₦4,791
= ₦1,212.

topics.title: BANK RECONCILIATION STATEMENT : PREPARATION OF BANK RECONCILIATION STATEMENT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: If a property developing company sells a completed building to an interested third party, this sale can be treated in the books of the company as
explanation: When a property developing company sells a completed building, it is considered a revenue receipt. This is because the sale of the building is part of the company's regular business operations and the proceeds from the sale contribute to the company's revenue. A capital receipt, on the other hand, is a non-recurring income, which is not a part of the regular business operations. Windfall receipt is an unexpected or unplanned income, and miscellaneous receipt is a non-specific category that doesn't apply in this context.

topics.title: COMPANY ACCOUNT : FINAL ACCOUNTS OF A LIMITED COMPANY AND TERMINOLOGIES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer this question.

Palaju Enterprises had fixed assets of ₦3,100,000 at the end of the year. Fixed assets costing ₦900,000 with provision for depreciation of ₦150,000 were disposed of during the period. Fixed assets of ₦100,000 were added during the year. Provision for depreciation was ₦350,000 at the beginning of the year and ₦300,000 at the end of the year

What was the value of fixed assets at the beginning of the year?
explanation:
Total fixed asset at beginning = fixed asset at the end of the year + disposed (fixed asset + depreciation) + new fixed assets - provision for depreciation at beginning of the year)
= ₦(3,100,000 + 900,000 + 150,000 + 100,000 - 350,000)
= ₦ 3,900,000

topics.title: DEPRECIATION OF FIXED ASSETS : CAUSES AND ELEMENTS OF DEPRECIATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer this question.

Palaju Enterprises had fixed assets of ₦3,100,000 at the end of the year. Fixed assets costing ₦900,000 with provision for depreciation of ₦150,000 were disposed of during the period. Fixed assets of ₦100,000 were added during the year. Provision for depreciation was ₦350,000 at the beginning of the year and ₦300,000 at the end of the year
The depreciation expense charged for the year is
explanation:
Depreciation - (provision for depreciation at year beginning - provision for depreciation at year end)
= ₦(150,000 - (350,000 - 300,000)
= ₦150,000 - 50,000
= ₦100,000

topics.title: CONTROL ACCOUNTS : PURCHASES LEDGER CONTROL ACCOUNT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: For an incomplete record to provide necessary information, it must be converted to
explanation: Incomplete records are usually converted to double entry records. Double entry bookkeeping is a system of accounting in which every transaction has a corresponding positive and negative entry (debits and credits). This system is used to satisfy the equation Assets = Liabilities + Equity, thereby ensuring that all the company's accounts are balanced.

topics.title: MANUFACTURING ACCOUNTS : INTRODUCTION AND PURPOSE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer this question.
₦
Bar opening stock 10 000
Amount owed to bar suppliers at the beginning of the year 10 000
Bar sales 50 000
Bar credit purchases 40 000
Payment to suppliers30 000
Bar expenses 1 000
Bar closing stock 15 000


What is the bar profit?
explanation:
The bar profit is calculated as bar sales - cost of goods sold - bar expenses.

The cost of goods sold is calculated as opening stock + purchases - closing stock. So, the cost of goods sold is 10,000 + 40,000 - 15,000
= 35,000.

The bar profit is then calculated as 50,000 - 35,000 - 1,000
= 14,000.

topics.title: ACCOUNTS OF NON PROFIT MAKING ORGANISATION : INCOME AND EXPENDITURE ACCOUNTS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Lubricating oil and spare parts of machinery are examples of
explanation: Lubricating oil and spare parts of machinery are examples of indirect materials. Indirect materials are materials used in the production process, but cannot be directly linked to the product. For example, lubricating oil used to maintain machinery is not a direct component of a product, but is necessary for the production process.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A club received rent ₦10,000 and donation ₦30,000. It paid ₦6,000 for entertainment and is still owing ₦16,000. The balance of the receipts and payments account is
explanation:
Total receipts = Rent received + Donation received = ₦10,000 + ₦30,000
= ₦40,000

Total payments = Payment for entertainment + Owing = ₦6,000 + ₦16,000
= ₦22,000

Balance of the receipts and payments account = Total receipts - Total payments
Balance = ₦40,000 - ₦22,000
Balance = ₦18,000

q:
Given

Other expenses 10 000

Determine the club's excess of income over expenditure.
explanation:
Income = Club trading profit + Members' subscription + Profit on sale of fixed assets
= ₦(12,000 + 15,000 + 2,500)
= ₦29,500

Expenditure = Loss on sale of investments + Wages + Other expenses
= ₦(1,000 + 16,500 + 10,000)
= ₦27,500

Excess = ₦29,500 - 27,500
= ₦2,000

topics.title: ACCOUNTS OF NON PROFIT MAKING ORGANISATION : INCOME AND EXPENDITURE ACCOUNTS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The excess of income over expenditure is usually transferred to the
explanation: Surplus is the excess of income over expenditure. A credit balance in the income and expenditure account shows surplus. It is the profit earned and should be added to the accumulated fund on the liabilities side of the balance sheet.

topics.title: ACCOUNTS OF NON PROFIT MAKING ORGANISATION : INCOME AND EXPENDITURE ACCOUNTS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Partners' salaries and drawings are usually posted to the
explanation: Partners salaries and drawings are usually posted to the current account. Note that drawings are funds taken from the business by the partners for personal use.

topics.title: PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : ACCOUNTING ENTRIES AT THE END OF THE FINANCIAL YEAR

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Marhumu and Yusuf are in partnership, sharing profit and losses in the ratio of 2:1. On 31/3/2000, the partnership decided to admit Idris who is to take 1/4 of future profits without changing the ratio of Marhumu and Yusuf. What is the new profit-sharing ratio of Marhumu and Yusuf?
explanation:
Marhumu and Yusuf are in partnership, sharing profit and losses in the ratio of 2:1.

If Idris is to take 1/4 of future profits, this means that the remaining 3/4 of the profits will be shared between Marhumu and Yusuf in their original ratio of 2:1.

Therefore, Marhumu's share will be 2/3 of 3/4 (i.e., 1/2 or 50%) and Yusuf's share will be 1/3 of 3/4 (i.e., 1/4 or 25%).

So, the new profit-sharing ratio of Marhumu and Yusuf is 50%:25%.

topics.title: GOODWILL ACCOUNTS : GOODWILL VALUATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: If a partner pays a premium of ₦500 for 1/5 share of profit, then the total value of goodwill is
explanation:
Goodwill is the value of the reputation of a firm built over time with respect to the expected future profits over and above the normal profits.

If a partner pays a premium of ₦500 for 1/5 share of profit, then the total value of goodwill is calculated by multiplying the premium by the reciprocal of the share of profit.

In this case, ₦500 x (5/1)
= ₦2,500.

Therefore, the total value of goodwill is ₦2,500.

topics.title: GOODWILL ACCOUNTS : GOODWILL VALUATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: On the dissolution of a partnership business, the net book value of the assets is transferred to
explanation: A realization account is an account used to record the disposal of an asset or assets and to determine the profit or loss on the disposal.

topics.title: DISSOLUTION OF PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : INTRODUCTION AND RULES FOR DISTRIBUTING OF ASSSETS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Bala Ltd. acquired the business of Bello Ltd. and caused the separate existence of the latter company to terminate. This situation is best described as
explanation: In the context of business, absorption refers to the process where one company takes over another company and completely establishes itself as the new company, eliminating the absorbed company's existence. In this case, Bala Ltd. has absorbed Bello Ltd., causing the latter's separate existence to terminate.

topics.title: PURCHASES OF BUSINESS : INTRODUCTION AND ADVANTAGES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Where a company acquires controlling shares of another and the consideration is paid in cash, the entries in the books of the purchases are debit
explanation: When a company acquires controlling shares of another company and pays the consideration in cash, the entry in the books of the purchasing company would be to debit 'Investment' and credit 'Cash'. This is because the purchasing company has made an investment by acquiring the shares, which is an asset, and has paid out cash, which reduces its asset. Therefore, 'Investment' is debited (as assets increase on the debit side) and 'Cash' is credited (as assets decrease on the credit side).

topics.title: PURCHASES OF BUSINESS : ENTRIES IN THE BOOK OF THE PURCHASER

explanation:
Purchase price = Total assets - Liabilities + Goodwill
Purchase price = ₦210,000 - ₦10,000 + ₦20,000
Purchase price = ₦200,000 + ₦20,000
Purchase price = ₦220,000

topics.title: PURCHASES OF BUSINESS : ENTRIES IN THE BOOK OF THE PURCHASER

the former have voting rights while the latter do not

topics.title: ISSUES OF SHARES : MEANING AND CLASSES OF SHARES

q:

Given:

topics.title: COMPANY ACCOUNT : FORMATION OF COMPANY

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: X and Y are two departments that are to share 50% of all joint costs equally and the balance in the ratio 2:1. If a sum of ₦150, 000 is incurred jointly, what will be the portion attributable to X?
explanation:
50% of ₦150,000
= ₦75,000

If they share this equally, then X will have ₦37,500

X's share of the balance of ₦75,000 will be 2/3 × 75,000
= ₦50,000

Total share for X will be ₦(37,500 + 50,000)
= ₦87,500.

topics.title: DEPARTMENTAL ACCOUNT : METHODS OF APPORTIONMENT OF EXPENSES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Department F transferred some goods to department G at a selling price. The goods were not sold at the end of the accounting period. Which account is affected at the time of preparing a combined balance sheet?
explanation: Branch stock account is an account which records transactions in regard to the stock in the branch at invoice price. If the goods were not sold at the end of the accounting period, it will affect the stock.

topics.title: DEPARTMENTAL ACCOUNT : PRESENTATION OF FINAL ACCOUNTS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The correct entry to reflect the receipt of cash sent by a branch to head office is
options: debit cash and credit branch current account
debit branch current account and credit cash
credit branch current account and debit branch debtors
explanation: When a branch sends cash to the head office, the head office will debit (increase) its cash account because it is receiving cash, which is an asset. At the same time, it will credit (decrease) the branch current account because the branch owes less to the head office after sending the cash. Therefore, the correct entry is to debit cash and credit branch current account.

topics.title: BRANCH ACCOUNTING : BRANCH ADJUSTMENT METHOD

q: To account for expenses paid by head office on behalf of the branch, the branch should

credit cash and debit profit and loss account
credit profit and loss account and debit head office account
explanation: The head office maintains a book (branch account) more or less like other personal accounts so that any expenses incurred on behalf of the branch will also be debited to the branch account. Similarly, in the branch books, there will be head office accounts.

topics.title: BRANCH ACCOUNTING : BRANCH ADJUSTMENT METHOD

q: The officer responsible for ascertaining whether all public expenditure and appropriation are in line with approve guidelines is the
explanation: The Auditor General is the officer responsible for ascertaining whether all public expenditure and appropriation are in line with approved guidelines. The role of the Auditor General is to audit the accounts of all government departments and agencies to ensure that public funds are being used appropriately and in accordance with approved guidelines. This includes checking that expenditure and appropriations are being made correctly.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: In an incomplete record, the preparation of bank reconciliation ensures that
explanation: In an incomplete record, the preparation of a bank reconciliation statement ensures that the cash book is correct. A bank reconciliation statement is a statement prepared at the end of every month or at the end of any period to reconcile the bank balance as per cash book with the balance as per the bank statement. It helps to detect any errors or omissions in the cash book or the bank statement. It does not ensure that all cash takings are banked, there are no overdrafts or there are no hidden loans.
