topics.title: MANUFACTURING ACCOUNTS : INTRODUCTION AND PURPOSE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
I. Orientation
II. Entity
III. Legal status
IV. Finance

Which of the characteristics above distinguishes a profit-making from a not-for-profit-making organization?
explanation: All the characteristics listed (Orientation, Entity, Legal status, Finance) distinguish a profit-making from a not-for-profit-making organization. Orientation refers to the goal or purpose of the organization, which is to make profit in a profit-making organization and to serve the public in a not-for-profit organization. Entity refers to the nature of the organization, which can be a business entity in a profit-making organization and a social entity in a not-for-profit organization. Legal status refers to the legal recognition and structure of the organization, which can be a corporation, partnership, or sole proprietorship in a profit-making organization and a trust, society, or association in a not-for-profit organization. Finance refers to the source of funds, which is through sales and investments in a profit-making organization and through donations and grants in a not-for-profit organization.

explanation: The only advantage that can be derived from single entry system of account is its simplicity in terms of operation. It does not take into consideration the principle of double entry or border to have final account figure.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Given:
₦
Sales 195 200
Stock 1st January 34 000
Purchases 126 000
Sales returns1 200
Purchases returns 2 000

If the gross profit is ₦66,000, what is the value of stock as at 31st December?
explanation:
Gross profit = Sales - Sales returns - Cost of goods sold
66,000 = 195,200 - 1,200 - Cost of goods sold
Cost of goods sold = 194,000 - 66,000
= 128,000

Cost of goods sold = Opening stock - Purchases returns + Purchases - Closing stock
128,000 = 34,000 - 2,000 + 126,000 - Closing stock
Closing stock = 158,000 - 128,000
= #30,000

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Given;

₦(m)
Balance at 31st December 14744
Treasury Bills issued Jan-Dec 7124
Revenue for the year 6387
Expenditure 8767


What is the opening balance on the consolidated revenue fund account?
explanation:
The opening balance on the consolidated revenue fund account can be calculated using the formula:
Opening Balance = Closing Balance + Expenditure - Revenue - Treasury Bills issued.

From the given data, we can substitute the values into the formula as follows:
Opening Balance = 14,744 + 8,767 - 6,387 - 7,124.
= '₦10, 000m'.

options: used to pay all taxes due to government

used in paying the debts and liabilities of the firm to person who are not partners
explanation: The proceeds from assets of partnership will be used to settle outsiders first i.e.,the creditors of the partnership. The rest will then be used to settle the partners as agreed upon in the deed of partnership.

topics.title: DISSOLUTION OF PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : INTRODUCTION AND RULES FOR DISTRIBUTING OF ASSSETS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: In the public sector, the method of accounting that reports revenues and expenditures in the period in which they are received and paid is called
explanation: Cash accounting method recognizes Income/Revenue when it is received and expenditures when they are paid for. This is in contrast to accrual accounting method that recognizes revenue and expenditures when they are earned and incurred, not necessarily when cash is involved.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : INTRODUCTION AND USERS OF GOVERNMENT ACCOUNTING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following is used to update the cash book in bank reconciliation?
explanation: In bank reconciliation, the cash book is updated for items that the company knows about, but the bank does not. Unpresented cheques and direct credits are such items. Unpresented cheques are cheques that the company has issued, but have not yet been presented to the bank for payment. Direct credits are amounts directly deposited into the company's bank account, which the company would be aware of, but the bank might not have recorded yet.

Institute of Chartered Accountants of Nigeria and the Association of National Accountants of Nigeria
Nigerian Accounting Association and the Executive Cost and Management Accountants of Nigeria
explanation: Institute of Chartered Accountants of Nigeria(ICAN) and the Association of National Accountants of Nigeria(ANA). ICAN was created by an Act of Parliament No. 15 of 1 September 1965 while ANAN was founded on 1 January 1979 and was incorporated on 28 September 1983. The Association was chartered on 25 August 1993 by Decree 76 of 1993.

The transaction would be recorded in the cash book as debit
explanation: The transaction involves cash being taken from the business and banked. This means cash is decreasing and bank is increasing. In double entry bookkeeping, a decrease is credited and an increase is debited. Therefore, the transaction would be recorded as 'cash and credit bank'.

explanation: Substance over form is an accounting concept which means that the economic substance of transactions and events must be recorded in the financial statements rather than just their legal form in order to present a true and fair view of the affairs of the entity.

explanation: The transaction that completes its double entry in the same ledger account is a cash transaction. This is because both the debit and credit aspects of the transaction are recorded in the same ledger account. Therefore, it appears in both the cash account and bank account.

explanation: The recording phase of accounting is known as bookkeeping. Bookkeeping is the process of recording all financial transactions made by a business. subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
During the year ended 31st, December 2001, Nuju Enterprises paid rent as follows;

Jan. 20 ₦24 000 for a period of 4 months
May 16 ₦ 30 000 for a period of 5 months
Oct. 15 ₦ 36 000 for a period of 6 months
What is the overpaid portion of the rent?
explanation:
Accounting period is usually a year (12 months).
A month rent will be 6000 for each month
Therefore 6000 x 12
= 72000

Total amount he paid for rent = 24000+30000+36000
= 90000

Overpaid rent = 90000 - 72000
= 18000

Alternatively
Nuju paid rent as follows : 4 months, 5 months and 6 month
Therefore, total months = 4+5+6 = 15 which exceeds 12 months
The overpaid month is 15 - 12
= 3

Since he pays 6000 a month, then 3 x 6000
= 18000

explanation: The Income and Expenditure Account is more informative than the Receipts and Payments Account because it discloses not only cash transactions but also non-cash items like depreciation, provision for doubtful debts, etc. It also includes stocks, which are a significant part of a company's assets. options: preference share capital, cash and allotment accounts
nominal value and ordinary share capital accounts
explanation: When ordinary shares are issued, the company receives cash from the shareholders. This cash is recorded in the bank account of the company. At the same time, the company's equity increases, which is recorded in the ordinary share capital account. Therefore, the necessary accounts to be opened on the issue of ordinary share capital are bank and ordinary share capital accounts.

explanation: The gross profit in the branch adjustment account is transferred to the profit and loss account. The profit and loss account is a financial statement that summarizes the revenues, costs, and expenses incurred during a specific period of time. The gross profit is the profit a company makes after deducting the costs associated with making and selling its products, or the costs associated with providing its services.

topics.title: ISSUES OF SHARES : CALLS IN ARREARS AND FORFEITURE OF SHARES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The accounting principle that is applied to check arbitrary actions on the part of accountants is
explanation: The accounting principle that is applied to check arbitrary actions on the part of accountants is Objectivity. The objectivity principle is a concept that stresses the need for accountants to remain unbiased and impartial when recording and reporting financial information. It ensures that financial statements are free from personal bias.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : ACCOUNTING CONCEPTS AND CONVENTIONS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The loss of equipment in a fire disaster results in
options: an increase in liabilities and a decrease in assets
explanation: The loss of equipment in a fire disaster results in a decrease in assets and capital. Usually, equipments are listed as non-current assets on the balance sheet.

topics.title: DEPRECIATION OF FIXED ASSETS : CAUSES AND ELEMENTS OF DEPRECIATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A source document is used for verifying the
explanation: A source document is a document in which a business transaction is first recorded. It serves as evidence that the transaction did occur. When a business owes money to its suppliers for goods or services purchased, the suppliers will send invoices to the business. These invoices are source documents that verify the amount due to creditors.

topics.title: SUBSIDIARY BOOKS : SOURCE DOCUMENT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The rule of accounting equation requires that account payable should be placed under
explanation: The accounting equation is a fundamental principle in double-entry bookkeeping. It states that assets = liabilities + equity. Accounts payable is a liability because it represents the amount of money a business owes to its creditors. Therefore, according to the rule of the accounting equation, accounts payable should be placed under 'Liabilities'.

explanation: A suspense account in the books of an organization is one in which items are entered temporarily before allocation to the correct or final account. A debit in it will be a current asset.

topics.title: SUSPENSE ACCOUNT : MEANING AND USES OF SUSPENSE ACCOUNT

explanation: Payee is a person to whom is paid or is to be paid, especially the person to whom a cheque is made payable.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : MEANING AND OBJECTIVES OF ACCOUNTING AND BOOKKEEPING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The imprest account is subsidiary to the
explanation: The imprest account is subsidiary to the Petty cash. The imprest system is a system of financial accounting mainly used in managing petty cash. The main idea behind the system is that a fixed amount is reserved, which after a certain period of time or when circumstances require, because money was spent, it will be replenished.

topics.title: PETTY CASH BOOK : MEANING AND IMPORTANCE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The three-column cash book differs from the two-column cash book in
explanation:
A two-column cash book has two columns: one for cash receipts and one for cash payments. A three-column cash book has three columns: one for cash receipts, one for cash payments, and one for discounts.

Discounts are reductions in the price of goods or services. They can be given for a variety of reasons, such as early payment, bulk purchases, or loyalty.

The discount column in a three-column cash book is used to record all discounts received and allowed. Discounts received are recorded on the debit side of the column, and discounts allowed are recorded on the credit side of the column.

explanation:
₦(54,000 + 7,600 + 22,100)
= ₦83,700

Closing Capital = ₦73,800

Personal Drawings = ₦83,700 - ₦73,800
= ₦9,900.

options: added to fixed assets on the balance sheet

deducted from the profit and loss account
added to the current liabilities of the account
explanation: Provision for depreciation account is the liability of business. By making provision for depreciation account, company's balance sheet will reflect the current value of fixed assets. When asset is sold, it accumulated provision for depreciation will be transfer from the credit side of provision for depreciation account. Therefore, It is deducted from fixed assets on the balance sheet.

credit side of the debtors' control account
credit side of the creditors' control account
explanation: In dual entry accounting system, a contra entry is an entry which is recorded to reverse or offset an entry on the other side of an account. explanation: When converting from single entry to final account, the balancing figures on the income and expenses accounts are transferred to the profit and loss account. subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Cost classification in relation to charges is determined by the level of
explanation: Cost classification in relation to charges is determined by the level of activity. This is because costs can be classified into fixed, variable, and semi-variable costs based on the level of activity or volume of production. Fixed costs do not change with the level of activity, variable costs change proportionately with the level of activity, and semi-variable costs have both fixed and variable components.

topics.title: ACCOUNTS OF NON PROFIT MAKING ORGANISATION : INCOME AND EXPENDITURE ACCOUNTS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer this question.
On January 1, 2000, a club's assets and liabilities were as follows.
₦
Club house 8 000
Subscription in arrears 300
Cash 1 000
Wages owed 100

The club has 50 members and annual subscription is ₦100 per member. Subscription received is ₦4,000 and this includes 1999 arrears of ₦200
In the balance sheet,the subscription in arrears will be
explanation:
Subscription in arrears is a liability because it is a debt that the club owes to its members. The members have already paid for their subscriptions, but the club has not yet provided them with the services or benefits that they are entitled to.

The amount of subscription in arrears is ₦300, because this is the amount that the members from 1999 have not yet paid.

topics.title: ACCOUNTS OF NON PROFIT MAKING ORGANISATION : INCOME AND EXPENDITURE ACCOUNTS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Where the debit side of the income and expenditure account is higher than the credit side, the difference is a
explanation: The closing balance of this account shows a surplus or deficit for the year. If the credit side exceeds the debit side, there is surplus. On the other hand, if the debit side exceeds the credit side, there is a deficit. The surplus is added to the Capital Fund while the deficit is deducted from the Capital Fund.

topics.title: ACCOUNTS OF NON PROFIT MAKING ORGANISATION : INCOME AND EXPENDITURE ACCOUNTS

explanation: Subscription in advance is the sum of money prepaid by the members of club representing their dues for the next year. It is treated as a liability in the balance sheet in a non for profit making organization.

explanation: Departmental Accounts are accounts relating to the several departments or sections of a busi­ness drawn up with a view to ascertaining their individual performances. A business may have a number of departments each dealing in a different type of goods.

explanation:

= ₦2,400.

explanation: Here, the consideration is for sale is settled on a later date. The seller provides the credit period to pay the bill on the later date. Therefore, the buyer becomes the debtor.

topics.title: DEPARTMENTAL ACCOUNT : INTRODUCTION AND ADVANTAGES OF DEPARTMENTAL ACCOUNT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Discounts granted to branch customers are treated as a debit to branch
options: stock account and a credit to branch debtors' account
discount account and a credit to branch bank account
stock account and a credit to branch bank account
discount account and a credit to branch debtors' account
explanation: Discounts granted to branch customers are treated as a debit to the branch discount account and a credit to the branch debtors' account. This is because when a discount is granted, it reduces the amount that the customer owes to the branch (hence, a credit to the branch debtors' account). At the same time, the discount amount is recorded as an expense for the branch (hence, a debit to the branch discount account).

q: Transfers from the head office to branches are best carried out at
explanation:
Transferring goods or products from the head office to branches is typically done at a price that includes the cost of the goods plus a mark-up. This mark-up is often added to cover additional expenses and generate a profit for the branch.

By using the cost plus mark-up approach, the branch receives the goods at a price that reflects the cost incurred by the head office, along with an additional amount that accounts for the branch's operational costs and potential profit.

topics.title: BRANCH ACCOUNTING : PRICING METHODS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: On dissolution, the final distribution of cash to partners is based on
explanation: Dissolution doesn't always end up with liquidation. It is based on their capital balances. The final distribution of cash to the partners shall be made based on their profit and loss sharing agreement.

topics.title: DISSOLUTION OF PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : INTRODUCTION AND RULES FOR DISTRIBUTING OF ASSSETS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer this question

Jumoke and Kunle have an original investment of ₦25,000 and ₦15,000 respectively in a partnership. The articles of partnership provide 3% interest on capital and salaries of ₦1,500 and ₦1,000 respectively for the parties. The profit & loss are to be shared in the ratio 3:2. Miscellaneous expenses of ₦2,500 were incurred with gross profit of ₦6,500 during the financial year.

The balance of profit and loss account will be
explanation:
Total expenses = Miscellaneous expenses + Salaries
= ₦2,500 + ₦1,500 + ₦1,000
= ₦5,000

Net profit = Gross profit - Total expenses
= ₦6,500 - ₦5,000
= ₦1,500

Now that we know the net profit, we can calculate the share of each partner.
Jumoke's share of profit = Net profit x Jumoke's profit ratio
= ₦1,500 x 3/5
= ₦900

Kunle's share of profit = Net profit x Kunle's profit ratio
= ₦1,500 x 2/5
= ₦600

The balance of the profit and loss account is calculated by subtracting the partners' shares of profit from the net profit.

Balance of profit and loss account = Net profit - Partners' shares of profit
= ₦1,500 - ₦900 - ₦600
= ₦300

topics.title: PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : ACCOUNTING ENTRIES AT THE END OF THE FINANCIAL YEAR

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer this question

Jumoke and Kunle have an original investment of ₦25,000 and ₦15,000 respectively in a partnership. The articles of partnership provide 3% interest on capital and salaries of ₦1,500 and ₦1,000 respectively for the parties. The profit & loss are to be shared in the ratio 3:2. Miscellaneous expenses of ₦2,500 were incurred with gross profit of ₦6,500 during the financial year.
What is Kunle's share of the profit?
explanation:
Removing the interests, 3% of ₦25,000 = ₦750 and 3% of ₦15,000 = ₦450

Salaries of ₦1,000 and ₦1,500, we have ₦(4,000 - 750 - 450 - 1,000 - 1,500)
= ₦300

Kunle's share of the profit will be = 2/5 × ₦300
= ₦120.

topics.title: PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : ACCOUNTING ENTRIES AT THE END OF THE FINANCIAL YEAR

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: When a new partner is admitted to a partnership there is a need to revolve the
explanation: When a new partner is admitted to a partnership, there is a need to revalue the assets and capital of the business. The assets need to be revalued because the new partner will be contributing to the assets of the business and the capital needs to be revalued to reflect the new partner's contribution. This is done to ensure that the new partner's capital account reflects their true contribution to the business.

topics.title: GOODWILL ACCOUNTS : TREATMENT OF GOODWILL ON ADMISSION OF PARTNERS AND CHANGES IN RATIO

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The accounting entry to record the premium on goodwill is to debit
explanation: The accounting entry to record the premium on goodwill is to debit goodwill and credit revaluation. Goodwill is an intangible asset that represents the value of a business's reputation and customer relationships. When a premium is paid for goodwill, it increases the value of the goodwill asset. This is recorded as a debit to the goodwill account. The credit entry is made to the revaluation account, which reflects the increase in the value of the business's assets.

topics.title: GOODWILL ACCOUNTS : GOODWILL VALUATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The expenses incurred in promoting a company are
explanation: Before incorporation and commencement of business, the company and the promoters of the company may incur so many types of expenses like statutory fees and company logo designing. In some cases, rent for the office premises during the time of incorporation not after incorporation etc. All these come under preliminary expenses. In simple words, preliminary expenses are the expenses that are spent by the promoters before the incorporation of the company.

topics.title: COMPANY ACCOUNT : FORMATION OF COMPANY

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Debentures can be redeemed out of
explanation: Debentures can be redeemed out of the proceeds from a new issue of shares. This is because when a company issues new shares, it raises additional capital. This capital can then be used to redeem (or pay off) the debentures. Debentures are a type of debt instrument that companies use to raise capital, and they must be paid back at a certain date. Redeeming them out of the proceeds from a new issue of shares is one way to do this.

What is the value of the authorized share capital?
explanation:
The authorized share capital is the maximum amount of share capital that the company is authorized by its constitutional documents to issue to shareholders. In this case, there are 600,000 10% preference shares of ₦0.50 each and 600,000 ordinary shares of ₦1 each.

Therefore, The authorized share capital is 600,000 shares of ₦1 = ₦600,000.

explanation: Share premium is the amount by which the amount received by a company for a stock issue exceeds its face value. It is a capital reserve.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: According to the provisions of the 1999 constitution, all revenue generated by the Federal Government must be paid into the
options: Revenue Mobilization Allocation and Fiscal Commission's account,
explanation: Consolidated revenue fund shows the total income derived by the government from all the sources of revenue. Revenue set aside for other funds will not be included in the fund.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : SOURCES AND CLASSIFICATION OF GOVERNMENT REVENUE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The budget statement presented to the legislature by the Chief Executive is called
explanation: The budget statement presented to the legislature by the Chief Executive is called a budget bill. A budget bill is a proposal for government spending that is presented to the legislature for approval. It outlines the government's financial plans, including its revenue and spending. This is different from an appropriation act or bill, which is a law passed by the legislature that authorizes the government to spend money. A budget proposal is a more general term that could refer to any proposal for a budget, not necessarily one presented to the legislature.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : ESTIMATES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The statement of assets and liabilities in the public sector can be described as the
explanation: The statement of assets and liabilities in the public sector can be described as the balance sheet of government. In accounting, a balance sheet is a financial statement that reports a company's assets, liabilities, and shareholders' equity at a specific point in time. It provides a basis for computing rates of return and evaluating its capital structure. Similarly, the balance sheet of government provides a snapshot of the financial position of the government at a specific point in time, including its assets (what it owns) and liabilities (what it owes).

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : INTRODUCTION AND USERS OF GOVERNMENT ACCOUNTING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The double entry principle of accounting was developed by
explanation: Italian merchants were the first to use double entry system to record their transactions as far back as 14th century. Luca Pacioli, an Italian was the first person to publish a book on double-entry. In the year 1494 precisely.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : ACCOUNTING CONCEPTS AND CONVENTIONS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The concept that has much influence over asset valuation and income determination is
explanation: The matching concept is the accounting principle that requires that the expenses incurred during a period be recorded in the same period in which the related revenues are earned.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : ACCOUNTING CONCEPTS AND CONVENTIONS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The journal entries for the purchase of stock on credit are to debit
explanation: When a business purchases stock on credit, it increases its inventory (stock) and also incurs a liability (the amount owed to the supplier). Therefore, the correct journal entry is to debit the stock account (increase in assets) and credit the liability account (increase in liabilities). This is in accordance with the double-entry bookkeeping system where every transaction has a dual effect and the total debits must equal total credits.

topics.title: TRIAL BALANCE : MEANING AND IMPORTANCE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: What are the appropriate recording procedures for entries in the trial balance?
explanation:
Following Steps are involved in the preparation of a Trial Balance:
- All Ledger Accounts are closed at the end of an accounting period.
- Ledger balances are posted into the trial balance.
- Trial Balance is cast and errors are identified.
- Suspense account is created to agree the trial balance totals temporarily until corrections are accounted for.
- Errors identified earlier are rectified by posting corrective entries.

topics.title: TRIAL BALANCE : RULES AND NATURE OF TRIAL BALANCE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Mr. Bassey purchased a motor vehicle for use in his business and debited the purchases account with the same value. This is an error of
explanation: The error made by Mr. Bassey is an error of principle. In accounting, an error of principle occurs when the correct amount is posted in the wrong type of account. In this case, Mr. Bassey debited the purchases account instead of the motor vehicle account. This is a violation of the accounting principles, hence it is an error of principle.

In accounting, a credit balance in a bank account indicates that the account holder has funds in the account.

Expected balance = 1 500 000 (Credit balance) + 1 700 000
= 3,200,000 is the total credit balance

explanation: A rent received by a trader for sub-letting part of his business premises should be added to the total revenue. This is because the rent received is an income for the business and all incomes are added to the total revenue of the business.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Goods withdrawn from business for private use are credited to
explanation: When goods are withdrawn from the business for private use, it reduces the owner's equity in the business. This is recorded as a debit to the drawings account. Therefore, goods withdrawn for private use are credited to the drawings account. The drawings account is a contra account to the owner's equity or capital account. It represents the amount of goods, cash, or other assets the owner has taken from the business for personal use.

explanation: Overhead manufacturing cost refers to the ongoing costs of operating a business. These are the costs not directly tied to a specific product, or the costs that are not directly accountable to the cost object. In this case, labour and rent are part of overhead manufacturing cost. However, labour is usually considered as direct cost if it is directly attributable to the production of goods. Therefore, the correct answer is 'rent'.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Departmental accounts are prepared to aid the comparison of the
explanation: Departmental accounts are financial statements that break down a company's financial performance by different departments or segments within the organization. These accounts help in assessing the performance of different departments and their contribution to the overall profitability of the company. It allows management to compare the financial performance of various departments and make informed decisions to improve the organization's overall efficiency and profitability.

topics.title: DEPARTMENTAL ACCOUNT : METHODS OF APPORTIONMENT OF EXPENSES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The movement of goods from head office to a branch is treated in the head office account as
explanation: When goods are moved from the head office to a branch, it is treated as a purchase in the head office account. This is because the head office is essentially 'buying' the goods for the branch. This does not mean that the head office is making a profit from the branch, but rather that it is accounting for the cost of the goods that it is providing to the branch.

topics.title: BRANCH ACCOUNTING : BRANCH ADJUSTMENT METHOD

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The head office of Emeka stores maintains the financial account of all its branches. The branch store account in the head office serves as the branch
explanation: The branch store account in the head office serves as the branch debtors' account. This is because the head office views the branch as a debtor, since the branch sells goods on behalf of the head office. The branch account is debited with all goods sent to the branch and credited with all cash remittances from the branch to the head office.

topics.title: BRANCH ACCOUNTING : INTRODUCTION AND ADVANTAGES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: In reconciling the branch and head office accounts, remittance in transit in the branch books is treated as a
explanation: In reconciling the branch and head office accounts, remittance in transit in the branch books is treated as a debit entry. This is because remittance in transit refers to the cash that has been sent by the branch to the head office but has not yet been received or recorded by the head office. Therefore, it is a debit entry in the head office books as it represents an increase in the cash balance of the head office.

topics.title: BRANCH ACCOUNTING : BRANCH ADJUSTMENT METHOD

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Profit or loss in a partnership is usually arrived at after deducting from gross profit all expenses including
explanation: In a partnership, the profit or loss is usually arrived at after deducting all expenses from the gross profit. This includes partners' salaries. Interest on capital, partner drawing, and interest on loan are not usually deducted from gross profit to arrive at the profit or loss. They are usually considered after the profit or loss has been determined.

Calculate the profit available for appropriation
explanation:
The profit available for appropriation is calculated by subtracting all the expenses from the total profit. In this case, the total profit is ₦10 000.

Profit for the year - partner's salaries = ₦10 000 - ₦2 000
= ₦ 8 000.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A company paid cash in redemption of ₦10 000 debenture. The accounting entry to record this will be to debit
explanation: When a company pays cash to redeem debentures, it means the company is repaying its long-term liabilities (debentures). In accounting, this transaction will be recorded by debiting (decreasing) the debentures account and crediting (decreasing) the cash account. Therefore, the correct accounting entry is to debit debentures and credit cash.

explanation: Earning per share measures the net returns on investment. Earning per share = Profit after tax/Equity.

explanation: Accrual concept means that revenues and expenses are recognized as they are earned or incurred and not when money is received or paid. The profit of a business will show the difference between the revenues and expenses.

explanation: Financial memorandum may be describe as a kind of formal documentation of revenue, cost and expenditure of government administration giving out the basis of which it will receive its allocation. The Local governments uses financial memorandum.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : INTRODUCTION AND USERS OF GOVERNMENT ACCOUNTING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Fatimo withdraws goods from the business for personal use. The accounting treatment is to debit
options: stock account and credit profit and loss account
profit and loss account and credit drawings account
explanation: When goods are withdrawn from the business for personal use, it is considered as drawings. In accounting, the drawings account is debited because it represents a decrease in owner's equity. The stock account is credited because it represents a decrease in assets. Therefore, the correct accounting treatment is to debit drawings account and credit stock account.

explanation:
Depreciation = cost - residual value/useful life

= 56,000 - 1,000/5

= 55,000/5

= ₦11,000.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The objective of the purchase ledger control account is to establish the
explanation:
The purchase ledger is a sub-ledger in which purchases are recorded. The purchase ledger is part of the accounting department's database; it is not maintained by the purchasing department.

The ledger is useful for segregating into one location a record of the amounts a company spends with its suppliers. The purchase ledger shows which purchases have been paid for and which purchases remain outstanding.

q: The objective of a trading account is to establish the
explanation: A trading account is similar to a traditional bank account, holding cash and securities and it's objective is to determine gross profit .

topics.title: THE FINAL ACCOUNTS OF A SOLE TRADER : TRADING ACTIVITIES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A seller of vegetables and fruits should be encouraged to value his stock at the end of a period using the
explanation: FIFO method will be encouraged. This is due to the fluctuations of the economy and the risk that the cost of producing goods will rise over time. Also, businesses using FIFO are considered to be more profitable — at least, on paper. For example, the fruit seller purchases fruits at regular intervals to stock its shelves. As customers purchase, the stock pushes the oldest product to the front of the shelf and replace newer stock behind those cartons.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The difference in the sales ledger control account is transferred to the
explanation: The difference in the sales ledger control account is transferred to the Balance Sheet. On the balance sheet it is known as trade debtors or account receivables. The sales ledger control account is a part of the general ledger and is used to record the total of all sales made on credit. Any difference in this account would be due to an error and would be adjusted in the balance sheet.

topics.title: CONTROL ACCOUNTS : SALES LEDGER CONTROL ACCOUNT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Tunde purchased goods from Femi by cash worth ₦1 000 with 10% cash discount. Tunde's purchases ledger control account is to be
explanation:
When Tunde, the buyer, purchases goods worth ₦1,000 with a 10% cash discount, he pays only ₦900. However, we need to correctly reflect this in the purchases ledger control account and the related accounts.

The correct accounting entries should be:
Debit the Purchases account (or Purchases Ledger Control account) by the full amount of the purchase (₦1,000), since this represents the total value of the goods bought.
Credit the Cash account by the amount actually paid (₦900), since this is the cash outflow.
Credit the Discount Received account by the cash discount (₦100), as this represents the discount Tunde received.
Therefore, the Purchases Ledger Control account should be debited by the full amount of the purchase, not just the discount.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Gross earnings 950 000
Current liabilities 100 000
Operating expenses 350 000
10% debentures 400 000
Shareholders' funds 1 300 000
Corporate tax is 30%

From the data above, calculate the return on equity
explanation:
Earnings before interest and taxes = gross earnings - operating expenses
= 950,000 - 350,000
= 600,000

Interest on debenture = 10% x 400,000
= 40,000

Earnings before taxes = 600,000 - 40,000
= 560,000

Corporate tax = 30% x 560,000
= 168,000

Net income = 560,000 - 168,000
= 392,000

Return on equity = net income/shareholders fund x 100

= 392,000/1300000 x 100
= 30.1%

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Accrual accounting differs from cash accounting because it recognizes
explanation: The difference between cash and accrual accounting lies in the timing of when sales and purchases are recorded in your accounts. Cash accounting recognizes revenue and expenses only when money changes hands, but accrual accounting recognizes revenue when it's earned, and expenses when they're billed (but not paid).

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : ACCOUNTING CONCEPTS AND CONVENTIONS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A private company is different from a public company because
options: it does not restrict the right to transfer its shares
it cannot invite members of the public to subscribe for its shares
its shares are owned by one person
it can only offer its shares to members of the public for subscription
explanation: The key difference between public and private companies is that public companies can generate funds by issuing shares to the public. Private companies are only allowed to issue stock to existing shareholders or current employers. However, in some cases, they can raise money from the public, but they must meet certain requirements.

explanation: The cash discount is allowed for prompt payment of an account or for payment within a specified period of time. It is divided into discount allowed and discount received making the cash discount the key factor.

topics.title: CASH BOOK : THREE COLUMN CASH BOOK

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A major factor that enhanced the early development accounting is the
explanation: The growth of trade and commerce significantly enhanced the early development of accounting. As trade and commerce expanded, there was a greater need for a systematic method of recording transactions. This led to the development of accounting. While the Industrial Revolution, the development of arithmetic, and the invention of writing all played roles in the evolution of accounting, they were not the major factors in its early development.

explanation: A sales invoice in financial accounting is a tool that a company uses to communicate to clients about the sums that are due in exchange for goods that have been sold. A sales invoice should include information about which items the customer has purchased, the quantities he has bought, discounts he has received, and the total amount he owes. In addition, a sales invoice should contain a brief summary of the terms of the transaction, such as the acceptable lag time between the sale and the payment. It is used to prepare the debtor's ledger account.

On January 30 Khalifa withdrew ₦2 500 from the bank for office use.
What was the bank balance at the end of the month?
explanation:
The bank balance at the end of the month can be calculated by adding the initial balance and the amounts credited to the bank, then subtracting the amounts debited from the bank. The initial balance is ₦5,000.

The amounts credited to the bank are ₦3,000 and ₦1,500, which sum up to ₦4,500. The amounts debited from the bank are ₦500 and ₦2,500 (withdrawn for office use), which sum up to ₦3,000.

Therefore, the bank balance at the end of the month is ₦5,000 + ₦4,500 - ₦3,000
= ₦6,500.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: When goodwill is not retained in the business, the entries in the new partners' books will be debit
options: goodwill account and credit partners' capital account
Partners capital account and credit goodwill account
explanation: Goodwill is an intangible asset that represents the non-physical assets, such as brand name, customer relationships, etc., that a company has. When goodwill is not retained in the business, it means that the value of these intangible assets is being reduced. In accounting terms, this reduction is recorded as a debit to the goodwill account and a credit to the partners' capital account. This reflects the decrease in the value of the company's assets and the corresponding decrease in the value of the partners' equity in the company.

Calculate the subscription in the income and expenditure account
explanation:
The subscription in the income and expenditure account can be calculated by adding the subscriptions received to the subscriptions owed and subtracting the subscriptions in advance.

In this case, the subscriptions received were ₦200,000, the subscriptions owed were ₦10,000, and the subscriptions in advance were ₦25,000.

Therefore, the subscription in the income and expenditure account is:
= ₦200,000 + ₦10,000 - ₦25,000
= ₦185,000.

In accounting, a debit entry in an account represents an increase in expenses or assets or a decrease in income, liability, or owner's equity. Therefore, a loss, which is an expense, is debited.

explanation: When a partnership is converted to a company, the expenses incurred during the conversion process are debited to the realization account. subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: In the trading and profit and loss account of a manufacturing organization, purchases is
options: always the same amount as the total factory overhead cost
equivalent to the total cost of goods manufactured
always the same as the prime cost
explanation: In the trading and profit and loss account of a manufacturing organization, 'purchases' is given separately. This is because 'purchases' refers to the raw materials bought for the production process, which is a direct cost and is different from the total factory overhead cost (indirect costs), the total cost of goods manufactured (which includes direct labor and factory overheads in addition to raw materials), and the prime cost (which includes direct materials and direct labor).

topics.title: MANUFACTURING ACCOUNTS : INTRODUCTION AND PURPOSE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Goods worth ₦50,000 were sent at different times from head office to the branch during the year. By the end of the period, only ₦40,000 worth of goods had arrived at the branch. Which of the following is correct about the treatment of this transaction?
options: Branch should debit goods received from head office with ₦50,000
Head office should debit goods sent to branch account with ₦10,000
Head office should debit goods sent to branch account with ₦50,000
Branch should debit goods received from head office with ₦10,000
explanation:
The head office incurs the cost of the goods, regardless of whether they reach the branch or not. Therefore, the head office should record the full value (₦50,000) of the goods sent to the branch in a "Goods Sent to Branch" account (or similar). This reflects the cost of inventory the head office is no longer holding.

Branch Perspective: The branch should only record the goods they have physically received. In this case, they received goods worth ₦40,000. They would credit a "Goods Received from Head Office" account (or similar) for this amount.

The discrepancy (₦10,000 worth of missing goods) will need further investigation. It could be due to damage, loss during transit, or delays in recording. However, for accounting purposes, the head office recognizes the initial cost of all goods sent, and the branch records only what they receive.

topics.title: BRANCH ACCOUNTING : BRANCH ADJUSTMENT METHOD

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: An accounting ratio that considers only quick assets to determine the short-term safety margin of a firm is the
explanation: The acid-test or quick ratio or liquid ratio measures the ability of a company to use its near cash or quick assets to extinguish or retire its current liabilities immediately. Quick assets include those current assets that presumably can be quickly converted to cash at close to their book values.

A company's authorized share capital was 1,000,000 ordinary shares at ₦1 each. It issued 80% of its shares at ₦1.10 per share. 70k was payable on application, 25k on allotment, 10k due on first call and the balance on second call.

What would be the total amount received on second call?
explanation:
The total amount received on the second call is the balance after the application, allotment, and first call.

This is calculated as ₦1.10 - (₦0.70 + ₦0.25 + ₦0.10)
= ₦0.05.

Therefore, the total amount received on the second call is:
800,000 shares x ₦0.05
= ₦40,000.

explanation:

This is calculated as 800,000 shares x ₦0.70
= ₦560,000.

topics.title: DEPARTMENTAL ACCOUNT : METHODS OF APPORTIONMENT OF EXPENSES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The gross profit disclosed in the branch stock adjustment account represents
explanation:
In accounting, the branch stock adjustment account is used to record adjustments made to the branch's stock or inventory. This account is maintained to reconcile any differences between the stock records of the head office and the branch.

When there is a discrepancy in the stock valuation between the head office and the branch, adjustments are made to correct the values. These adjustments can be due to various factors such as stock losses, damaged goods, or differences in valuation methods.

The gross profit disclosed in the branch stock adjustment account reflects the profit earned by the branch. It represents the difference between the sales revenue and the cost of goods sold for the branch's operations.

topics.title: BRANCH ACCOUNTING : BRANCH ADJUSTMENT METHOD

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Partner's share of profit is credited to
explanation: A partner's share of profit is credited to a partner's current account. In partnership accounting, the partner's current account is used to record the share of profits or losses of each partner, as well as any withdrawals they make from the business. The profit share is credited to the account, increasing its balance, while withdrawals are debited, decreasing the balance.

explanation: A pension granted to any past president or vice-president is charged to the Consolidated Revenue Fund. The Consolidated Revenue Fund is a principal account of the government which includes all revenues received by the government. It is used to meet the recurrent and capital expenditure of the government.

topics.title: DEPRECIATION OF FIXED ASSETS : METHODS OF DEPRECIATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer this question
An asset was purchased for ₦343m in 2003. The estimated life of the asset was 3 years with a residual value of ₦28m.
What was the asset value at the beginning of the third year?
explanation:
Depreciation per year = (Initial cost - Residual value) / Estimated life

Depreciation per year = (₦343m - ₦28m) / 3
= ₦105m

After the first year, the asset value would be:

Asset value at the end of the first year = Initial cost - Depreciation per year
Asset value at the end of the first year = ₦343m - ₦105m
= ₦238m

After the second year, the asset value would be:

Asset value at the end of the second year = Asset value at the end of the first year - Depreciation per year
Asset value at the end of the second year = ₦238m - ₦105m
= ₦133m

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : ACCOUNTING EQUATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
I. Facilitating the preparation of final accounts
II. Making the existence of error easy to detect
III. Checking the accuracy of postings periodically

From the above, which of the following are among the uses of control accounts?
explanation: Control accounts are used for facilitating the preparation of final accounts, making the existence of error easy to detect, and checking the accuracy of postings periodically. Therefore, all the options I, II, and III are correct. A control account is a summary account in the general ledger. The details that support the summary amounts in the control account will be found in a subsidiary ledger. This allows the general ledger to remain clutter-free and yet have the correct balance for the financial statements.

explanation:

= ₦25,000.

explanation: Journal proper is book of original entry (simple journal) in which miscellaneous credit transactions which do not fit in any other books are recorded. It is also called miscellaneous journal. The form and procedure for maintaining this journal is the same that of simple journal.

explanation: Cheque endorsement refers to the process where the payee or endorser of a cheque transfers his or her rights to the cheque to another party, known as the endorsee. This is done by signing the back of the cheque. Cheque crossing and payment in cheque are different concepts related to cheques, and cheque transfer is not a recognized term in banking.

explanation:

An increase in total liabilities and owners' equity by ₦15,000
An increase in the total assets by ₦15,000
explanation: A decrease in the total liabilities and assets by ₦5,000. He will debit total liabilities and credit cash.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : ACCOUNTING EQUATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: One of the benefits of book-keeping records is that they
options: give prospective investors accurate and direct information for decision-making
provide the public and creditors with details of the operations of an entity
give legal backing to all the transaction undertaken by a business entity
provide information without which the task of management would be difficult
explanation: Book-keeping records are essential for the management of a business entity. They provide detailed information about the financial transactions of the business, which helps in decision-making, planning, and controlling processes. They do not necessarily provide direct information for prospective investors or give legal backing to all transactions. While they may provide some information to the public and creditors, their primary purpose is to aid in the management of the business.

topics.title: TRIAL BALANCE : RULES AND NATURE OF TRIAL BALANCE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A sole trader purchased goods in cash worth ₦2000 at 2% discount and made a cash sale of ₦1000 at the same rate of discount. Determine the amount of discount allowed.
explanation:
The amount of discount allowed is calculated by multiplying the cash sale by the rate of discount. In this case, the cash sale is ₦1000 and the rate of discount is 2%.

Therefore, the amount of discount allowed is ₦1000 x 2/100
= ₦20.

topics.title: PRINCIPLE OF DOUBLE ENTRY : CASH AND CREDIT TRANSACTIONS

options: provides permanent records for all financial transactions
does not give room for double entry procedure
explanation: Bookkeeping is the process of recording all financial transactions on a day-to-day basis and is a key component of building a financially successful business. It provides permanent records for all financial transactions, which is essential for managing your operations and reporting to your stakeholders.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : MEANING AND OBJECTIVES OF ACCOUNTING AND BOOKKEEPING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following accounting conventions suggests that accountants should use a method of valuation that understates rather than over-states results?
explanation: The conservatism principle is the general concept of recognizing expenses and liabilities as soon as possible when there is uncertainty about the outcome, but to only recognize revenues and assets when they are assured of being received.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : ACCOUNTING CONCEPTS AND CONVENTIONS

explanation: Financial analysts need accounting information to assess the financial health of a company or the lack thereof for various reasons, such as analysis for the company itself, so it can improve its status, or investors, who are interested in investing in a company that is financially sound, or for potential lenders, who wish to be assured their loans will be paid back.

debit and credit entries in corresponding accounts
explanation: The basic principle of double entry bookkeeping is that there are always two entries for every transaction. One entry is known as a credit entry and the other a debit entry.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Bariga, a stock broker bought stationery for ₦12,000 by cash. To record this transaction, debit
explanation: In accounting, when a business buys an item, it debits the account of the item bought and credits the account from which the payment was made. In this case, Bariga bought stationery for ₦12,000 by cash. Therefore, the stationery account should be debited because the stationery is what was received (an increase in assets), and the cash account should be credited because cash was given out (a decrease in assets). Therefore, the correct option is 'stationery and credit cash'.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: An entry made in the bank column of the cash book to record movement of cash between the office and the bank is the
explanation: Since the entry involves only movement of cash, it is a contra entry. In dual entry accounting system, a contra entry is an entry which is recorded to reverse or offset an entry on the other side of an account. If a debit entry is recorded in an account, contra entry will be recorded on the credit side and vice-versa.

topics.title: PRINCIPLE OF DOUBLE ENTRY : CASH AND CREDIT TRANSACTIONS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Abba offered 5% cash discount on goods worth ₦10,000. The discount allowed account is to be
explanation:
A cash discount is a deduction allowed by the seller of goods or by the provider of services in order to motivate the customer to pay within a specified time.

The discount is often expressed as a percentage of the total amount due. In this case, Abba offered a 5% cash discount on goods worth ₦10,000.

This means the discount is 5/100 x 10,000
= ₦500.

The discount allowed account is to be debited with ₦500 because in accounting, a debit entry is made when there is an increase in expenses or assets, or a decrease in liability, equity or income. Here, the discount allowed is an expense to Abba, hence the account is debited.

topics.title: RESERVES AND PROVISIONS : PROVISION FOR DISCOUNTS ALLOWED AND RECEIVED

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Commission-on-turnover is charged on
explanation: Commission-on-turnover is a fee charged by a broker or agent for its services, such as transactions, sales, purchases, etc. It is usually calculated as a percentage of the transaction amount. In the context of banking, commission-on-turnover is typically charged on current accounts only. This is because current accounts are transactional accounts where the account holders frequently conduct deposit and withdrawal transactions. The bank charges a commission on these transactions as a service fee. Savings accounts and fixed deposit accounts are not transactional accounts and therefore do not attract commission-on-turnover.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO DATA PROCESSING : MEANING, CLASSIFICATION AND COMPONENTS OF COMPUTER

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The medium that enables the ATM to read the account details and process transactions directly with the account held in the bank is the
explanation: The ATM reads account details and processes transactions directly with the account held in the bank through the magnetic strip on the ATM card. The magnetic strip contains the cardholder's account details encoded in magnetic form. When the card is swiped or inserted into the ATM, the machine reads this information and communicates with the bank's systems to process transactions.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : ACCOUNTING EQUATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The factors that determine the periodic depreciation charge are
options: asset cost, market value and useful life
asset cost market value and residual value
asset value, cash value and useful life
explanation: The periodic depreciation charge is determined by the asset cost, its salvage value, and its useful life. The asset cost is the initial price paid for the asset. The salvage value is the estimated residual value of an asset at the end of its useful life. The useful life is the estimated time period that the asset is expected to be used by the business. The depreciation expense is calculated by subtracting the salvage value from the asset cost and then dividing by the useful life.

Aug. 10 Issued 16 units
Aug. 20 Received 40 units at ₦80 each
Aug. 31 Issued 48 units

Using the simple average method, what is the cost per unit of the closing stock?
explanation: Simple Average Method is a method for inventory valuation or delivery cost calculation, where even if accepting inventory goods with different unit cost, the average unit cost is calculated by multiplying the total of these unit costs simply by the number of receiving. In this question, the cost per unit will be ₦80.

Aug. 20 Received 40 units at ₦80 each
Aug. 31 Issued 48 units
Using the FIFO method, what is the value of the closing stock?
explanation:
Using FIFO, the value of the closing stock will be (20 + 20 - 16 + 40 - 48) at ₦80
= 16 × ₦80
= ₦1,280.

The total floor space is 200sqm (manufacturing) + 300sqm (administration)

= ₦8,000.

explanation: The formation of a company involves several stages, including the ascertainment of the particulars of the proposed company, preparation of the incorporation documents, filing of the documents, and registration of the company. topics.title: COMPANY ACCOUNT : FORMATION OF COMPANY

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Cash received from the subscribed shares is recorded by debiting
explanation: When cash is received from the subscribed shares, the cash account is debited because cash is an asset and when assets increase, they are debited according to the rules of debit and credit. The subscribed shares are credited because it represents a liability to the company as it is an amount that the company owes to its shareholders. Therefore, the correct entry is 'Debit Cash, Credit Subscribed Shares'.

topics.title: ISSUES OF SHARES : SHARES ISSUED PAYABLE BY INSTALMENTS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Ordinary shareholders received dividend before
explanation:
Deferred shareholders are a type of shareholder whose rights to dividends and capital are deferred until after the rights of all other shareholders have been met.

Deferred shares are often issued to founders or early investors as a way to compensate them for their initial risk and investment. Deferred shares may also be issued as part of a company's stock options plan, or to attract investors who are willing to accept a lower return in exchange for the potential for a higher return in the future.

While deferred shareholders have lower priority than other shareholders, they may still receive a significant return on their investment if the company is successful. Deferred shares can also be a valuable tool for companies that are looking to raise capital without diluting the ownership of existing shareholders.

explanation: The accrual concept in accounting means that expenses and revenues are recorded in the period they occur, whether or not cash is involved. explanation: A source document is the original record containing the details to substantiate a transaction entered in an accounting system. q: If Odukoya takes money out of the business bank account for his own private use, the effect of the transaction is
options: increase in assets and increase in capital
increase in asset and decrease in capital
decrease in capital and increase in assets
decrease in capital and decrease in assets
explanation: When Odukoya takes money out of the business bank account for his own private use, it decreases the assets (cash in the bank account) and also decreases the capital (equity in the business). This is because the money is no longer available for use in the business and it reduces the owner's equity in the business. Therefore, the correct option is 'decrease in capital and decrease in assets'.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Cash discount is often recorded on
options: the debit side of the cash book
the credit side of the cash book
the folio column of the cash book
both credit and debit side of the cash book
explanation:
A cash discount is a reduction in the amount a customer has to pay if they settle their invoice within a specific period.

Recording the Cash Discount:
Debit Side: When a customer takes advantage of the discount and pays a reduced amount, the cash received is debited to the cash account at the discounted amount.

Credit Side: The difference between the invoice amount and the cash received (the amount of the discount) is credited to a cash discount account. This reflects the reduction in revenue due to the discount offered.

explanation: Current assets are assets that a company expects to convert to cash or use up within one year or its operating cycle, whichever is longer. In this case, stock, bills receivable, cash, and debtors are all current assets. Stock is the inventory a business uses in its production process. Bills receivable are amounts owed to a business by its customers. Cash is the most liquid asset a company has. Debtors are people who owe money to the company.

explanation: LIFO assumes that goods which made their way to inventory (after purchase, manufacture etc.) later are sold first and those which are manufactured or acquired early are sold last. Thus LIFO assigns the cost of newer inventory to cost of goods sold and cost of older inventory to ending inventory account. This method is exactly opposite to first-in, first-out method.

explanation: A control account is a summary account in the general ledger. Apart from the common control accounts, a control account can be opened for wages account. This is because wages account is a type of expense account and it is common for businesses to maintain a control account for wages to keep track of wage expenses.

explanation: A Receipts and Payments Account is a summary of the cash book. subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:

III. Departmental turnover
IV. Number of articles sold by each department.

From the information above, the two most logical bases for apportioning expenses that are common to departments are
explanation: The two most logical bases for apportioning expenses that are common to departments are the space occupied by each department (I) and the average value of stock held by each department (II). This is because these two factors directly influence the amount of common expenses each department incurs. The space occupied by a department can determine the amount of rent, utilities and other space-related costs it should bear. Similarly, the average value of stock held by a department can determine the amount of inventory-related costs it should bear, such as insurance and storage costs.

q:
Use the information below to answer this question.
A company advertised and issued 750,000, 12% preference shares of ₦1 each to be issued at ₦1.50 per share. Applications for 1,370,000 were received at 30k per share, 70k per share (including premium) was due on allotment while 25k per share was due on each of the remaining two calls. All amount due were received. Application money for 120,000 shares was refunded to unsuccessful applicants and the remaining applicants were alloted shares on pro-rata basis.

The share premium account would be
explanation:
Premium shares = Issue price per share – Face value/par value per share x No of shares.

Issue price = N1.50

Face value = N1

No. of shares = 750,000

1.50 - 1 = 0.50

0.50 x 750,000 = 375,000

Share Application or share allotment or Share capital A/c all are personal accounts as they represent money from the shareholders and when money is due, these are to be debited because of the rule "Debit the receiver".

explanation: The Provision for Bad and Doubtful Debts will appear in the Balance Sheet. Next year, the actual amount of bad debts will be debited not to the Profit and Loss Account but to the Provision for Bad and Doubtful Debts Account which will then stand reduced.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The book value per share is obtained by dividing
explanation:
To calculate the book value per share, you must first calculate the book value, then divide by the number of common shares. Also, since you're working with common shares, you must subtract the preferred shareholder equity from the total equity. Hence we have;

Book Value per Share = (Shareholders' Equity - Preferred Equity) / Total Outstanding Common Shares.

explanation: A revenue expenditure is an amount that is expensed immediately—thereby being matched with revenues of the current accounting period. Routine repairs are revenue expenditures because they are charged directly to an account such as Repairs and Maintenance Expense.

explanation: A capital expenditure is an amount spent to acquire or improve a long-term asset such as equipment or buildings. Usually the cost is recorded in an account classified as Property, Plant and Equipment. The cost (except for the cost of land) will then be charged to depreciation expense over the useful life of the asset.

topics.title: DEPRECIATION OF FIXED ASSETS : CAUSES AND ELEMENTS OF DEPRECIATION

q: Which of the following is credited to the consolidated revenue fund?
explanation: The Consolidated Revenue Fund is the main bank account of the government. It is credited with all government revenues, including internal revenue. The other options listed are expenditures or transfers, which would be debited from the fund, not credited to it.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : SOURCES AND CLASSIFICATION OF GOVERNMENT REVENUE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: One of the reasons for the existence of the public sector is to
explanation: The public sector exists to correct perceived inequalities in society. This includes providing services and resources to those who may not be able to afford them in a purely market-driven economy. While the public sector does also provide services and care for the needy, its primary purpose is to correct inequalities, making option 4 the most accurate answer.

explanation: A ledger account is a nominal account. Nominal accounts in accounting are the temporary accounts, such as the income statement accounts. In other words, nominal accounts are the accounts that report revenues, expenses, gains, and losses.

explanation: The error committed is that of principle. An error of principle in accounting occurs when the fundamental principles of accounting are not observed or violated. subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A major cause of discrepancy between bank statement and the cash book that overstates the bank statement balance is the
explanation: A major cause of discrepancy between bank statement and the cash book that overstates the bank statement balance is the direct payment. This is because direct payments are payments made by the bank on behalf of the account holder. These payments are recorded by the bank immediately but may not be recorded in the cash book until the account holder is aware of them. This delay in recording in the cash book can cause the bank statement balance to be higher than the cash book balance.

explanation: The bank charges levied on a current account holder is the charges on transaction. This is because banks typically charge fees for services such as processing cheques, wire transfers, and overdrafts. These charges are often referred to as transaction fees. They are not based on the turnover, transfer or cash received.

Monthly drawing averaged ₦330.
Determine the net profit
explanation:
To determine the net profit, the closing capital must be determined
Closing capital = Total assets - Total liabilities
= (28000+13200+5200+4600) - (5100+1200)
= 51000 - 6300
= 44700
Since the closing capital is greater than the opening capital, then there is profit and it is calculated as:
NP = CC +D- OC
Where NP = Net profit, CC = closing capital , D = drawings, OC = Opening capital
NP = 44700 + 330 - 39700
= 5330

topics.title: THE FINAL ACCOUNTS OF A SOLE TRADER : PROFIT OR LOSS ACCOUNT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The gross loss on manufacturing is always transferred to the
options: credit side of profit and loss account
explanation:
The gross loss on manufacturing is transferred to the debit side of the Profit and Loss Account.

The Profit and Loss Account is a T-account, which means that it has two sides: a debit side and a credit side. All revenues of the business are recorded on the credit side of the Profit and Loss Account, and all expenses of the business are recorded on the debit side.

The gross loss on manufacturing is an expense of the business, so it is recorded on the debit side of the Profit and Loss Account. This will reduce the net profit of the business.

topics.title: MANUFACTURING ACCOUNTS : INTRODUCTION AND PURPOSE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The depreciation on motor vehicle that is being used for manufacturing and administration is charged to the
options: debit side manufacturing and profit and loss account
debit side of profit and loss account only
credit side of profit and loss account only
debit side of manufacturing and balance sheet
explanation: The depreciation on a motor vehicle that is being used for manufacturing and administration is charged to the debit side of the manufacturing and profit and loss account. Depreciation is the reduction in the value of an asset over time due to wear and tear, and it is considered an expense. In accounting, expenses are always recorded on the debit side. Since the vehicle is used for both manufacturing and administration, the depreciation expense is split between the two and recorded in both accounts.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: If goods were returned to branch by the customers, the correct posting for this transaction is to debit
options: branch debtor's account an credit head office account
head office account and credit branch stock account
branch stock account and credit branch debtor's account
branch cash account and credit branch stock account
explanation: When goods are returned to the branch by the customers, the branch stock account is debited because the stock of the branch increases. At the same time, the branch debtor's account is credited because the amount the customer owes to the branch decreases. Therefore, the correct posting for this transaction is to debit the branch stock account and credit the branch debtor's account.

discount allowed account and crediting branch debtor's account
explanation: When the head office pays for branch expenses, it is recorded by debiting the branch expenses account and crediting the bank account. This is because the branch expenses account is increased (hence debited) and the bank account is decreased (hence credited).

goods sent to branch account and crediting branch debtor's account
branch stock account and crediting branch stock account
discount allowed account and crediting branch debtor's account
explanation: When goods are returned to the branch by branch customers, it is recorded in the head office books by debiting the 'goods sent to branch account' and crediting the 'branch debtor's account'. This is because the goods sent to branch account is decreased (hence debited) and the branch debtor's account is also decreased (hence credited).

explanation: The basic principle of double entry book-keeping is that there are always two entries for every transaction. One entry is known as a credit entry and the other a debit entry.

topics.title: PRINCIPLE OF DOUBLE ENTRY : MEANING AND APPLICATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
I. Deduct the smaller side figure from the bigger side figure
II. Add up both
III. Take the difference to the smaller side and add to the smaller figure

The correct procedure for balancing account is
explanation:
II. Add up both sides: This step involves adding up the debit side and credit side of the account separately to determine their totals.

I. Deduct the smaller side figure from the bigger side figure: Once the totals on both sides are equalized in step III, you deduct the smaller side figure from the bigger side figure to determine the balance of the account.

III. Take the difference to the smaller side and add it to the smaller figure: In this step, you compare the totals from step II and determine which side (debit or credit) has the smaller total. The difference between the two totals is calculated, and this difference is added to the smaller side to make the totals equal.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: In bank reconciliation process, discrepancies caused by timing arises as a result of
options: cash book, bank statement and other incidental records
explanation: In the bank reconciliation process, discrepancies caused by timing arise as a result of both the cash book and bank statement. This is because the bank may have recorded a transaction that the company has not yet recorded or vice versa. For example, the bank may have charged a fee that the company is not yet aware of, or the company may have issued a check that the bank has not yet cleared. Therefore, both the cash book and bank statement are involved in these timing discrepancies.

explanation: Cheque clearing is the process of moving a cheque from the bank in which it was deposited to the bank on which it was drawn, and the movement of the money in the opposite direction. This process is called the clearing cycle and normally results in a credit to the account at the bank of deposit, and an equivalent debit to the account at the bank on which it was drawn.

explanation: Interest charged for overdrawn balances is an expense for the business, and it reduces the balance of the bank account. Therefore, it must be added to the balance as per bank statement to arrive at the adjusted balance as per cash book.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer this question.
June 30closing stock 100 units at ₦90
July 8purchased 200 units at ₦95
July 10sold 160 units
July 17returned 80 faulty units purchased at ₦95
July 18purchased 200 units at ₦105
July 20sold 180 units
July 28purchased 200 units at ₦110
July 29sold 120 units
July 30sold 100 units


How many units were at hand as at July 30?
explanation:
The number of units at hand as at July 30 can be calculated by adding the initial stock and the purchased units, then subtracting the sold units and the returned units. Initial stock is 100 units. 200 units were purchased on July 8, 200 units on July 18, and 200 units on July 28, making a total of 600 units purchased. 160 units were sold on July 10, 180 units on July 20, 120 units on July 29, and 100 units on July 30, making a total of 560 units sold. 80 units were returned on July 17. Therefore, the number of units at hand as at July 30 is 100 (initial stock) + 600 (purchased) - 560 (sold) - 80 (returned) = 60 units.

NOTE:
(100 + 200 - 160 - 80 + 200 - 180 + 200 - 120 - 100)units
= 60 units.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A typical example of a real account is
explanation:
A real account is a general ledger account that does not close at the end of the accounting year. In other words, the balances in the real accounts are carried over to become the beginning balances of the next accounting period.
Generally, the real accounts are the balance sheet accounts. Balance sheet accounts are the asset accounts (cash, accounts receivable, buildings, etc.), liability accounts (notes payable, accounts payable, wages payable, etc.), and stockholders' equity accounts (common stock, retained earnings, etc.).
Real accounts are also referred to as permanent accounts.
On the other hand, the income statement accounts (revenues, expenses, gains, losses) are known as temporary accounts because their balances are not carried over to the next accounting period. The income statement accounts begin each new accounting year with zero balances.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : ACCOUNTING EQUATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The control account can be used in
options: keeping records of all direct deposits in the bank account
recording all the business transactions of the enterprise
monitoring the efficiency of book keeping by accountants
explanation:
A control account is a general ledger account that summarizes the balances or transactions in a subsidiary ledger. It is used to maintain control and monitor the accuracy of the subsidiary ledger. The subsidiary ledger contains detailed information about individual accounts, such as accounts receivable or accounts payable, while the control account provides a summarized view of those accounts.

By using a control account, all the business transactions of the enterprise can be recorded and tracked. The control account acts as a summary or overview of the transactions recorded in the subsidiary ledger. It helps ensure that the subsidiary ledger is accurately maintained and that the balances in the control account align with the total of the subsidiary ledger.

q:
