Incomplete Record (Extract)
₦ ₦
Sales 10600
Rent 200

Depreciation of vehicle(cost ₦5000)10%
Gross profit margin is 20%

What is the cost of goods sold?
explanation:
The cost of goods sold is calculated as Sales - Gross Profit.

Given that the gross profit margin is 20%, the gross profit is 20% of the total sales, which is 20% of ₦10600

= ₦2120.

Therefore, the cost of goods sold is:
= ₦10600 - ₦2120
= ₦8480.

topics.title: SINGLE ENTRY AND INCOMPLETE RECORDS : COMPUTATION OF PROFIT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer this question.
Incomplete Record (Extract)
₦ ₦
Sales 10600
Rent 200

Depreciation of vehicle(cost ₦5000)10%
Gross profit margin is 20%
Calculate the net profit
explanation:
The net profit is calculated by subtracting the total expenses from the gross profit. The total expenses include rent and depreciation of the vehicle.

The depreciation of the vehicle is 10% of the cost of the vehicle, which is 10% of ₦5000
= ₦500.

Therefore, the total expenses are ₦200 (rent) + ₦500 (depreciation)
= ₦700.

Given that the gross profit margin is 20%, the gross profit is 20% of the total sales, which is 20% of ₦10600

= ₦2120.

Therefore, the net profit is ₦2120 - ₦700
= ₦1420.

q: The correct entries to record goods transferred to branch from head office is to debit
options: branch supplies account and credit branch stock account
branch stock account and credit goods sent to branch account
branch stock account and credit purchase account
goods sent to branch account and credit branch stock account
explanation: The correct entries to record goods transferred to branch from head office is to debit branch stock account and credit goods sent to branch account. This is because when goods are transferred from the head office to a branch, the branch's stock account (an asset account) is increased (debited), and the goods sent to branch account (a contra asset account) is decreased (credited). This reflects the fact that the branch now has more stock, and the head office has less.

the profit and loss sharing ratio of the partners
explanation: Since the purpose of any business is to make a profit, the major point of agreement carried by the partnership deed has to be the profit and loss sharing ratio of the partners.

topics.title: PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : INTRODUCTION AND TYPES OF PARTNERS( PARTNERSHIP)

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:

Keme and Kemi are in a partnership sharing profit and losses in the ratio 3:2.

- Kemi ₦10 000

- Kemi ₦3 000
Profit # 6 000
Interest on capital 5%
Interest on drawings 10%


The interest on kemi's capital is
explanation:
The interest on Kemi's capital is calculated by multiplying the capital by the interest rate.

In this case, Kemi's capital is ₦10,000 and the interest rate is 5%.

Therefore, the interest on Kemi's capital is ₦10,000 x 5/100
= ₦500.

topics.title: PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : ACCOUNTING ENTRIES AT THE END OF THE FINANCIAL YEAR

q:

- Kemi ₦3 000

Interest on drawings 10%

The interest on kemi's drawing is calculated as
explanation:
The interest on Kemi's drawing is calculated by multiplying the amount of her drawing by the interest rate on drawings.

In this case, Kemi's drawing is ₦3,000 and the interest rate on drawings is 10%.

Therefore, the interest on Kemi's drawing is ₦3,000 x10%
= ₦300.

topics.title: PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : ACCOUNTING ENTRIES AT THE END OF THE FINANCIAL YEAR

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following drawing can be used on admission of a new partnership?
explanation: Revaluation account can be used. The revaluation account records the positive or negative holding gains accruing during the accounting period to the owners of financial and non-financial assets and liabilities. The actual value of the assets and liabilities may be different from their book value as shown by the balance sheet. So Revaluation account is prepared at the time of admission of a new partner to record any increase/decrease in the value of assets and liabilities.

topics.title: GOODWILL ACCOUNTS : TREATMENT OF GOODWILL ON ADMISSION OF PARTNERS AND CHANGES IN RATIO

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Goodwill is determined using
explanation:
The circumstances giving rise to the ascertainment of goodwill are:
- Admission of a new partner
- Change in profit sharing ratio
- Death or retirement of a partner
- The business has been purchased
- Dissolution of a business

topics.title: GOODWILL ACCOUNTS : INTRODUCTION AND REASONS FOR GOODWILL

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The founders of a company are
explanation: Any person who does an act with reference to the formation of a company or in aid of its organization whether he has the intention to be partners or not is a promoter.

topics.title: COMPANY ACCOUNT : INTRODUCTION , TYPES AND KINDS OF COMPANIES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The fund in which all government receipts are paid is
explanation: Consolidated revenue fund is a central government purse in which all government receipts are paid into and expenditure allocated from this account and is usually estimated in the constitution.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : SOURCES AND CLASSIFICATION OF GOVERNMENT REVENUE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following accounts for the highest revenue to Nigeria?
explanation: Petroleum resources account for the highest revenue to Nigeria. Nigeria is one of the largest oil producers in the world and the oil sector contributes the largest percentage of government revenue.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : SOURCES AND CLASSIFICATION OF GOVERNMENT REVENUE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: if Aboki Holdings Limited issued 120 000 ordinary share of ₦2.00 each at market value of 5.50k each, the share premium would be
explanation:
The share premium is calculated as the difference between the market value of the share and the nominal value of the share, multiplied by the number of shares issued.

In this case, the market value is ₦5.50, the nominal value is ₦2.00, and the number of shares issued is 120,000.

Therefore, the share premium is (₦5.50 - ₦2.00) x 120,000
= ₦420,000.

topics.title: ISSUES OF SHARES : MEANING AND CLASSES OF SHARES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following is a signatory to federal government account?
explanation: The Accountant-General is a signatory to the federal government account. The Accountant-General is responsible for the issue and control of public funds, and therefore, is a signatory to the federal government account.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : INTRODUCTION AND USERS OF GOVERNMENT ACCOUNTING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The chief accounting officer of a local government is the
explanation: The chief accounting officer of a local government is typically the treasurer. The treasurer is responsible for managing the financial affairs of the local government, including budgeting, financial reporting, and financial control. The chairman is the head of the local government, but they are not typically involved in the day-to-day financial management. The director of personnel is responsible for human resources, not financial management. The auditor is responsible for auditing the financial statements, not managing the financial affairs of the local government.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : INTRODUCTION AND USERS OF GOVERNMENT ACCOUNTING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following signs generally warrant for the release of money from the consolidated revenue fund?
explanation: Consolidated revenue fund is a central government purse in which all government receipts are paid into and expenditure allocated from this account and is usually estimated in the constitution. The Minister of Finance generally prepares the warrant for the release of such funds, usually a budget which has to be presented before the legislature.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : SOURCES AND CLASSIFICATION OF GOVERNMENT REVENUE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: What subject are you doing?
explanation: The subject in question is 'Accounts' as indicated in the id of the question. Therefore, the correct answer is 'Accounts'.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : MEANING AND OBJECTIVES OF ACCOUNTING AND BOOKKEEPING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The base upon which assets of an organization is valued is the
explanation:
Historical cost is the value of a resource given up or a liability incurred to acquire an asset/service at the time when the resource was given up or the liability incurred.
In subsequent periods when there is appreciation, the value is not recognized as an increase in assets value except where allowed or required by accounting standards.

topics.title: ACCOUNTING RATIO ( 1) : INTRODUCTION AND USES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: An accountant must not only be interested in record keeping but also in
explanation: The duties of an accountant revolve around financial record keeping, decision making, and other aspects that can ensure that the company thrives well in the market. An accountant must prepare and analyze the financial records of a company while managing data that can be analyzed and used for consultation.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : USERS OF ACCOUNTING & ROLES OF ACCOUNTING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The correct posting in the double entry system of account when there is an increase in assets, expenses, capital or liability is to debit
explanation: In the double entry system of accounting, an increase in assets or expenses is recorded as a debit, while an increase in capital or liabilities is recorded as a credit. Therefore, the correct posting when there is an increase in assets, expenses, capital or liability is to debit assets and credit capital. This is based on the fundamental principle of double entry bookkeeping where every financial transaction affects at least two accounts and the total debits must equal total credits.

topics.title: PRINCIPLE OF DOUBLE ENTRY : MEANING AND APPLICATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The capital of a sole trader can be increased by
explanation: The capital of a sole trader can be increased by crediting the capital account. In accounting, a credit to the capital account means an increase in the owner's equity. This is based on the fundamental accounting equation where Assets = Liabilities + Owner's Equity. When the owner injects more capital into the business, the owner's equity increases, hence the capital account is credited.

topics.title: THE FINAL ACCOUNTS OF A SOLE TRADER : BALANCE SHEET

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Akirika bought a motor van for his business by cash. The entries are to debit
explanation: When Akirika bought a motor van for his business by cash, the entries are to debit motor van and credit cash. This is because in accounting, a debit entry signifies an increase in asset (in this case, the motor van) while a credit entry signifies a decrease in asset (in this case, cash). Therefore, the motor van account is debited to show an increase in business assets and the cash account is credited to show a decrease in cash assets.

topics.title: CASH BOOK : MEANING AND TYPES OF CASH BOOK

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer this question.
Ibrahim, a microbusiness operator, sold 10 bags of sugar to Jide at total cost of ₦12,000.

In the stock of Ibrahim, the entry to record the transactions would be to debit
explanation: In accounting, when a sale is made, the seller debits the receivable account and credits the sales account. This is in accordance with the double-entry bookkeeping system, which states that for every financial transaction, corresponding debit and credit entries must be made.

topics.title: SUBSIDIARY BOOKS : MEANING AND IMPORTANCE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer this question.
Ibrahim, a microbusiness operator, sold 10 bags of sugar to Jide at total cost of ₦12,000.
The record in Jide's book would be to debit
explanation: From the buyer's perspective, the purchase of goods is an expense, so his purchases account should be debited. At the same time, he owes the seller for the goods, so the seller's account should be credited. This is also in accordance with the double-entry bookkeeping system.

topics.title: PRINCIPLE OF DOUBLE ENTRY : CASH AND CREDIT TRANSACTIONS

q:

explanation:

The bank balance will be a ₦(30,800 - 23,850)
= ₦6,950 debit.

topics.title: THE FINAL ACCOUNTS OF A SOLE TRADER : BALANCE SHEET

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: In a three-column cash book, dishonoured cheques will be shown on the
explanation: The dishonour of cheques is recorded on the payment side or credit side of the bank column of cash book. When a cheque is dishonoured, the Bank A/c is credited and, thus, it is credited in the bank column of the cash book.

topics.title: CASH BOOK : THREE COLUMN CASH BOOK

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Date Particulars Folio Details Total
1/6/02 2 bags of maize SL 1 800 1 800
12/6/02 3 bags of salt
less 5% discount 7 000 7 000
8 800

The 5% discount shown above indicates
explanation: The 5% discount shown in the question is a trade discount. A trade discount is a reduction in the listed price of an item when it's sold for resale. It's used to incentivize bulk purchasing. In this case, the discount is given on the purchase of goods, hence it's a trade discount. The other options are incorrect: a cash discount is a reduction in the amount to be paid if the payment is made within a certain period, discount allowed is a reduction in the amount of money received from a debtor, while discount received is a reduction in the amount of money paid to a creditor.

topics.title: SUBSIDIARY BOOKS : SOURCE DOCUMENT

explanation: ATM is a machine that dispenses cash or performs other banking services when an account holder inserts a bank card. For one to withdraw money from the machine, you are required to own an ATM card

does not reside in the bank's country
explanation: If the transferor's bank account does not have sufficient funds to cover the cash transfer, the transfer may be declined. In this case, the transferor needs to have enough money in their account to initiate the transfer.

topics.title: BANK RECONCILIATION STATEMENT : INTRODUCTION AND REASONS FOR DISAGREENMENT BETWEEN CASH BOOK & BANK STATEMENT

q:

direct debit ₦ 500

The adjusted cash book is
explanation:
The adjusted cash book is calculated by adding the cash, discount cheque, and direct credit, then subtracting the direct debit.
So, ₦5 000 (Cash) + ₦1 000 (Discount cheque) + ₦800 (Direct credit) - ₦500 (Direct debit)
= ₦6 300.

topics.title: BANK RECONCILIATION STATEMENT : PREPARATION OF BANK RECONCILIATION STATEMENT

q:

Cost of goods

rent expenses 35000

explanation:
Gross profit is calculated by subtracting the cost of goods sold from the net sales.

In this case, the gross profit is ₦370,000 (net sales) - ₦230,000 (cost of goods sold)

topics.title: THE FINAL ACCOUNTS OF A SOLE TRADER : TRADING ACTIVITIES

Cost of goods

explanation:
Net profit is calculated by subtracting the total expenses from the gross profit.

In this case, the net profit is ₦140,000 (gross profit) - ₦35,000 (total expenses)
= ₦105,000.

topics.title: THE FINAL ACCOUNTS OF A SOLE TRADER : PROFIT OR LOSS ACCOUNT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer this question.
At the end of a financial period, the trading, profit and loss account of a sole trader shows a profit of ₦180,000. It is however discovered that revenue of ₦15,000 is recorded as expenses while expenses of ₦4,000 is recorded as revenue.

Determine the adjusted net profit
explanation:
The adjusted net profit is ₦180,000 + (₦15,000 - ₦4,000) + (₦15,000 - ₦4,000)
= ₦202 000.

topics.title: CORRECTION OF ERRORS : MEANING AND PROCEDURES

The revenue to be added as an adjustment is
explanation:
The revenue to be added as an adjustment is ₦15 000 + ₦15 000(to show adjustment)
= ₦30 000.

topics.title: CORRECTION OF ERRORS : MEANING AND PROCEDURES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following is an item of special assets?
explanation: Trademark is a special asset. It can also be called an intangible asset. A trademark is any word, name, symbol, or design, or any combination thereof, used in commerce to identify and distinguish the goods of one manufacturer or seller from those of another and to indicate the source of the goods.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer this question.
1/5/07 Purchased 100 bags of milk at ₦10.00k each
3/5/07 Purchase 60 bags of milk at ₦11.50k each
8/5/07 Issued 85 bags of milk
15/5/07 Purchased 180 bags of milk at #12.80k each
22/5/07 Issued 145 bags of milk

Using simple average method, calculate the value of stock after 22/5/07
explanation:
Total purchases = 100 + 60 + 180
= 340

Total issue = 85 + 145
= 230

Stock left will be 340 - 230
= 110

The value will be 110 × ₦12.80
= ₦1,408

topics.title: STOCK VALUATION : MEANING OF STOCK VALUATION AND METHODS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The LIFO method has an advantage over FIFO in that stocks are valued at
explanation:
The advantages of LIFO are:
- It is easy to understand and simple to apply
- It is an actual cost method
- value of issues are close to current economic value
- Stock valuation is usually conservative especially when prices are rising.

topics.title: STOCK VALUATION : MEANING OF STOCK VALUATION AND METHODS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: In sales ledger control account, returns inwards is usually
explanation: Returns inwards are goods returned to the selling entity by the customer, such as for warranty claims or outright returns of goods for a credit. For the customer, this results in the following accounting transaction. Returns inwards is usually credited and sales returns debited.

topics.title: CONTROL ACCOUNTS : SALES LEDGER CONTROL ACCOUNT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Purchase ledger control account can also be referred to as
explanation: The purchase ledger control account can also be referred to as the creditors ledger. This is because the purchase ledger control account is a summary account that records the total amount owed to suppliers for goods purchased on credit. These suppliers are the business's creditors, hence the term 'creditors ledger'.

topics.title: CONTROL ACCOUNTS : PURCHASES LEDGER CONTROL ACCOUNT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following is a debit item in the sales ledger control account?
explanation: Dishonoured cheques (bill) is a debit item on the sales ledger control account. Others include: Carriage outwards, Debit note issued, Discounts disallowed etc.

topics.title: CONTROL ACCOUNTS : SALES LEDGER CONTROL ACCOUNT

explanation:
Sales will be receipt from customers + amount paid by debtors
= ₦ 16,400 + ₦(7,600 - 6,500)
= ₦(16,400 + 1,100)
= ₦17 500

explanation:
On the debit side, we have fixtures + debtors + stock + goodwill = ₦(30,000 + 7,000 + 8,000 + 10,000)
= ₦55,000

On the credit side, we have creditors = ₦3,000

= ₦(55,000 - 3,000)
= ₦52,000.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : ACCOUNTING EQUATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: In manufacturing accounts,finance expenses are charged to the
explanation: In manufacturing accounts, finance expenses are charged to the profit and loss account. This is because finance expenses are considered as operating expenses, which are deducted from the gross profit to arrive at the net profit or loss. The profit and loss account is used to record all the revenues and expenses of a business during a specific period of time.

topics.title: MANUFACTURING ACCOUNTS : INTRODUCTION AND PURPOSE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer this question.
Total production cost 360 000
Factory overhead cost 56 000
Selling price per unit 120

Production volume 4 000 units

What is the total manufacturing profit?
explanation:
Gross sales = 4,000 × 120
= ₦480,000

total manufacturing profit = gross sales - total production cost
= ₦480,000 - ₦360,000
= ₦120,000.

topics.title: MANUFACTURING ACCOUNTS : INTRODUCTION AND PURPOSE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer this question.
Total production cost 360 000
Factory overhead cost 56 000
Selling price per unit 120

Production volume 4 000 units
Determine the value of prime cost.
explanation:
The prime cost is the total production cost minus the factory overhead cost.
Therefore, the prime cost is 360,000 - 56,000
= 304,000.

topics.title: MANUFACTURING ACCOUNTS : INTRODUCTION AND PURPOSE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The excess of revenue over expenditure is charged to
explanation: The excess of revenue over expenditure is charged to the accumulation fund. The accumulation fund is a reserve account in which the surplus of income over expenditure is accumulated. It is used to finance future growth and expansion.

topics.title: ACCOUNTS OF NON PROFIT MAKING ORGANISATION : INCOME AND EXPENDITURE ACCOUNTS

explanation:

Since the debit side exceeds the credit side we have: 350,000 - 135,000

topics.title: ACCOUNTS OF NON PROFIT MAKING ORGANISATION : RECEIPTS AND PAYMENTS ACCOUNTS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The main advantage of a departmental account is that its
explanation: A departmental account is a way of keeping financial records for different departments within a business. The main advantage of this is that it allows for the computation of gross profit for each department. Gross profit is the profit a company makes after deducting the costs associated with making and selling its products, or the costs associated with providing its services. This helps in identifying which department is performing well and which is not, thereby aiding in decision making.

topics.title: DEPARTMENTAL ACCOUNT : INTRODUCTION AND ADVANTAGES OF DEPARTMENTAL ACCOUNT

q:

explanation:

= ₦(3,000 - 800)
= ₦2,200

topics.title: DEPARTMENTAL ACCOUNT : PRESENTATION OF FINAL ACCOUNTS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: In the head office ledger, the value of goods sent to the branch are
options: debited to the head office current account
credited to the head office current account
explanation: In the head office ledger, the value of goods sent to the branch are debited to the branch current account. This is because when the head office sends goods to the branch, it is essentially transferring assets to the branch. In accounting, when assets are increased, they are debited. Therefore, the branch account (which is an asset to the head office) is debited.

topics.title: BRANCH ACCOUNTING : BRANCH ADJUSTMENT METHOD

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following methods of invoicing goods to branches facilitate easy checks on the activities of branches?
explanation: The method of invoicing goods to branches that facilitates easy checks on the activities of branches is the invoice price method. This is because the invoice price includes both the cost of the goods and the profit margin. This allows the head office to easily track both the cost and the profit margin of the goods sold by the branch, thereby facilitating easy checks on the branch's activities.

topics.title: BRANCH ACCOUNTING : PRICING METHODS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:

The partners capital are: Modibbo ₦60 000 and Jakata ₦90 000. The partners share profit and loss in the ratio of their capital contributions. The net profit for the year is ₦12 000

Find Modibbo's share of the profit.
explanation:
ratio of their capital = 60000/90000
= 2/3

Total ratio = 2+3 = 5
Modibo's share of profit = 2/5 x 12000
= 4800

explanation:
Jakata's profit sharing ratio is 3:5 which is acsertain from their capital
i.e 60,000/90,000 = 2/3
= 2:3

Therefore, total ratio = 2 + 3
= 5

topics.title: PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : ACCOUNTING ENTRIES AT THE END OF THE FINANCIAL YEAR

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: In what ways can a goodwill be written off in partnership business?
options: using the partner's profit and loss sharing ratio
By neglecting the ratio of partners capital contribution
by sharing it unequally among the partners where no agreement exists
by sharing it among the active partners' only
explanation:
Raise the goodwill at its value by crediting all the partners' capital accounts (including that of the retired/ deceased partners) and then
Written off by debiting the remaining partners in their new profit sharing ratio and crediting the goodwill account with its full value.

topics.title: GOODWILL ACCOUNTS : TREATMENT OF GOODWILL ON ADMISSION OF PARTNERS AND CHANGES IN RATIO

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Ngozi and Musa with a capital of ₦30 000 each decide to admit Mary into the partnership business with a capital of ₦20 000 and goodwill ₦15 000. If the profits and losses are to be shared equally, the journal entries to record goodwill are
options: debit goodwill ₦15 000, cash ₦20 000 and credit Mary's capital ₦35 000
credit goodwill ₦15 000, cash ₦20000 and debit mary's capital ₦35 000
debit goodwill ₦15 000, credit cash ₦20 000 and credit Mary's capital ₦20 000
debit old partners capital ₦15 000, credit cash ₦20 000 and Mary's capital ₦35 000
explanation:
When Mary is admitted into the partnership, her capital contribution and goodwill are recorded as debits to the partnership's accounts, as they represent increases in the partnership's assets. The corresponding credit is made to Mary's capital account, reflecting her claim on the partnership's assets.

Therefore, the correct journal entry is to debit goodwill for ₦15 000, debit cash for ₦20 000, and credit Mary's capital for ₦35 000.

topics.title: GOODWILL ACCOUNTS : TREATMENT OF GOODWILL ON ADMISSION OF PARTNERS AND CHANGES IN RATIO

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: In converting a partnership into a limited liability company, the necessary accounts to be opened in the books of the company are
options: business purchase account and ordinary share capital account
business purchase account, vendor account and ordinary share capital account
ordinary share capital account, vendor account and unpaid share capital account
explanation: When a partnership is converted into a limited liability company, the necessary accounts to be opened in the books of the company are business purchase account, vendor account and ordinary share capital account. The business purchase account is used to record the purchase of the business. The vendor account is used to record the amount owed to the vendor for the purchase of the business. The ordinary share capital account is used to record the capital contributed by the shareholders of the company.

topics.title: PURCHASES OF BUSINESS : ENTRIES IN THE BOOK OF THE PURCHASER

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer this question.
Purchases ₦44880
Sales ₦85850
Trade creditors₦12250
Trade debtors ₦ 24000
Accrued expenses ₦350
Prepaid expenses ₦700
Stock 1/1/2006 ₦25120
Stock 31/12/2006 ₦27840


Calculate the acid test ratio
explanation:
The Acid-test or quick ratio = Current asset - Closing stock/ Current liabilties

Current assets = 24000+ 700+ 27840 = 52540
Current liabilities = 12250+ 350 = 12600

Therefore Acid ratio = 52540 - 27840/12600
24700/12600
= 1.96:1

topics.title: ACCOUNTING RATIO (2) : PROFITABILITY, LIQUIDITY AND INVESTMENT RATIO

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer this question.
Purchases ₦44880
Sales ₦85850
Trade creditors₦12250
Trade debtors ₦ 24000
Accrued expenses ₦350
Prepaid expenses ₦700
Stock 1/1/2006 ₦25120
Stock 31/12/2006 ₦27840

Determine the number of times stock was turned over during the period to the nearest figure
explanation:
Stock turnover = Cost of goods sold /Average stock

Cost of goods sold = Opening stock +purchases - Closing stock
= 25120+44880 - 27840
= 42160

Average stock = Opening stock + Closing stock / 2
= 25120 + 27840 / 2
= 52960 / 2
= 26480
Therefore, Stock turnover = 42160 / 26480
= 1.59
≏ 2

explanation: The federation account is a central pool of revenue from which all tiers of government in a federation draw funds. explanation: The power to appoint the Auditor General of the federation is vested in the President of the country. explanation: When the debit side total of an account exceeds the credit side total, it means that the account has a debit balance. In accounting, a debit balance is the ending balance in an account that has more debits than credits. This is traditionally the normal balance for asset accounts, expense accounts, and the owner's drawing account.

topics.title: PRINCIPLE OF DOUBLE ENTRY : CASH AND CREDIT TRANSACTIONS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer this question
July 1 - Started business with ₦ 10,500
July 31 - Paid Agromachinex owing to them ₦6,000
The double entry for July 31 would be
explanation: The double entry system of accounting means that every transaction will involve two accounts. In this case, the business is paying a debt it owes to Agromachinex. This means cash (an asset) is decreasing, which is a credit, and the liability (amount owed to Agromachinex) is also decreasing, which is a debit. Therefore, the correct double entry is to debit Agromachinex and credit cash.

topics.title: CASH BOOK : MEANING AND TYPES OF CASH BOOK

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Alaka who owed Saka ₦15,000, settled his debt after deducting cash discount of 10%. To record the discount in the book of Saka, debit
options: discount received account and credit Alaka's account
Alaka's account and credit discount received account
Saka's account and credit discount received account
discount allowed account and credit Alaka's account
explanation: When Alaka settles his debt after deducting a cash discount, Saka will record the discount as 'discount received'. This is because from Saka's perspective, the discount is an income or gain. Therefore, Saka will debit the 'discount received account' (increasing his income) and credit Alaka's account (decreasing his debtor's balance). This follows the rule of 'Debit the receiver, Credit the giver' in accounting.

topics.title: RESERVES AND PROVISIONS : PROVISION FOR DISCOUNTS ALLOWED AND RECEIVED

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
I. The amount of the imprest is the same from one organization to another

II. At the end of a fixed period, the petty cashier is reimbursed with the amount spend in that period
III. At the end of a period, the petty cashier is reimbursed with the amount spend in that period
IV. The system is a method by which a measure of control is kept on petty cash expenses.
Which of the above is correct about the imprest system?
explanation: Imprest system is a system for recording petty cash transactions, which are paid out of cash float. The system is set up by giving a specified sum known as cash float to the cashier to cover petty expenses during the period. The petty cashier will pay out money from the float for all authorized vouchers. At frequent intervals, he will be re-imbursed for the amount spent thus bringing it to the original amount.

explanation:

= ₦5,200.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : ACCOUNTING EQUATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The estimate profit or loss for a period is calculated by
options: closing capital less opening capital add drawings
opening capital less closing capital add drawings
opening capital less drawings add closing capital
opening capital add closing capital add drawings
explanation:
The estimated profit or loss for a period is calculated by subtracting the opening capital from the closing capital and adding any drawings. This is because the closing capital represents the total value of the business at the end of the period, while the opening capital represents the total value at the beginning. Drawings are amounts taken out of the business by the owner, which reduce the capital.

Therefore, the formula is: Profit or Loss = Closing Capital - Opening Capital + Drawings.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Use the information below to answer this question.


Department K Y
₦ ₦
Opening stock 2 500 800
Purchases 120 000 100 000
Sales 180 000 200 000
Salaries 8 000 30 000
Closing stock 3 000 1 500

Rate expenses are of ₦1,500 are apportioned in the ratio 1:2
What is the net profit of Y?
explanation:
Net profit = Gross profit - Expenses
Total ratio = 1+2 = 3

Gross profit = Sales - Cost of goods(Opening stock + Purchases - Closing stock ) - salaries - expenses
= 200,000 - [800 + 100,000 - 1500)
GP = 100,700

Expenses = Salaries + Rate ( 2/3 x 1500)
= 30,000 + 1000 = 31,000
NP = 100,700 - 31,000
= 69,700

A capital reserve is an account in the equity section of the balance sheet that can be used for contingencies or to offset capital losses. It is derived from the accumulated capital surplus of a company, created out of capital profit.

Capital Reserve is a type of profit that is earned on capital transactions - an example would be the profit arising from the forfeiture of shares, share premium, Capital Redemption Reserve or Debenture Redemption Reserve.

explanation: Supplementary budget is the additional estimate as a result of short falls in the main budget. It must be given approval by the legislature.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : MEANING AND OBJECTIVES OF ACCOUNTING AND BOOKKEEPING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: One of the differences between book keeping and account is that the former
options: records data while the latter interprets it
is regarded as the language of the business while the latter ascertains its strength
interprets data while the latter records it
summaries information while the latter communicates it
explanation: In the simplest of terms, bookkeeping is responsible for the recording of financial transactions whereas accounting is responsible for interpreting, classifying, analyzing, reporting, and summarizing the financial data. Bookkeeping and accounting may appear to be the same profession to an untrained eye.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : ACCOUNTING CONCEPTS AND CONVENTIONS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The concept which states that revenue should be recognized at the point when the sale is deemed to have been made is
explanation: This is the Realization Concept. The realization principle is the concept that revenue can only be recognized once the underlying goods or services associated with the revenue have been delivered or rendered, respectively. Thus, revenue can only be recognized after it has been earned.

topics.title: SUSPENSE ACCOUNT : MEANING AND USES OF SUSPENSE ACCOUNT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A cheque of ₦5,000 paid to Suleiman had been correctly entered in the cash book but had not been entered in Suleiman's account. To correct this error, debit Suleiman's account and credit
explanation:
The error described in the question is an omission in Suleiman's account. The correct entry should have been a debit to Suleiman's account (increasing his liability) and a credit to the bank account (decreasing the asset).

Therefore, to correct this error, Suleiman's account should be debited and the bank account should be credited.

topics.title: CASH BOOK : MEANING AND TYPES OF CASH BOOK

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Aduke Motors bought three Toyota Hilux vans on cash at the cost of ₦6,000,000. On debiting the vehicle account, the corresponding credit for the purchase will appear in the
explanation: When Aduke Motors bought three Toyota Hilux vans on cash, the corresponding credit for the purchase will appear in the cash book. This is because the cash book is a record of all cash transactions, including cash receipts and cash payments. In this case, the cash payment for the vans is recorded in the cash book.

topics.title: TRIAL BALANCE : ERRORS NOT DISCLOSED BY TRIAL BALANCE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following affects the accuracy and authenticity of the trial balance?
explanation: Transposition errors are errors where figures (digits) are written in the wrong order in either a credit or a debit entry. This would create an imbalance between credits and debts, and so the error would be indicated by extracting a trial balance.

topics.title: THE LEDGER : MEANING AND DIVISION OF LEDGER

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A ledger is classified into
explanation: Ledger is classified into : Personal ledger( sales and purchase ledger), General ledger and Private ledger.

topics.title: THE LEDGER : MEANING AND DIVISION OF LEDGER

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The book of account in which information from the source documents are recorded consist of
explanation: The book of account in which information from the source documents are recorded consists of the ledger and subsidiary books. The ledger is the principal book of accounts where all types of accounts (personal, real, and nominal) are maintained. Subsidiary books are books of original entry (also known as special journals) where similar transactions are recorded. Examples of subsidiary books include the cash book, purchases book, sales book, purchases return book, sales return book, bills receivable book, bills payable book, and journal proper.

topics.title: SUBSIDIARY BOOKS : MEANING AND IMPORTANCE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The accounting entries to record a cheque issued by a business is to
options: debit cash book and credit suspense account
credit cash book and debit suspense account
explanation: When a business issues a cheque, it decreases its cash balance (credit) and the amount is debited to the drawer's account. The cash book is credited because the cash balance is decreasing, and the drawer's account is debited to show the increase in the amount owed to the drawer (the person or entity who will receive the funds from the cheque).

options: ensures a proper accountability for every expenditure
trains the young accountants in the preparation for greater responsibility
relieves the chief cashier of the numerous petty cash payments
serves as an analysis column for very expenses
explanation: The imprest system is a financial accounting system that is used to maintain the petty cash fund. The main advantage of this system is that it ensures that every expenditure is accounted for, which helps in maintaining financial control and preventing fraud.

topics.title: PETTY CASH BOOK : MEANING AND IMPORTANCE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts

explanation:
Transactions are recorded in both debit and credit side.
The credit side is wider than debit side and also the credit side is the analysis columns while the debit side is the receipt side.

topics.title: PETTY CASH BOOK : MEANING AND IMPORTANCE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A major way by which the headquarters guard against fraud in branches on cash remittance is through the introduction of
explanation: The imprest system is a financial accounting system that maintains a fixed amount as reserve cash and is restored to the fixed amount periodically. This system is often used to manage petty cash. It helps to prevent fraud by ensuring that only a certain amount of cash is available at any given time, and that all expenditures are tracked and accounted for.

topics.title: BRANCH ACCOUNTING : BRANCH ADJUSTMENT METHOD

q:
I. Cash sales
II. Cash purchased
III. Cash discount allowed
IV. Cash payment

Which of the following are recorded on the debit side of the cash book?
explanation: In a cash book, all cash receipts are recorded on the debit side and all cash payments are recorded on the credit side. Therefore, cash sales (I) and cash receipts (V) are recorded on the debit side of the cash book.

explanation:
A standing order is an instruction a bank account holder gives to his or her bank to pay a set amount at regular intervals to another's account. The bank will make the payment and debit the bank statement but no entry in the Cash book.

Compare the Dr of the bank statement with Cr of cash book. Any item on the Dr of bank statement not in the Cr side of cash book can be bank charges, dishonoured cheques, standing order.

q: If goods were bought from Tanko at a cost price of ₦9 000 with a cash discount of 5%, how much will be paid assuming prompt payment was made?
explanation:
If goods were bought from Tanko at a cost price of ₦9 000 with a cash discount of 5%, the amount to be paid assuming prompt payment was made would be ₦8 550.

This is calculated by subtracting the discount (5% of ₦9 000 = ₦450) from the cost price (₦9 000 - ₦450 = ₦8 550).

explanation: The Sales ledger control account or Trade Debtors control account is part of the balance sheet and shows at any given time how much customers owe a company. Its value gives the sum of the total debtors' account.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Subscription in arrears is treated in the balance sheet of a club as
explanation: Subscription in arrears refers to the amount of subscription that is due but has not yet been received by the club. It is treated as a current asset in the balance sheet of a club because it is expected to be received within a short period of time, typically within the financial year. It is not a liability because it is not an amount the club owes, but rather an amount the club is owed. It is also not a fixed or intangible asset because it does not have a physical presence and is not held for a long period of time.

explanation: In departmental accounting, when there is no specific basis for apportionment of expenses, the expenses are apportioned on an equal basis among the departments. This is because it is assumed that all departments are equally responsible for the incurred expenses.

explanation: The profit of a branch is usually credited to the head office current account. This is because the head office current account is used to record all transactions between the head office and its branches, including the branch's profits.

explanation: When a partnership is converted into a limited company, the current account balances of the partners are transferred to the share capital account. This is because the partners' capital in the partnership becomes the share capital in the limited company.

explanation: The memorandum of association is the main document by which the registration of a company is achieved and the provisions contained within the memorandum will prevail over any conflicting provisions contained in the articles of association. q: ₦800 000 worth of ordinary shares of 50K were issued at ₦1 each, payable in full on application. The entry in the cash book would be to
explanation:
When shares are issued at a price and payable in full on application, the company receives cash, which is an asset. Therefore, the cash account (which is an asset account) is debited (since assets increase with a debit).

In this case, ₦800 000 worth of ordinary shares of 50K were issued at ₦1 each, so the cash book would be debited ₦800 000.

explanation:
The Institute of Chartered Accountants of Nigeria (ICAN) was established by Act of Parliament No. 15 of 1965 to
-Determine what standards of knowledge and skill are to be attained by persons seeking to become members of the accountancy profession and to raise those standards from time to time as circumstances may permit;
-Secure in accordance with the provision of the Act, the establishment and maintenance of the registers of fellows, associates and registered accountants entitled to practise as accountants and auditors and to publish from time to time a list of those persons;

q: An advantage of the use of the voucher system is that it
options: reduces the number of cheques that will be written during any given period
provides a highly flexible system for handling unusual transaction
provides a comprehensive record of business done with particular suppliers
ensures that every expenditure is reviewed and verified before payment is made
explanation: A voucher system is an accounting system that uses vouchers as a means of transferring or disbursing cash. An advantage of the use of the voucher system is that it ensures that every expenditure is reviewed and verified before payment is made. This is because, in a voucher system, an invoice is not paid unless it has been approved at several points. This reduces the risk of errors, fraud, and misuse of company funds.

overstate the income for the two-year period

Overstated net income in the following year due to the understated beginning inventory, which results in understated COGS for the following year.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A powerful instrument of control over both the sales ledger and the clerk whose duty is to post the ledgers the
explanation: A powerful instrument of control over both the sales ledger and the clerk whose duty is to post the ledgers is the Total debtors account. This is because the total debtors account, also known as the sales ledger control account, is a summary account that reflects the total amount receivable from customers for goods sold on credit. It is used to cross-check the accuracy of entries made in the sales ledger and to prevent fraud or errors by the clerk responsible for posting the ledgers.

q: The contribution margin on a job is the
options: Excess of sales revenue over variable costs
explanation: Contribution Margin (CM) is the revenue left over after paying all the variable costs – both direct and indirect. Therefore, the contribution margin on a job is the excess of sales revenue over available costs.

topics.title: MANUFACTURING ACCOUNTS : INTRODUCTION AND PURPOSE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The objective of allocation of all costs to products is to
options: avoid unallocated overhead and compute total product cost
compute the contribution of the product to the final profit
explanation: The objective of allocating all costs to products is to avoid unallocated overhead and compute total product cost. This is because cost allocation helps in determining the total cost of producing a product, which includes both direct and indirect costs (overhead). By allocating all costs, a company can accurately determine the total cost of a product, which can then be used to set selling prices, identify inefficiencies, and make informed decisions about production levels.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: A non-profit-making organization differs from a profit-making one in that
options: proceeds from sales of shares form part of its income
annual subscriptions and levies form parts of its income.
explanation: A non-profit-making organization differs from a profit-making one in that all its income is committed. This means that all the income that a non-profit organization generates is used to further its goals and objectives, rather than being distributed to shareholders or owners as in the case of a profit-making organization. The other options are not necessarily true for all non-profit organizations. For example, some non-profit organizations do earn income through various activities, and not all non-profit organizations have annual subscriptions and levies as part of their income.

It fails to show the transactions on an accrual basis.

Receipts and payments account does not show Non-Cash transactions like depreciation of assets, pilferage etc.

Which of the following indicate that a partnership business is in place?
(i) There is a business
(ii) It is run commonly by partners
(iii) It has profit making in view
(iv) Partners' liability is limited
explanation: A partnership business is indicated by the presence of a business that is commonly run by partners with the aim of making profit. The liability of partners in a partnership is not limited, it is unlimited. Therefore, options I, II and III are correct.

q: When forming a partnership, new partners should record non-monetary assets on the new partnership's books at
options: their historical costs when first purchased by each new partner
the highest values practical so that future income tax deductions are maximized.
explanation: When a partnership is formed or a partner is added and contributes assets other than cash, the partnership establishes the net realizable or fair market value for the assets. For example, if the Walking Partners company adds a partner who contributes accounts receivable and equipment from an existing business, the partnership evaluates the collectibility of the accounts receivable and records them at their net realizable value.

topics.title: PARTNERSHIP ACCOUNT : INTRODUCTION AND TYPES OF PARTNERS( PARTNERSHIP)

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following is an intangible asset and a measure of a firm's superior earning power?
explanation: Goodwill is an intangible asset that is associated with the purchase of one company by another. Specifically, goodwill is the portion of the purchase price that is higher than the sum of the net fair value of all of the assets purchased in the acquisition and the liabilities assumed in the process. The value of a company's brand name, solid customer base, good customer relations, good employee relations, and any patents or proprietary technology represent goodwill.

topics.title: GOODWILL ACCOUNTS : INTRODUCTION AND REASONS FOR GOODWILL

explanation: Purchase consideration is the value that is projected to be gotten from the acquisition from the acquisition of asset or another organization. The price paid to acquire another company is described as purchase consideration.

Dan and Baker are in partnership with capital of ₦50,000 and ₦30,000 each. The partnership agreement provides that (i) Profit be shared in the ratio of capital (i) Baker be paid a salary of ₦8,000 (iii) Both partners earn interest on capital at 6% p.a. (iv) Both partners pay interest on drawing at 6% p.a. At the end of the year, Dan drew ₦15,000 while Baker drew ₦14,000 in four equal installments on 31/3, 30/9 and 31/12. The net profit for the year was ₦48,000, ₦5,000 is to be written off the Goodwill account.

What is the interest on the drawing by Baker?
explanation:
Four equal installments = 14000/4
= 3500

Interest on drawings = 6% x 3500
= #210

OR
The interest on each installment is calculated for the period it was drawn.
The total interest is therefore:
(₦3,500 x 6% x 9/12) + (₦3,500 x 6% x 3/12) + (₦3,500 x 6% x 0/12)
= ₦157.5 + ₦52.5 + ₦0
= ₦210.

explanation: The ordinary shareholders enjoy the following right except the right to receive dividends at a pre-determined ratio because they are not preference shareholder and they are the one to firstly lose their investments or share in the vent of liquidation.

topics.title: ISSUES OF SHARES : MEANING AND CLASSES OF SHARES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Granada Corporation has net assets of ₦600,000 and contributed capital of ₦180,000. The corporation has 30,000 shares of common stock outstanding with no preferred stock. This suggests that the corporation has 30,000 shares of common stock outstanding with no preferred stock. This suggests that the corporation has
explanation: The book value per share is calculated by subtracting the contributed capital from the net assets and then dividing by the number of shares outstanding. In this case, the net assets are ₦600,000 and the contributed capital is ₦180,000, leaving ₦420,000. This amount divided by the 30,000 shares outstanding gives a book value of ₦14 per share. However, the question seems to have a typographical error as it repeats a sentence. The correct answer should be 'a book value of ₦14 per share', but since it's not an option, the closest correct answer is 'a book value of ₦20 per share'.

topics.title: COMPANY ACCOUNT : FINAL ACCOUNTS OF A LIMITED COMPANY AND TERMINOLOGIES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Under which of these conditions can a company issue shares at discount?
(i) A resolution must be passed at a general meeting
(ii) The amount of discount must be stated in the resolution
(iii) The share must have existed for at least six years
(iv) On the order of a court.
explanation:
Generally a Company is not allowed to issue shares at a discount, i.e. at a price less than the face value of the shares. However, if a Company wants to raise capital by way of issuing shares at discount, i.e. the buyer is required to pay less amount than the face value, then the shares are said to have been issued or sold at a discount.
However, the following must be considered:
- The shares to be issued at a discount must be of a class already issued.
- The issue must be authorised by a resolution passed by the company in General Meeting and sanctioned by the Company Law Board.
- Rate of discount should not be more than 10%.

topics.title: ISSUES OF SHARES : MEANING AND CLASSES OF SHARES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The part of equity which is payable on winding up is called
explanation: The part of equity which is payable on winding up is called Capital reserve. Capital reserves are funds that are not going to be distributed as dividends but are to be used for future growth and expansion. In the event of winding up, these reserves are used to pay off the company's obligations. General reserve, Revenue reserve, and Normal reserve are types of reserves that are typically used for different purposes, not specifically for winding up.

topics.title: COMPANY ACCOUNT : INTRODUCTION , TYPES AND KINDS OF COMPANIES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: To compute gearing ratio, divide
explanation: A gearing ratio is a general classification describing a financial ratio that compares some form of owner equity (or capital) to funds borrowed by the company. Net gearing can also be calculated by dividing the total debt by the total shareholders' equity.

topics.title: ACCOUNTING RATIO (2) : PROFITABILITY, LIQUIDITY AND INVESTMENT RATIO

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following entries is effected by a department when goods are charged to it at selling prices?
explanation: When goods are charged to a department at selling prices, the department's Stock account is debited. This is because the department is acquiring goods (an asset), and when an asset increases, it is debited according to the rules of double-entry bookkeeping. Therefore, the correct answer is 'Stock account is debited'.

topics.title: DEPARTMENTAL ACCOUNT : METHODS OF APPORTIONMENT OF EXPENSES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The difference between the closure of the books of a branch and those of a separate company is that
options: there is retained earnings account on the branch books
the revenue and expenses account is closed to branch current account
there is no retained earnings account on the branch books
the revenue and expenses account is not closed to the home office current account
explanation: In branch accounting, the revenue and expenses accounts are closed to the branch current account. This is because the branch is not a separate legal entity, but a part of the main company. The branch current account is a representation of the branch's financial activities and is used to track the branch's income and expenses. The branch does not have its own retained earnings account because it is not a separate legal entity.

topics.title: BRANCH ACCOUNTING : BRANCH ADJUSTMENT METHOD

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The term fiscal compliance means
options: all financial and related laws and regulations are adhered to
only the budget for the current period is complied with and no deficits allowed
all physical assets requirements have been met
fiscal planning is necessary for proper accountability in the public sector.
explanation: In terms of accounting, compliance simply means making sure that a company's financial matters are being handled in accordance with federal laws and regulations.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : PURPOSE AND DIFFERENCE BETWEEN GOVERNMENT AND COMMERCIAL ACCOUNTING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: In a public corporation, the capital expenditure incurred in a financial period is
options: spread over the useful life of the assets through depreciation
apportioned at a pre-determined rate stipulated by law
written off in the year in which they occur
merged with recurrent expenditure and reported in one lump sum
explanation: In a public corporation, the capital expenditure incurred in a financial period is spread over the useful life of the assets through depreciation. This is because capital expenditure represents a long-term investment, and its cost is spread over the useful life of the asset to match the revenue it generates. This is done through the process of depreciation, which is the systematic allocation of the cost of an asset over its useful life.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : ESTIMATES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Money not required to meet chargeable expenditure in any fiscal year under cash account should be
options: carried forward to the next financial year
reserved to meet any deficit or contingencies
explanation: It should be surrendered to the consolidated revenue fund. Consolidated revenue fund is a special revenue fund is a fund that government entities use to record receipt of funds for certain revenue sources whose use is restricted. The fund makes it easy for a government entity to track cash inflows and outflows related to the intended purpose.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : SOURCES AND CLASSIFICATION OF GOVERNMENT REVENUE

q: If a monthly insurance premium is remitted to a company by the state government, the initial deductions from employees are recorded in
explanation: Agency fund is a Conduit or clearinghouse funds established to account for assets received for and paid to other funds, individuals, or organizations.

During the year ended 31st, December 2001, Nuju Enterprises paid rent as follows;
January 20, ₦ 24,000 for a period of 4 months,
May 16, ₦30,000 for a period of 5 months,
October 15, ₦36,000 for a period of 6 months,
What is the overpaid portion of the rent?
explanation:
From the question above, per month is #6,000
Therefore, Oct to Dec = #18,000 and he paid #36,000
It is assumed that half of the payment have been utilized by 31st December 2001, so the overpayment is ₦ 18,000.

explanation: A cashbook is a financial journal that contains all cash receipts and payments, including bank deposits and withdrawals. Entries in the cashbook are then posted into the general ledger. explanation: Working capital is calculated as current assets less current liabilities. This is because working capital represents the short-term liquidity of a company, which is the difference between its short-term assets (current assets) and its short-term liabilities (current liabilities).

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Clifford pays rent of ₦200 per month for premises. He paid ₦2,800 within the year. Compute the prepayment.
explanation:
Prepayment is an expense or income that is paid or received in advance of the period to which it relates. In this case, Clifford pays a monthly rent of ₦200 and he paid ₦2,800 within the year.

This means he paid for 14 months i.e (₦2,800 / ₦200).

Since a year has 12 months, he has prepaid for 2 months.

Therefore, the prepayment is 2 months x ₦200
= ₦400.

topics.title: THE FINAL ACCOUNTS OF A SOLE TRADER : TRADING ACTIVITIES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Given:
Cost of machinery 1/1/08 = ₦200,000.
Depreciation at 10% reducing balance.

- Determine the value of depreciation on 31/12/10
explanation:
Calculate the annual depreciation rate:
Annual Depreciation Rate = 10% = 0.10

Calculate the depreciation for the first year (2008):
Depreciation for 2008 = Cost of machinery x Annual Depreciation Rate
Depreciation for 2008 = ₦200,000 x 0.10
= ₦20,000

Calculate the book value at the end of the first year (2008):
Book Value on 31/12/08 = Cost of machinery - Depreciation for 2008
Book Value on 31/12/08 = ₦200,000 - ₦20,000
= ₦180,000

Calculate the depreciation for the second year (2009) based on the reduced book value:
Depreciation for 2009 = Book Value on 31/12/08 x Annual Depreciation Rate
Depreciation for 2009 = ₦180,000 x 0.10
= ₦18,000

Calculate the book value at the end of the second year (2009):
Book Value on 31/12/09 = Book Value on 31/12/08 - Depreciation for 2009
Book Value on 31/12/09 = ₦180,000 - ₦18,000
= ₦162,000

Calculate the depreciation for the third year (2010) based on the reduced book value:
Depreciation for 2010 = Book Value on 31/12/09 x Annual Depreciation Rate
Depreciation for 2010 = ₦162,000 x 0.10
= ₦16,200

So, the value of depreciation on 31/12/10 is ₦16,200.

explanation: Credit balance in a rent ledger account will be shown in the balance sheet as current liabiity because it is an outstanding but otherwise,it is an asset.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: If a company values its stock in the period of rising prices using LIFO method, there is a tendency for it to
options: have a higher cost of goods sold
have a higher value for closing stock
explanation: LIFO (Last In, First Out) is an inventory valuation method which assumes that the latest items purchased are the first ones to be sold. In a period of rising prices, using LIFO will result in a higher cost of goods sold because the latest purchased goods (which are sold first according to LIFO) are more expensive. This will reduce the company's net income and, consequently, its income tax liability.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: One of the source of income of a not-profit-making organization is
explanation: A not-for-profit organization does not operate for the purpose of making profit. Its main sources of income are donations, grants, and membership fees. Therefore, the correct answer is 'grant from the federal government'. This is because the federal government often provides grants to not-for-profit organizations to support their activities.

topics.title: ACCOUNTS OF NON PROFIT MAKING ORGANISATION : INCOME AND EXPENDITURE ACCOUNTS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: When using the first-in first-out method of valuation in a period of falling prices, profits are
explanation: The First-In, First-Out (FIFO) method is an inventory valuation method where the costs of the oldest inventory items are recorded as sold first. In a period of falling prices, using the FIFO method will result in lower cost of goods sold because the older, cheaper items are recorded as sold first. This leads to higher profits, hence the profits are understated.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
July 1 - Started business with ₦ 10,500
July 31 - Paid Agromachinex ₦ 6,000 owing to them
The double entry for 31 July would be
explanation: The double entry system in accounting means that every business transaction will involve two accounts (or more). For example, when a company borrows money from its bank, the company's Cash account will increase and its liability account Loans Payable will increase. In this case, the business paid Agromachinex, which means cash (an asset) decreased. In double entry bookkeeping, a decrease in an asset is credited. Therefore, we credit cash. The business also settled a liability (amount owing to Agromachinex), and a decrease in a liability is debited. Therefore, we debit Agromachinex.

explanation: The realization principle is the concept that revenue can only be recognized once the underlying goods or services associated with the revenue have been delivered or rendered, respectively.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q:
Given:
I. Payment of debts owed to creditors
II. Payment of any loan
III. Repayment of capital contributed
IV. Sharing of surplus among the partners on an agreed term.
According to the partnership Act, when partnership is dissolved, payment should be prioritized as
explanation: According to the Partnership Act, when a partnership is dissolved, the payment should be prioritized as follows: Payment of debts owed to creditors (I), Payment of any loan (II), Repayment of capital contributed (III), and Sharing of surplus among the partners on an agreed term (IV). This is because the law requires that the partnership's external liabilities be settled first before the internal liabilities and distributions.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following is the capital reserve of a company?
explanation: The capital reserve of a company is an account on the company's balance sheet that is dedicated to money reserved for long-term projects or large, anticipated expenses. This can include the share premium, which is the amount received from the issue of shares above their face value. The other options do not represent capital reserves. Retained profit is a part of earned reserves, loss on forfeited shares is a loss, not a reserve, and accumulated depreciation is a contra asset account, not a reserve.

explanation: Details of share that can be issued by the company must be contained in the Memorandum of Association of the company. Memorandum of Association is a document that contains terms and conditions that governs the overall business of a company and how shareholders relate.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Investors are interested in accounting information to determine the organization's
explanation: Investors are interested in accounting information to determine the organization's solvency level. Solvency refers to the ability of a company to meet its long-term financial obligations. Investors need to know this to assess the risk associated with their investment. While profitability, liquidity, and current ratios are also important, they are aspects of a company's financial health, not the overall solvency level.

explanation: The cost of putting goods into a saleable condition is charged to the trading account. explanation: The money measurement concept in accounting means that only transactions and events that are capable of being measured in monetary terms are recognized in the financial statements. One of its limitations is that it does not disclose the efficiency of management. This is because many aspects of management's performance, such as leadership ability, employee morale, and customer satisfaction, cannot be easily quantified in monetary terms.

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The vote book in governmental accounting is used to
explanation: In governmental accounting, the vote book is used to control expenditure. It is a record of all financial transactions related to a particular budget or fund. It helps to ensure that expenditures do not exceed the approved budget. It is not used to register voters, record government revenue, or record taxes paid.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : PURPOSE AND DIFFERENCE BETWEEN GOVERNMENT AND COMMERCIAL ACCOUNTING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following is correct about cost of goods sold?
explanation: The correct formula for calculating the cost of goods sold is: Opening stock + purchases - closing stock. This formula is used to calculate the direct costs attributable to the production of the goods sold in a company. This includes the cost of the materials used in creating the goods along with the direct labor costs used to produce the good.

topics.title: THE FINAL ACCOUNTS OF A SOLE TRADER : TRADING ACTIVITIES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The accounting treatment when goods are sent to branch at cost is, debit Branch Stock Account and credit
explanation: The accounting treatment when goods are sent to branch at cost is, debit Branch Stock Account and credit Goods send to Branch Account. This is because when goods are sent to a branch, the branch stock account (an asset account) is increased (debited), and the goods sent to branch account (also an asset account) is decreased (credited). This reflects the movement of goods from the main business to the branch.

topics.title: DEPARTMENTAL ACCOUNT : INTRODUCTION AND ADVANTAGES OF DEPARTMENTAL ACCOUNT

topics.title: ACCOUNTING RATIO (2) : PROFITABILITY, LIQUIDITY AND INVESTMENT RATIO

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following is not a revenue to a local government?
explanation: Personal income tax is not a revenue to a local government. Personal income tax is a type of tax that governments impose on income generated by businesses and individuals within their jurisdiction. In most jurisdictions, including Nigeria, personal income tax is collected by the state government, not the local government. The local government collects revenues through sources like fines, licenses, and fees from local craftsmen and other local levies.

topics.title: PUBLIC SECTOR ACCOUNTING : SOURCES AND CLASSIFICATION OF GOVERNMENT REVENUE

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: In the preparation of financial statements, full disclosure of minor events are ignored in line with
explanation: In the preparation of financial statements, minor events are often ignored in line with the materiality concept. The materiality concept, in accounting, refers to the relative significance of an amount, transaction, or discrepancy. The concept suggests that all relatively insignificant items are permissible to be disregarded if their effect on the financial statements is unimportant to the user's understanding.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : ACCOUNTING CONCEPTS AND CONVENTIONS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Net debtors is
explanation: Net debtors is calculated by subtracting the provision for bad debts from the total debtors. This is done to account for the debts that are expected to be uncollectible.

topics.title: RESERVES AND PROVISIONS : BAD DEBTS AND PROVISIONS FOR DOUBTFUL DEBTS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following is not part of the Central Processing Unit of a computer?
explanation: The Central Processing Unit (CPU) of a computer is the primary component of a computer that performs most of the processing inside the computer. It is often referred to as the 'brain' of the computer. The CPU is made up of three main parts: the Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) that performs arithmetic and logical operations, the Control Unit (CU) that manages the various components of the computer, and the Memory Unit (MU) that stores data. The Input Unit is not part of the CPU; it is a separate component that allows data to be input into the computer.

topics.title: FINANCIAL SYSTEM : MEANING, FUNCTIONS AND FEATURES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: "The Accountant thinks the investment in the book are worthless". This is
explanation: Money measurement states that only monetary transactions which can be expressed in monetary terms are recorded.

explanation: 'X' refers to sales. In a debtor's control account, sales are recorded on the debit side, while payments from debtors (bank) and discounts allowed are recorded on the credit side.

explanation: The closing balance of a debtor's control account is a current asset. Current assets are assets that are expected to be converted to cash within one year. Debtors are people who owe the business money, and this is expected to be received within one year.

topics.title: CONTROL ACCOUNTS : SALES LEDGER CONTROL ACCOUNT

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The double entry for increasing provision for bad debt is debit
options: Debtors's Account, Credit provision for Bad Debt Account
Profit and loss Account, Credit debtor's Account
Debtor's Account, Credit profit and loss Account
Profit and loss Account, credit Provision for Bad Debt Account
explanation: In accounting, a provision is an account which records a present liability of an entity. The recording of the liability in the entity's balance sheet is matched to an appropriate expense account on the entity's income statement. Therefore, when increasing provision for bad debt, the double entry would be to debit (increase) the expense account (Profit and Loss Account) and credit (increase) the liability account (Provision for Bad Debt Account).

topics.title: RESERVES AND PROVISIONS : BAD DEBTS AND PROVISIONS FOR DOUBTFUL DEBTS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following is an example of direct expense?
explanation: Direct expenses are costs that can be directly tied to a cost object, which can be a product, department or project. This can include items such as direct materials, direct labor, machine repairs, and machine depreciation. In the given options, 'Manufacturing wages' is an example of direct expense as it can be directly attributed to the production of goods.

topics.title: THE FINAL ACCOUNTS OF A SOLE TRADER : TRADING ACTIVITIES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The systematic recording of business transactions in monetary term is
explanation: The systematic recording of business transactions in monetary terms is known as book-keeping. Book-keeping is the process of recording daily transactions in a consistent way, and is a key component to building a financially successful business. It ensures that records of the individual financial transactions are correct, up-to-date and comprehensive. Therefore, the correct answer is option 2.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO ACCOUNTING : MEANING AND OBJECTIVES OF ACCOUNTING AND BOOKKEEPING

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following transactions is posted to the nominal ledger?
explanation: Nominal ledger are accounts in which income and expenses are recorded e.g sales account, rent account, discounts account etc.

topics.title: PRINCIPLE OF DOUBLE ENTRY : CASH AND CREDIT TRANSACTIONS

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following is used to record the disposal of a fixed asset?
explanation: The Journal Proper is used to record transactions that do not fit into any other specific journal such as the sales journal, purchases journal, cash receipts journal, or cash disbursements journal. Disposal of a fixed asset is a unique transaction that does not fit into the other specific journals, hence it is recorded in the Journal Proper.

explanation: cash discount received can be shown in Cash book ,Discount Received Account and Suppliers' Account

topics.title: DEPRECIATION OF FIXED ASSETS : METHODS OF DEPRECIATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following best describes the meaning of a financial system?
options: A network of financial institutions and markets that facilitate the flow of funds
A system that only includes banks and credit unions
explanation: A financial system is a comprehensive network of financial institutions, markets, instruments, and regulations that facilitate the flow of funds between savers and borrowers. It encompasses various entities, including banks, stock markets, and insurance companies, which work together to allocate resources efficiently.

topics.title: FINANCIAL SYSTEM : MEANING, FUNCTIONS AND FEATURES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: The following are functions of a financial system except:
explanation: While a financial system aims to manage and mitigate financial risks, it cannot eliminate all financial risks. Financial systems provide mechanisms for risk management and facilitate savings and investments, but risks are inherent in financial transactions and markets.

topics.title: FINANCIAL SYSTEM : MEANING, FUNCTIONS AND FEATURES

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following accounts is debited when a company purchases inventory on credit?
explanation: When a company purchases inventory on credit, the Inventory account is debited to reflect the increase in assets, while Accounts Payable is credited to show the obligation to pay the supplier in the future.

topics.title: ACCOUNTING EQUATION

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: In the double-entry accounting system, which of the following statements is correct regarding the ledger?
options: The ledger is a collection of all accounts used by a business.
Only asset accounts are recorded in the ledger.
The ledger does not show the balance of accounts.
explanation: The ledger is indeed a collection of all accounts used by a business, where each account records its transactions and balances. This is fundamental to the double-entry system, which requires that every transaction affects at least two accounts.

topics.title: THE LEDGER : MEANING AND DIVISION OF LEDGER

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following best describes data processing?
options: The collection of data without any analysis
The conversion of raw data into meaningful information
The storage of data in a physical format
The deletion of unnecessary data from a database
explanation: Data processing refers to the systematic collection, organization, and analysis of data to convert it into meaningful information. This process involves various operations such as sorting, filtering, and analyzing data to derive insights.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO DATA PROCESSING : MEANING, CLASSIFICATION AND COMPONENTS OF COMPUTER

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following is NOT a component of a computer used in data processing?
explanation: A printer is an output device that produces a physical copy of data but is not a component used directly in the data processing cycle. The CPU, RAM, and HDD are essential components that facilitate data processing by performing calculations, storing data, and managing memory.

topics.title: INTRODUCTION TO DATA PROCESSING : MEANING, CLASSIFICATION AND COMPONENTS OF COMPUTER

subject: Accounts - Principles of Accounts
q: Which of the following is an example of production overhead?
explanation: Production overhead refers to the indirect factory-related costs that are incurred when a product is manufactured. This includes costs related to the operation of the factory such as rent, utilities, and maintenance. Therefore, 'factory rent' is an example of production overhead.
